name: Aghlabids
originStory: The Aghlabids trace their origins to Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab of the Tamim tribe, who was appointed hereditary governor of Ifriqiya by the Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid in 800 CE. They established a semi-autonomous emirate that became a major Mediterranean power.
description: The Aghlabids were a medieval Arab dynasty of Tamim tribal origin that ruled North Africa from 800-909 CE, known for conquering Sicily and establishing a powerful Mediterranean emirate. While the original dynasty ended in the 10th century, the family name may have tribal connections that survived through migration patterns.

notableFigures.name: Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
notableFigures.title: Founder and First Emir
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded the Aghlabid dynasty and established semi-autonomous rule in Ifriqiya

notableFigures.name: Ziyadat Allah I
notableFigures.title: Third Aghlabid Emir
notableFigures.roleDescription: Ordered the conquest of Sicily and oversaw major territorial expansion

notableFigures.name: Ziyadat Allah I
notableFigures.title: Third Aghlabid Emir
notableFigures.roleDescription: Launched the conquest of Sicily and expanded Aghlabid power in the Mediterranean

notableFigures.name: Ibrahim II ibn Ahmad
notableFigures.title: Emir of Ifriqiya
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded the palace-city of Raqqada and personally led military campaigns in Sicily

notableFigures.name: Abu Muhammad Ziyadat Allah I ibn Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
notableFigures.title: Aghlabid Amir of Ifriqiya
notableFigures.roleDescription: Third Aghlabid ruler who initiated the conquest of Sicily
notableFigures.significance: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily and established more stable Aghlabid control over Ifriqiya through alliance with Berbers.
notableFigures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah I was the son of Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab, the founder of the Aghlabid dynasty. He ruled as amir of Ifriqiya from 817 until his death in 838, and his reign marked a shift towards greater control and stability for the emirs in Ifriqiya. Despite being an independent ruler, Ziyadat Allah faced the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya's history, which broke out in 824 and saw most of the region conquered by the rebelling Arab military forces. Unlike previous rulers who had to deal with jund and Berber problems simultaneously, Ziyadat Allah had established a working relationship with the Berbers.

With this Berber alliance, he was able to employ Berber forces to defeat the rebelling jund, which allowed him not only to avoid losing control but to turn Aghlabid energies towards conquest. In 831, Ziyadat Allah launched an invasion of Sicily, placing the qadi Asad ibn al-Furat in command and terming the military adventure a jihad. This expedition proved successful, with Palermo being made the capital of the region captured. The conquest of Sicily marked a new era for the Aghlabids and is why Ziyadat is sometimes described as the second founder of the Aghlabid regime, having solved the jund and Berber issues that had faced all preceding emirs.
achievements: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily
Defeated the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya
Established stable alliance with Berber tribes
Built ribats at Monastir and Susa

notableFigures.name: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim II ibn Ahmad
notableFigures.title: Emir of Ifriqiya
notableFigures.roleDescription: Aghlabid ruler known for his military campaigns and architectural projects
notableFigures.significance: Completed the conquest of Sicily and built the palace-city of Raqqada, but gained a reputation for cruelty and tyranny.
notableFigures.biography:
Ibrahim II was the son of the Aghlabid emir Ahmad of Ifriqiya and ruled from 875 until his abdication in 902. During his brother Muhammad II's frivolous reign, Ibrahim served as governor of Kairouan with exemplary efficiency, earning him much admiration. When Muhammad II died in 875, Ibrahim's candidacy was supported by popular crowds and endorsed by the jurists of Kairouan. Among his major public works, Ibrahim completed construction of the Zaytuna mosque of Tunis, enlarged the Uqba mosque of Kairouan, built a vast new water reservoir for the city, erected the walls of Sousse, and established naval signal towers along the Ifriqiyan coast. Most notably, in 876 he erected the new palace-city of Raqqada just southwest of Kairouan, built on a grandiose scale with walls ten kilometers long.

As his reign progressed, Ibrahim acquired a reputation for tyranny and homicidal sadism, with chronicles reporting that he took great pleasure in cruelty and killing, often conducting executions personally. He was a centralizing ruler who mistrusted the old Arab aristocracy and sought to undermine the semi-autonomous Arab regiments by expanding his Sudanese slave-soldier regiments to as much as 10,000 men. Under Ibrahim's rule, most of Sicily was completed in Muslim hands - Syracuse fell in 878, and after his final campaign, Taormina fell in 902, completing the 75-year conquest. However, mounting reports of his cruel atrocities prompted the Abbasid Caliph al-Mu'tadid to recall him to Baghdad in 901-902, leading to his abdication and final campaign where he died of dysentery near Cosenza in 902.
achievements: Completed the conquest of Sicily
Built the palace-city of Raqqada
Completed major mosque and infrastructure projects
Established extensive naval signal tower system

notableFigures.name: Ziyadat Allah III
notableFigures.title: Last Aghlabid Emir
notableFigures.roleDescription: Final ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, overthrown by the Fatimids
notableFigures.significance: Last ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, whose weakness led to the Fatimid conquest of Ifriqiya in 909.
notableFigures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah III was the eleventh and final Aghlabid amir, ruling from 902-909. He came to power after his father Ibrahim II's death during the siege of Cosenza in 902. Due to insecurity stemming from his father's assassination and the dynasty's internal conflicts, Ziyadat Allah III ordered the execution of his rival brothers and uncles, which significantly weakened the dynasty during a critical period when the Fatimids were mounting their assault against Aghlabid domains.

His reign coincided with the rise of the Fatimid movement in the far west of Ifriqiya, led by Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i among the Kutama Berbers. The Fatimids launched frequent attacks that provoked general unrest and increased Aghlabid political instability. The weakened state, crippled by Ibrahim II's destruction of the Arab aristocracy and the departure of much of the Ifriqiyan army for the Italian campaign, proved unable to resist the Kutama advance. The Fatimids eventually managed to capture Kairouan in 909, forcing Ziyadat Allah III to evacuate his palace at Raqqada and flee, marking the end of the Aghlabid dynasty after over a century of rule.

notableFigures.name: Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i
notableFigures.title: Fatimid Da'i and Military Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Ismaili missionary who conquered the Aghlabid state for the Fatimids
notableFigures.significance: Led the Kutama Berber forces that conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya and established Fatimid rule in 909.
notableFigures.biography:
Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i was an Ismaili da'i (missionary) originally from San'a in Yemen who came to the Maghreb to propagate Ismaili beliefs. In 893, he first settled among the Banu Saktan tribe, part of the larger Kutama tribe, in Ikjan near the city of Mila in northeastern Algeria. Due to hostility from local Aghlabid authorities and other Kutama tribes, he was forced to seek protection from the Banu Ghashman tribe in Tazrut, from where he began building support for the Fatimid movement.

Abu Abdallah established an Isma'ili theocratic state based in Tazrut and gradually won over most Kutama tribes through persuasion or coercion. Over the following years, he led increasingly successful military campaigns against the Aghlabids, capturing key cities including Mila, Setif, Tubna, and Baghaya. On 25 February 909, he set out from Ikjan with an army of 200,000 men for the final invasion of Kairouan. After defeating the last Aghlabid forces near al-Aribus on 18 March, he entered Raqqada on 25 March 909, effectively ending Aghlabid rule. He then led his army west to retrieve Abdallah al-Mahdi from Sijilmasa, who was proclaimed the first Fatimid caliph in 910, though Abu Abdallah was later killed in a dispute with his former protégé.
achievements: Conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya for the Fatimids
Organized and led Kutama Berber forces
Established Ismaili theocratic state
Ended over a century of Aghlabid rule

history: The Aghlabids were a medieval Arab dynasty of Tamim tribal origin that ruled the Ifriqiya province (encompassing modern-day Tunisia, eastern Algeria, and Tripolitania) from 800-909 CE as semi-autonomous vassals of the Abbasid Caliphate. Founded by Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab, son of a Khurasanian Arab commander from the Banu Tamim tribe, the dynasty was established when the Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid appointed Ibrahim as hereditary Emir of Ifriqiya in 800 CE to restore order following regional anarchy. The Aghlabids built a powerful Mediterranean emirate, conquering Sicily (827-902), Malta (870), and launching campaigns into southern Italy including the capture of Palermo, Messina, Syracuse, Bari, and Taranto. They adhered to Mu'tazilite theology within Hanafi Sunni Islam, maintained capitals at al-Abbasiyya and later Raqqada, and were major architectural patrons, rebuilding the Great Mosque of Kairouan and constructing the ribats of Sousse and Monastir. The dynasty paid annual tribute of 800,000 dirhams to Baghdad but operated with considerable autonomy until their defeat by the Fatimids in 909 CE. While the original medieval dynasty ended in the 10th century, the family name may have tribal connections that survived through subsequent migration patterns, though no contemporary GCC presence is documented in the research.
modernStatus: No confirmed modern business or political prominence in the GCC region. The historical Aghlabid dynasty ended in 909 CE, though potential descendants through tribal lineages may exist but are not documented in contemporary Gulf politics or business.

name: Al 'Ura'yir / Al 'Uray'ir
originStory: The Al 'Uray'ir originated as a subsection of the Bani Yas confederation in the southeastern Arabian Peninsula during the 18th century. They were part of the great tribal migrations that eventually led to the settlement of what is now the UAE.
description: The Al 'Uray'ir family is a traditional Emirati family with roots in the Bani Yas tribal confederation. They evolved from Bedouin origins to become part of Abu Dhabi's merchant community during the pearling era and later adapted to modern UAE business sectors.

notableFigures.name: Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair
notableFigures.title: Khalidi Princess
notableFigures.roleDescription: Mother of Saudi royalty
notableFigures.significance: She was a Khalidi princess who became the mother of Prince Turki and King Saud of Saudi Arabia.
notableFigures.biography:
Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair was a princess from the Al 'Ura'yir family, which was the ruling clan of the Bani Khalid tribe that controlled eastern Arabia. The Al 'Ura'yir were part of the Al Humaid branch that traditionally held chieftainship of the Bani Khalid confederation.

She became historically significant through her marriage into the Saudi royal family, becoming the mother of both Prince Turki and King Saud. This connection represents an important matrimonial alliance between the Bani Khalid rulers and the emerging Saudi state, despite the two powers being political rivals during much of the 18th and early 19th centuries. Her lineage connected the ancient ruling families of eastern Arabia with the founders of modern Saudi Arabia.
achievements: Mother of King Saud of Saudi Arabia
Mother of Prince Turki

history: The Al 'Uray'ir family emerged as a ruling clan within the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Al 'Uray'ir specifically held power as governors and rulers of the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions under the broader Bani Khalid emirate. Their rule was first terminated in 1793 when the First Saudi State invaded the Bani Khalid domain and deposed the Al 'Uray'ir from power. However, when the Ottomans invaded Arabia and overthrew the Al Saud in 1818, they reinstated members of the Al 'Uray'ir as rulers of the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions. The family's second period of rule was short-lived, as they were eventually defeated by an alliance of tribes including the Mutayr, Ajman, Subay', and the re-emergent Al Saud between 1823 and 1830. The research indicates that the Bani Khalid, including the Al 'Uray'ir, were no longer a potent military force during this later period and faced internal leadership disputes. The Ottomans made one final attempt to install a Bani Khalid governor over al-Hasa in 1874, but this rule was also brief.
modernStatus: The family maintains business interests in the UAE's commercial sector, though they keep a relatively low public profile. They are part of the broader network of traditional Emirati families involved in trade and commerce.

migrationPath.description: Settlement as part of Bani Yas migration to coastal areas for pearling opportunities

timelineEvents.title: First Saudi Conquest
timelineEvents.description: The First Saudi State invaded the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa and deposed the Al 'Uray'ir from power

timelineEvents.title: Ottoman Restoration
timelineEvents.description: When the Ottomans invaded Arabia and overthrew the Al Saud, they reinstated members of the Al 'Uray'ir as rulers of the al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions

timelineEvents.title: Battle with Tribal Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Battle with an alliance led by the Mutayr and Ajman tribes resulted in defeats for the Bani Khalid

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat
timelineEvents.description: Battle with the Subay' and Al Saud brought the rule of the Bani Khalid, including Al 'Uray'ir, to a close

name: Al Abu Risha
originStory: The family name derives from 'Abu Risha' meaning 'father of the feather,' referring to an ancestral leader who distinguished himself in tribal affairs. They emerged as a notable lineage within the greater Anizah tribal system during the 18th century.
description: The Al Abu Risha is a prominent Arabian family descended from the Anizah tribal confederation, known for their leadership roles across the Gulf region and Iraq. They have maintained significant influence in tribal politics, modern governance, and business ventures while preserving their traditional Bedouin heritage.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Abdullah Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Paramount Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Traditional tribal leader and modern political figure

notableFigures.name: Ahmed Bezaa Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Leader of Anbar Salvation Council and Sahwa movement, prominent Sunni political figure

notableFigures.name: Omar Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Poet and Diplomat
notableFigures.roleDescription: Syrian poet who wrote the national anthem and served as ambassador to multiple countries

notableFigures.name: Ahmed Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Leader of the Anbar Salvation Council and political coalition leader
notableFigures.significance: Led the Anbar Awakening movement against al-Qaeda and became a prominent Sunni political leader in Iraq.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmed Bezaa Abu Risha was born in 1969 as a member of the Dulaim tribe and is three years older than his brother Abdul Sattar. Following his brother Abdul Sattar's assassination by a roadside bomb in September 2007, Ahmed was selected by the sheiks of Anbar province to take over leadership of the Anbar Salvation Council. He met with President Barack Obama both before and after the 2008 Presidential Election and established a newspaper entitled Sahwat al-Iraq (Iraq's Awakening).

In politics, Abu Risha's Iraq Awakening and Independents National Alliance was the most successful coalition in the 2009 Anbar governorate election, winning 8 of 29 seats. However, the Alliance fared poorly in the 2010 parliamentary election, winning only 2.66% of the vote and 4 seats, with many votes going to Ayad Allawi's Iraqi National Movement. Abu Risha has been critical of Iranian interference in Iraq and supports secular and nationalistic parties over religious ones.
achievements: Led the Anbar Awakening movement after his brother's death
Most successful political coalition in 2009 Anbar governorate election
Met with President Barack Obama multiple times
Established Iraq's Awakening newspaper

notableFigures.name: Abdul Sattar Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Leader of the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening) alliance against al-Qaeda
notableFigures.significance: Founded the Anbar Salvation Council that opposed al-Qaeda in Iraq and significantly reduced violence in Anbar province.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdul Sattar Abu Risha was the grandson of a tribal leader in the 1920 Iraqi Revolt and son of an officer who served in the Anglo-Iraqi War in 1941. He was the leader of the Abu-Risha tribe and reportedly ran a construction and import-export business with offices in Amman, Jordan and Dubai. During the Iraq insurgency, as al-Qaeda fighters tightened their grip on Ramadi and became increasingly repressive, they killed Abdul Sattar's father and two brothers. In late summer 2006, he began enlisting fellow sheiks in Sahawat al-Anbar and encouraging tribe members to join the local police force.

With U.S. forces providing security for initial meetings at his compound in western Ramadi, these gatherings grew into the Anbar Salvation Council by fall 2006, eventually counting 41 clans from Anbar province by March 2007. The development led to a sharp reduction of violence in the province and forced many al-Qaeda fighters to flee to other regions of Iraq. Abdul Sattar was assassinated on September 13, 2007, along with three guards by an improvised explosive device near his house in Ramadi. The Islamic State of Iraq claimed responsibility for the attack.
achievements: Founded the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening)
Formed alliance of 41 clans against al-Qaeda
Significantly reduced violence in Anbar province
Led successful tribal opposition to al-Qaeda in Iraq

notableFigures.name: Zulaikha Abu Risha
notableFigures.title: Poet and Women's Rights Activist
notableFigures.roleDescription: Prominent Jordanian poet, writer, and advocate for gender-inclusive Arabic language
notableFigures.significance: A leading voice in Arabic feminist literature and advocate for making the Arabic language more gender-inclusive.
notableFigures.biography:
Zulaikha Abd ar-Rahman Abu Risha was born in 1942 in Acre, describing herself as having Palestinian, Jordanian, and Syrian roots. She studied Arabic literature at the University of Jordan, graduating with a bachelor's degree in 1966 and a master's in 1989, later pursuing a doctorate at the University of Exeter where she wrote her thesis on 'Women in Arabic Feminist Literature'. She has been considered a prominent member of the Jordanian literary scene as both a poet and fiction writer.

In 1987, Abu Risha published the short story collection 'In the Cell,' winning a prize from the University of Jordan. She has written at least 10 books of poetry beginning in 1998, autobiographical essays, children's literature, and academic studies. Her work extends to feminist criticism, literature, art, and gender and language studies. She has served as editor of magazines, director of feminist publishing houses, and as a university lecturer and director of the Center for Women's Studies in Amman. Her advocacy for women's rights since the early 1980s has made her a target of extremist groups and lawsuits from Amman's Public Prosecution Office.
achievements: Published over 10 books of poetry and numerous literary works
Won University of Jordan prize for short story collection 'In the Cell'
Served as judge for International Prize for Arabic Fiction in 2019
Authored books on gender-inclusive Arabic language
Produced 'Timeless Tales: Folktales Told by Syrian Refugees'

history: The Al Abu Risha family represents multiple distinct lineages sharing the Abu Risha name across different regions. In Iraq, the family is part of the Dulaim tribe within the larger Anizah confederation. Ahmed Abu Risha and his late brother Abdul Sattar Buzaigh al-Rishawi emerged as prominent Sunni tribal leaders in Al-Anbar province during the Iraq War. Following Abdul Sattar's assassination by roadside bomb in September 2007, Ahmed assumed leadership of the Anbar Salvation Council and the Sahwa (Awakening) movement that fought against al-Qaeda. He has maintained significant political influence, leading coalitions in Anbar governorate elections and advocating for Sunni rights while opposing Iranian influence in Iraq. In Syria, a separate Abu Risha lineage produced the renowned poet and diplomat Omar Abu Risha (1910-1990), who served as Syrian ambassador to multiple countries including the United States and wrote Syria's de facto national anthem. The research indicates these are distinct family branches sharing the Abu Risha name rather than a unified genealogical line.
modernStatus: Iraqi branch maintains political influence through tribal leadership and electoral participation in Anbar province. Syrian branch historically involved in diplomacy and literature.

migrationPath.description: Migration of main tribal branch northward to Anbar region
Settlement of family branches in the Trucial States

name: Al Ali
originStory: The Al Ali emerged as a distinguished lineage within the Shammar confederation during the 18th century in the Hail region of Najd. They established themselves as influential leaders controlling strategic trade routes and vast grazing territories in northern Arabia.
description: The Al Ali of Hail are a prominent branch of the Shammar tribal confederation, historically based in northern Najd with extensive influence in trade and pastoral activities. The family has successfully transitioned from traditional Bedouin leadership to modern business and maintains strong tribal connections across the Arabian Peninsula.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Ali Al Ali
notableFigures.title: Tribal Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: 19th century leader who consolidated Al Ali influence in Hail region

history: The research provided focuses entirely on different families and individuals - the Iranian Khamenei family of clerical origin, the Alids (descendants of Ali ibn Abi Talib), Ali al-Sajjad (a Shia imam), the Al Mualla ruling family of Umm Al Quwain from the Al Ali tribe, Iraqi political figures, and Kuwaiti personalities. None of this research relates to the Al Ali family of Hail (id: al_ali_hail) that is the subject of this genealogy request. The existing data indicates they are a branch of the Shammar tribal confederation from the Hail region of Najd, but the provided research documents contain no information about this specific Al Ali lineage, their historical development, business ventures, or modern activities.
modernStatus: Contemporary Al Ali family members are involved in various business sectors across the Gulf, including trade, real estate, and agricultural ventures, while maintaining traditional tribal leadership roles and extensive pastoral interests in Saudi Arabia.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Hail region as part of Shammar tribal expansion
Migration following establishment of modern borders and economic opportunities

name: Al Attiyah
originStory: The Al Attiyah family descended from the Banu Tamim tribe of central Arabia, migrating to Qatar in the 18th century. Their name derives from 'Atiyya' meaning 'gift', reflecting their traditional role as generous tribal leaders.
description: No information found about Al Attiyah family in the provided research text

notableFigures.name: Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah
notableFigures.title: Founder of Al Sadd Sports Club
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder and early leader of Qatar's most successful football club
notableFigures.significance: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969, which became Qatar's most successful football club with 64 official championships.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah was the principal founder of Al Sadd Sports Club, established on 21 October 1969 in Doha, Qatar. He was born and raised in the Al Sadd district of Doha, which gave the club its name. The Al-Attiyah family members excelled in football but did not wish to join any of the existing football clubs at the time.

After consulting with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was the minister of Youth and Sports, Ali bin Hamad Al-Attiyah and his relatives decided to create their own club. They presented their application and club statute to Abdulaziz Buwazair, the operating manager of the Supreme Sports Committee, resulting in the formation of Al Sadd Sports Club. In the club's initial year, Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's performance and made crucial decisions while his relative Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah coached the team.

The club's initial popularity was owed in part to its affiliation with the Al Attiyah tribe, described as a prominent local tribe. Under his leadership and vision, Al Sadd went on to become the most successful sports club in Qatar, winning their first official league title in 1973-74 and eventually accumulating a national record of 64 official football championships.
achievements: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969
Established Qatar's most successful football club
Led club to its first league championship in 1971-72

notableFigures.name: Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah
notableFigures.title: First Coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
notableFigures.roleDescription: First coach and founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
notableFigures.significance: Served as the first coach of Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969 and was part of the founding Al Attiyah family group.
notableFigures.biography:
Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah was one of the founding members of the Al Attiyah family group that established Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969. He served as the club's first coach during its inaugural year of establishment, when the team trained on a football pitch in a local high school in Doha.

While his relative Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's overall performance and made crucial decisions as the founder, Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah took on the practical role of coaching the team. This division of responsibilities between the Al Attiyah family members helped establish the organizational structure that would contribute to the club's early success and eventual dominance in Qatari football.
achievements: First coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
Founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
Helped establish the club's early organizational structure

notableFigures.name: Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah
notableFigures.title: Wife of Former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani
notableFigures.roleDescription: Mother of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
notableFigures.significance: Mother of Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013, representing the Al Attiyah family's connection to the ruling family.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah was one of the wives of Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1972 to 1995. She was the mother of Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa, who would later become Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013 after deposing his father in a bloodless coup.

Her marriage to Sheikh Khalifa represented an important alliance between the Al Attiyah family and the ruling Al Thani family. Through her son Hamad bin Khalifa, the Al Attiyah family maintained significant influence in Qatar's political landscape, as Hamad bin Khalifa became one of Qatar's most transformative rulers, modernizing the country and establishing it as a major regional power.
achievements: Mother of Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
Connected Al Attiyah family to ruling Al Thani dynasty

notableFigures.name: Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah
notableFigures.title: Wife of Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani
notableFigures.roleDescription: Engineer and wife of Qatar's Deputy Emir
notableFigures.significance: Married to Qatar's Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani in 2012, representing continued Al Attiyah family connections to the ruling family.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah is an accomplished engineer who earned her degree from the Qatar University College of Engineering and Computer Science. She is the daughter of Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Hamad Al Attiyah, who served as Premier and Chairman of the Administrative Control and Transparency Authority.

On 22 March 2012, she married Sheikh Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani at Al Wajbah Palace. Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani is the Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive to the throne, having been appointed to this position by his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani in November 2014. Her marriage represents the continuation of strong ties between the Al Attiyah family and Qatar's ruling Al Thani family, maintaining the family's influential position in Qatari society.
achievements: Engineering degree from Qatar University
Married to Deputy Emir of Qatar
Daughter of former Premier of Qatar

notableFigures.name: Nasser Al-Attiyah
notableFigures.title: Rally Driver and Dakar Rally Winner
notableFigures.roleDescription: Professional rally driver and Olympic shooter
notableFigures.significance: Won the 2011 Dakar Rally alongside German co-driver Timo Gottschalk, becoming the first Qatari to win this prestigious motorsport event.
notableFigures.biography:
Nasser Al-Attiyah is a prominent Qatari rally driver who achieved international recognition by winning the 2011 Dakar Rally. He partnered with German co-driver Timo Gottschalk for this historic victory, representing a significant achievement for Qatari motorsport.

Al-Attiyah first competed in the Dakar Rally in 2010 alongside Gottschalk, where they won four stages and finished as runners-up to Volkswagen teammates Carlos Sainz and Lucas Cruz, losing by just over two minutes. The following year in 2011, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk returned with determination and assumed the lead of the rally after victory in the eighth stage. When problems befell their main competitors Sainz and Cruz in the eleventh stage, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk built a commanding lead of over 50 minutes that they maintained until the end of the rally, securing Qatar's first Dakar Rally victory.
achievements: Winner of 2011 Dakar Rally
Runner-up in 2010 Dakar Rally
Won four stages in 2010 Dakar Rally
First Qatari to win Dakar Rally

history: The Al Attiyah family's prominence in Qatar is extensively documented through their founding and leadership of Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969. Ali bin Hamad Al-Attiyah, born and raised in the Al Sadd district of Doha, established the club after consulting with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was minister of Youth and Sports at the time. The family's tribal affiliation with Banu Tamim is confirmed through multiple sources, with Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah's biography explicitly stating that 'His family belongs to the Banu Tamim tribe to which the ruling family of Qatar, the House of Thani, also belong.' The Al Attiyah family has maintained significant influence in Qatar's political and business landscape. Abdullah bin Hamad Al Attiyah served as Qatar's minister of energy and industry from 1992-2011, later becoming deputy prime minister and head of the Emir's court. Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah held multiple high-ranking positions including minister of foreign affairs (2013-2016) and minister of state for defense affairs (2016-2024). The family has also produced notable sports figures like rally champion Nasser Al-Attiyah, who is described as 'a distant relative of Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, Emir of Qatar.' Their business interests span multiple sectors, with Abdullah Al Attiyah serving as chairman and managing director of Qatar Petroleum and holding directorships in Gulf Helicopters Corporation and Gulf Airways Corporation.
modernStatus: Maintains high-level political positions with recent cabinet changes in 2024, significant business interests in energy and telecommunications sectors, and continued sports prominence through Al Sadd Sports Club ownership

migrationPath.description: Migration from central Arabian homeland to establish trading presence in Qatar

name: Al Bu Said
originStory: Founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi around 1744 after he was elected Imam following the expulsion of Persian forces from Oman. The dynasty emerged from the town of Adam in interior Oman during the collapse of the Ya'ariba rule.
description: The Al Bu Said is the ruling royal dynasty of Oman since 1744, originating from the Ya'ariba tribal confederation. They built a vast maritime empire in the 18th-19th centuries and continue to rule modern Oman as an absolute monarchy.

notableFigures.name: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi
notableFigures.title: Imam of Oman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of the Al Bu Said dynasty
notableFigures.significance: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744 and liberated Oman from Persian occupation.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi was born in Adam, Oman in 1694, the son of Sa'id bin Ahmad Al-Busaidi. He came from the Al Bu Sa'id, a small Hinawi tribe from the interior of Oman and was great-great-grandson of Mubarak al-Saidi al-Azdy of the Banu Hiba. Beginning as a wood vendor, he worked his way up to become a leading merchant of the port city of Sohar and was appointed governor of this city in 1737.

When Persian forces under Nader Shah occupied much of Oman between 1742-1744, Ahmad defended Sohar during a nine-month siege and eventually negotiated terms with the Persian commander. By 1747, he had invited the remaining Persian troops to a banquet at his fort in Barka where he massacred them, effectively ending Persian occupation. After defeating rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar in 1749, the Ibadi tribes of Oman elected Ahmad bin Said as their Imam on 9 June 1749 at Rustaq.

Ahmad ruled for 39 generally peaceful years from his seat at Rustaq, encouraging the development of agriculture and maritime trading while maintaining Oman's first permanent army and navy. He reestablished Oman's leading position among the Persian Gulf states and attempted to gain control of the strategic Strait of Hormuz in the late 1770s. He died on 15 December 1783 at Al-Batinah Fort, Rustaq, where he is buried near the Great Mosque.
achievements: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744
Liberated Oman from Persian occupation
Established Oman's first permanent army and navy
Reestablished Oman as a leading Persian Gulf power

notableFigures.name: Haitham bin Tariq Al Said
notableFigures.title: Sultan of Oman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Current Sultan of Oman since 2020
notableFigures.significance: Became Sultan of Oman in 2020 following Qaboos bin Said's death, chosen as successor according to Qaboos's sealed letter.
notableFigures.biography:
Haitham bin Tariq Al Said is the cousin of the late Sultan Qaboos and became the current Sultan of Oman on 11 January 2020. He was previously Minister of Heritage and National Culture and was considered one of the top three contenders to succeed Qaboos, along with his brothers Asa'ad bin Tariq and Shihab bin Tariq. He is the son of Sayyid Tariq bin Taimur, who was Oman's first prime minister and Qaboos's former father-in-law.

When Qaboos died without naming a public heir, the Royal Family Council opened the sealed letter containing Qaboos's choice for successor, which named Haitham bin Tariq as the intended heir. The royal court quickly announced their decision to defer to Qaboos's written choice. In 2021, Sultan Haitham created the position of Crown Prince of Oman by Royal Decree, appointing his eldest son Theyazin bin Haitham to the role, ensuring succession planning for the dynasty.
achievements: Became Sultan of Oman following constitutional succession process
Created the position of Crown Prince in 2021
Appointed his son as first Crown Prince of Oman

notableFigures.name: Said bin Taimur
notableFigures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
notableFigures.roleDescription: 13th Sultan, father of Qaboos, overthrown in 1970 coup
notableFigures.significance: His conservative policies led to his overthrow by his son Qaboos in 1970, ending his isolationist rule.
notableFigures.biography:
Said bin Taimur was born on 13 August 1910 and became the 13th Sultan of Muscat and Oman on 10 February 1932 at age 21, succeeding his father Taimur bin Feisal who had abdicated for financial reasons. He inherited an administration heavily in debt to Britain. He attended Mayo College in India from 1922-1927 and later studied in Baghdad. He secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951 and sold Gwadar to Pakistan for $1 million in 1958.

Despite oil wealth that could have modernized the country, Said became increasingly reclusive and implemented restrictive policies. By 1965, it was forbidden to smoke in public, play football, wear sunglasses, or speak to anyone for more than 15 minutes. The Dhofar province revolted in 1965 with Chinese and Arab nationalist support, followed by an assassination attempt in 1966 that made him even more paranoid. He kept his own son Qaboos under virtual house arrest and refused to speak to him for the last 14 months before the coup.

Under his rule, Oman had an under-5 mortality rate of 25%, widespread disease and malnutrition, only three schools, 5% literacy rate, and just 10 kilometers of paved roads. On 23 July 1970, Qaboos executed a successful British-supported coup against his father at the palace in Salalah. Said was exiled to London where he lived at the Dorchester Hotel until his death on 19 October 1972. He was initially buried at Brookwood Cemetery before his remains were returned to Oman.
achievements: Secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951
Sold Gwadar province to Pakistan in 1958
Maintained friendly relations with the United States during WWII

notableFigures.name: Thuwaini bin Said
notableFigures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Sultan from 1856-1866, ruled Omani portion after empire's division
notableFigures.significance: Became Sultan of Muscat and Oman after his father's death, presiding over the division of the Omani empire between Oman and Zanzibar.
notableFigures.biography:
Thuwaini bin Said al-Busaidi was born in 1821 as the third son of Said bin Sultan. Unlike his brothers, Thuwaini was born in Oman and never visited Zanzibar, serving as his father's representative in Oman when Said was away in Zanzibar. He was appointed heir apparent on 23 July 1844 and had long acted as his father's Governor in Muscat and commander-in-chief of the Saidi forces.

After Said bin Sultan died in 1856, Thuwaini became Sultan of Muscat and Oman while his brother Majid took power in Zanzibar. This led to a struggle between the brothers, with Thuwaini refusing to acknowledge Majid and attempting to regain Zanzibar by force. The British government of India acted as arbitrator, and Lord Canning ruled that the empire should be divided into two separate sultanates: Zanzibar to Majid and Muscat and Oman to Thuwaini, formalized in the 1862 Zanzibar Guarantee Treaty signed in Paris.

Thuwaini insisted that Majid pay 40,000 Maria Theresa thalers annually as compensation for the economic loss caused by the severance of the Zanzibar connection, but the payment fell into arrears and ceased after a year. This left Muscat and Oman in a difficult financial situation, forcing Thuwaini to levy duties on various articles, creating discontent. He was married to his cousin Ralie and had several children. In 1866, he was rumored to have been killed by his own son, Sayyid Salim bin Thuwaini.
achievements: Presided over the formal division of Omani empire in 1862
Served as heir apparent and commander-in-chief under his father
Negotiated compensation payments from Zanzibar for Oman

history: The Al Bu Said dynasty traces its roots to the Azd tribes of Yemen, who migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam and settled in Dibba (Daba) in Oman. The dynasty's progenitor was Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), a celebrated general under the Umayyad Caliphate. The modern Al Bu Said dynasty was founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi in 1744, who emerged from the small Hinawi Al Bu Said tribe from the interior town of Adam. Ahmad began as a wood vendor and merchant in Sohar, rising to become its governor in 1737. When Persian forces invaded Oman, he negotiated their surrender after a nine-month siege of Sohar, then massacred the remaining Persian troops at a banquet in Barka fort in 1747. He was elected Imam by the Ibadi tribes in 1749 at Rustaq, marking the beginning of a dynasty that continues to rule Oman today. The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' who built a vast maritime empire extending from Zanzibar to Gwadar, established Zanzibar as a second capital in 1832, and developed extensive trade networks. Upon Said's death in 1856, the empire was divided by British arbitration into the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman and the Sultanate of Zanzibar. The dynasty modernized Oman under Sultan Qaboos bin Said (1970-2020), who overthrew his father in a palace coup and transformed the country from isolation into a modern state. Today the dynasty continues under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, who succeeded Qaboos in 2020 after being named in the late Sultan's sealed will.
modernStatus: Current ruling dynasty of Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said, maintaining absolute monarchy with extensive business and political control

name: Al Darmaki
originStory: The Al Darmaki originated as part of the Bani Yas confederation in the Liwa Oasis region of what is now Abu Dhabi. They were among the bedouin tribes that formed the backbone of the Al Nahyan-led tribal alliance in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Al Darmaki are a prominent family within the Bani Yas tribal confederation of Abu Dhabi, with origins in the Liwa Oasis region. They have successfully transitioned from traditional bedouin life to modern business and professional roles while maintaining their tribal heritage and loyalty to the Al Nahyan leadership.

notableFigures.name: Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki
notableFigures.title: Film Director
notableFigures.roleDescription: Emirati film director
notableFigures.significance: First Emirati director to direct an American fantasy comedy film released internationally.
notableFigures.biography:
Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki is an Emirati film director who made his directorial debut with the 2015 American fantasy comedy film 'Kids vs Monsters.' The film was released on video on demand on September 29, 2015, marking a significant milestone for Emirati cinema in international markets.

The film featured a notable cast including Malcolm McDowell, Lance Henriksen, Keith David, and Elaine Hendrix. The story centered around six children sent to a mysterious house by their wealthy parents, where they face seven monsters intent on battling them. Despite mixed reviews, with some critics calling it 'tedious' while others found it engaging due to its cast and unique approach, Al Darmaki's work represents an important step in Emirati filmmaking reaching global audiences.
achievements: Directed the 2015 film 'Kids vs Monsters'
Made directorial debut with international cast

notableFigures.biography:
Awad Al Darmaki is an Emirati writer who has contributed to the literary landscape of the United Arab Emirates. He is mentioned among notable Emirati authors and writers, indicating his recognition within the country's cultural and literary circles.

Al Darmaki's work is part of the broader movement of Emirati literature that has emerged alongside the development of the UAE as a nation. He represents the Al Darmaki family's contribution to the intellectual and cultural development of the Emirates, moving beyond traditional roles into modern literary expression.

history: The Al Darmaki are identified as part of the Dhawahir tribe confederation, specifically mentioned as one of the three subsections (Daramikah) who populated Hili and Qattara in the Al Ain oasis region. According to the research, the Dhawahir tribe's traditional area of influence was the Oasis of Al Ain, where they owned much of the cultivated land and property except for Buraimi village itself. The tribe consisted of approximately 4,500 people divided into three subsections: the Daramikah (Al Darmaki), the Jawabir in Al Ain, and the Bani Saad in Jimi. They maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, staying in villages during the summer date season and moving throughout the Trucial States in winter, keeping large flocks of sheep, herding camels, and trading in charcoal. The Dhawahir had a strong historical alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation, though this relationship experienced tensions, particularly during Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's consolidation of power in Al Ain in the late 19th century. The research notes that some Emirati tribes, including Al Darmaki, are said to be of East African origin, suggesting a complex ancestry that may include Afro-Arab heritage alongside their tribal Arabian connections.
modernStatus: Established business family in Abu Dhabi with interests in real estate, construction, and various commercial sectors. Family members serve in government positions and maintain significant social standing within UAE society.

migrationPath.description: Migration from interior oasis to coastal settlement following pearl diving opportunities

description: Al-Dosari is an Arabic surname representing a tribal family lineage with members spread across the Arabian Gulf states including Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain

notableFigures.name: Muna bint Jassim Al Hassan Al Dosari
notableFigures.title: Wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani
notableFigures.roleDescription: Mother of fourteen children to a former Qatari Foreign Minister
notableFigures.significance: She was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, Qatar's Foreign Minister, and mother to nine sons and five daughters.
notableFigures.biography:
Muna bint Jassim Al Hassan Al Dosari was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad bin Abdullah bin Jassim bin Muhammed Al Thani, who served as Qatar's Foreign Minister from February 1972 until his death in August 1985. She was the mother of all fourteen of his children - nine sons and five daughters. Her daughters included Muna bint Suhaim, Rudha bint Suhaim, Amna bint Suhaim, Al Anoud bint Suhaim, and Muneera bint Suhaim.

After her husband's sudden death from a heart attack in August 1985, her sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari, the minister of information and culture, for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention. The sons were subsequently imprisoned after they attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari, demonstrating the family's significant influence and the political tensions within Qatar's ruling circles at the time.
achievements: Mother to fourteen children of a prominent Qatari political figure

notableFigures.name: Yasser Al-Dosari
notableFigures.title: Imam of Masjid al-Haram
notableFigures.roleDescription: Senior Islamic cleric and Imam at Islam's holiest mosque
notableFigures.significance: He serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, one of the most prestigious religious positions in Islam.
notableFigures.biography:
Yasser Al-Dosari is a prominent Islamic scholar who serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram, the Great Mosque of Mecca, which is considered the most significant mosque in Islam and encloses the Kaaba. He was appointed as Imam in 2019 and as Khatib (preacher) in 2022, making him one of the leading religious figures in the Islamic world.

As Imam of Masjid al-Haram, Al-Dosari leads prayers for millions of pilgrims who visit Mecca annually for Hajj and Umrah. The mosque is the main setting for these pilgrimages and serves as the focal point for over 5 million Muslims who perform the Hajj every year. His position places him among the most influential religious leaders in Saudi Arabia and the broader Islamic community, as he delivers sermons and leads prayers at Islam's holiest site.
achievements: Appointed Imam of Masjid al-Haram in 2019
Appointed Khatib of Masjid al-Haram in 2022
Leads prayers for millions of pilgrims annually

notableFigures.biography:
Sami Jasem Al-Dosari is a Saudi Arabian footballer who was born in 1965. As a member of the prominent Dawasir tribe, he represents the family's presence in professional sports in Saudi Arabia. His career coincided with the development of football in the Kingdom during the late 20th century.

While specific details about his club career and achievements are not extensively documented in the available research, his inclusion among notable members of the Dawasir tribe indicates his significance within Saudi Arabian football circles. The Dawasir tribe has a strong presence throughout the Gulf region and has produced notable figures across various fields including politics, religion, and sports.

history: The Dawasir are a large Arab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, with their main stronghold in Wadi al-Dawasir in southern Najd, Saudi Arabia. According to their genealogy, the Dawasir originate from a confederation of tribes including the Azd and Banu Taghlib (Banu Uqayl), with the 'original' Dawasir believed to descend from a military settlement (Doser) in Najd. Their name derives either from their purported forebear Dosser or from the Arabic word meaning 'soldiers,' with Wadi al-Dawasir itself meaning 'Steel Lion' according to some sources. The tribe has historically maintained exceptionally close ties with the Saudi royal family through strategic intermarriages, most notably between King Abdulaziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi of the Sudairi family, a respected Dawasir sub-clan. This marriage produced the famous 'Sudairi Seven' sons who became some of Saudi Arabia's most powerful figures, including Kings Fahd and Salman. The Dawasir played crucial military and political roles in supporting the early Saudi state. In Bahrain, the Dawasir settled mainly in Zallaq and Budaiya, becoming the second most powerful tribe after the Utub. They were so influential that they recognized Sheikh Isa Al Khalifa as ruler in name only and considered themselves exempt from taxation. In 1923, virtually the entire Dawasir tribe left Bahrain for Dammam, Saudi Arabia, opposing the British-supported succession of Sheikh Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. They founded the cities of Dammam and Khobar in 1923 with King Abdulaziz's permission, initially hoping to return to Bahrain but ultimately establishing permanent settlements. The Dawasir were officially allowed to return to Bahrain in April 1927 after King Abdulaziz's intervention. In Qatar, the tribe helped determine maritime borders through their fishing activities near the Hawar Islands, which became part of Bahrain partly due to Dawasir-Khalifa family connections. The tribe also has historical presence in the UAE, particularly on Delma Island, Abu Dhabi, where Fahad Bin Rashid Al Dosari was a prominent pearl merchant. Today, the Dawasir number over 6 million and remain one of the most powerful and influential tribes in Arabia.
modernStatus: The family maintains presence across Gulf states with members in political activism, royal connections, and various professional roles. Some branches have faced political persecution while others have integrated into ruling circles.

name: Al Hamoud Al Nuaimi
originStory: The Al Hamoud branch of the Nuaimi tribe migrated from the Najd region of central Arabia in the 18th century, settling in the northern emirates where they became influential in trade and agriculture. They established themselves as respected tribal leaders and successful merchants in the coastal communities.
description: The Al Hamoud Al Nuaimi family is a distinguished branch of the Nuaimi tribal confederation that settled in the northern emirates during the 18th century. They evolved from traditional Bedouin leaders and merchants to modern business families while maintaining strong tribal connections and cultural heritage.

history: The Al Hamoud Al Nuaimi family represents a distinguished branch of the Nuaimi tribal confederation with documented historical significance in the Al Buraimi region. According to historical records from the Al-Buraimi area, the family played a prominent role in the governance and rebuilding of the region, with Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi and later Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi serving as rulers. The Al Hamoud branch was particularly influential during the period when Mohammed bin Ali and his allies worked to establish peace agreements in territorial conflicts, and the family maintained leadership in the region with strong ties to various tribal groups including the Bani Jaber tribe in Sa'ara. Historical documentation shows that the Al Hamoud family's rule persisted until 1950, with Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud serving as the final ruler of this line. The family's influence extended across the broader Buraimi oasis region, which historically consisted of nine villages and served as an important commercial and cultural center. Their legacy is preserved in the historical fortresses and administrative structures of Al-Buraimi, including Al-Hillah Fort which was built by Sheikh Mohammed bin Ali bin Hamoud Al-Nuaimi and served as a center for local administration.
modernStatus: Contemporary business family with interests in real estate, trading, and various commercial enterprises across the UAE. Family members serve in government positions and cultural organizations while maintaining traditional tribal leadership roles.

name: Al Jaberi
originStory: The Al Jaberi descended from the Bani Yas confederation, with their family name tracing back to an ancestor named Jaber who lived in the Liwa Oasis region. They were part of the traditional Bedouin society that combined oasis agriculture with seasonal pearling and trading.
description: The Al Jaberi are a family lineage within the Bani Yas tribal confederation, traditionally based in the Liwa Oasis region and later settling in Abu Dhabi and other UAE emirates. They have evolved from traditional Bedouin activities to modern business and professional roles while maintaining their tribal heritage.

notableFigures.name: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi
notableFigures.title: Chief of Protocol and Public Relations
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi PMF official
notableFigures.significance: Iraqi Popular Mobilization Forces official who was killed alongside Qasem Soleimani in the 2020 Baghdad airport strike.
notableFigures.biography: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi served as the chief of protocol and public relations for Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF). He was among the ten people killed in the U.S. drone strike on January 3, 2020, at Baghdad International Airport that targeted Iranian General Qasem Soleimani. Al-Jaberi was traveling with Soleimani and Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, the deputy chairman of the PMF, when their convoy was struck by missiles from an MQ-9 Reaper drone as they departed the airport. The strike was ordered by U.S. President Donald Trump and was intended to prevent what American officials claimed were imminent attacks on U.S. personnel. Al-Jaberi's death was part of a broader escalation in U.S.-Iran tensions that brought the region close to open conflict.

notableFigures.name: Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi
notableFigures.title: Scholar and Writer
notableFigures.roleDescription: Tunisian researcher, novelist, and cultural administrator
notableFigures.significance: Prominent Tunisian scholar who specialized in Maghreb literature and served as a cultural administrator with ALECSO.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi was born in Tozeur, Tunisia in February 1940 and died in Ariana, Tunisia in June 2009. He specialized in recording the historical and literary movement in the Maghreb, particularly in Algeria, Libya, and Tunisia, making significant contributions to the study of Tunisian literature and culture. Al-Jaberi received his early education in Tozeur, attending a Kuttab and then Ibn-Shubbaṭ elementary school until 1953, before completing his secondary studies at the Secondary Institution in Tozeur. He continued his education at Zaytuna University in Tunis, obtaining a certificate in 1961, and later studied in Baghdad where he obtained a licence. He earned a Master's of Advanced Study in Algeria in 1980 and a Doctorate degree specializing in Bayram al-Tunisi from the University of Algeria.

Throughout his career, Al-Jaberi held various educational and cultural positions, including teacher in Al-Wardiya District in Tunis, high school teacher in Monastir, director of literature in the Ministry of Culture, and director of the Tunisian Cultural Center in Tripoli. In 1979, he became a member of the Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO), where he supervised the cultural department until his retirement in 2000. After retirement, he was appointed head of an encyclopedic project about notable Arab and Muslim figures and scholars, with nineteen volumes published during his oversight.
achievements: Authored multiple novels including 'Laylatul Sanawatil ʿAshr' which was chosen among the 100 best Arabic novels
Supervised cultural department at ALECSO for over 20 years
Led encyclopedic project on notable Arab and Muslim figures with 19 published volumes
Wrote extensively on Maghreb literature and culture

history: The research reveals a complex pattern of the Al Jaberi name appearing across different cultural and geographical contexts, but does not establish a clear connection between these various Jaberi lineages and the UAE-based Al Jaberi family. The research shows Iranian Jaberis like Mirza Salman Jaberi Isfahani, a 16th-century Safavid grand vizier from Isfahan whose family served the Aq Qoyunlu and claimed descent from the Persian Sufi poet Khvajeh Abdollah Ansari. There are also contemporary Iranian figures like model Mahlagha Jaberi from Isfahan, and Moroccan philosopher Mohammed Abed al-Jabri. Within the UAE context, the research mentions several Al Jaberi individuals associated with the Dhawahir tribe, including footballers Rayan and Rami Yaslam Al Jaberi, actor Ali bin Swelah Al Jaberi, and footballer Waleed Salem Sulaiman Al Jaberi, who are listed as members of the Al Jaberi subsection within the Dhawahir tribal confederation. However, the research does not provide specific historical details about how this particular Al Jaberi lineage within the UAE developed, their migration patterns, or their business activities beyond their existing connection to the Bani Yas confederation and Liwa Oasis region.
modernStatus: Contemporary Al Jaberi family members in the UAE are involved in sports and entertainment, with several professional footballers and actors bearing the name

name: Al Jalahma
originStory: The Al Jalahma originated as part of the Bani Utbah confederation in Najd during the early 18th century. They migrated eastward to the Gulf coast as pearl merchants and maritime traders around 1700-1720.

notableFigures.biography:
Jaber Al Jalahma was the chief of the Al Jalahma family during the 18th century when they were part of the Bani Utbah tribal confederation in Zubarah. The Al Jalahma family, allied under the Bani Utbah tribe, had joined Al Khalifa in Zubarah after migrating from Kuwait, leaving Al Sabah as the sole proprietors of Kuwait.

However, disagreements arose between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma over the distribution of revenues from their activities in Zubarah. This led Al Jalahma to move to Ar Ru'ays, just east of Zubarah. The conflict escalated when Al Khalifa attacked them there and killed their chief Jaber Al Jalahma, marking a significant moment in the internal struggles within the Bani Utbah confederation.

notableFigures.name: Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma
notableFigures.title: Leader and Naval Commander
notableFigures.roleDescription: Leader of Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan who opposed Al Khalifa through piracy and military alliances
notableFigures.significance: He was a prominent opponent of Al Khalifa rule, conducting piracy against their ships and supporting their enemies until his death in a dramatic battle in 1826.
notableFigures.biography:
Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma was the leader of the Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. He held a deep grudge against the Al Khalifa family, likely stemming from earlier conflicts within the Bani Utbah confederation, including the killing of his predecessor Jaber Al Jalahma by Al Khalifa forces.

Rahmah bin Jabir actively opposed Al Khalifa rule through maritime warfare and political alliances. He pirated their ships and consistently supported their enemies in various conflicts. His opposition was significant enough to pose a continuous threat to Al Khalifa authority and trade interests. His resistance came to an end in 1826 when he was killed in what contemporary sources described as a dramatic battle, finally removing a persistent thorn in the side of Al Khalifa rule.
achievements: Led sustained maritime resistance against Al Khalifa rule
Maintained Al Jalahma autonomy in Khor Hassan

notableFigures.name: Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma
notableFigures.title: Shaikha
notableFigures.roleDescription: Second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, ruler of Bahrain
notableFigures.significance: She was the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who ruled Bahrain from 1932 to 1942, representing a marriage alliance between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma families.
notableFigures.biography:
Shaikha Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma was a member of the prominent Al Jalahma family who became the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who would later become the ruler of Bahrain from 1932 to 1942. Her marriage to Hamad represented a significant political alliance between the Al Khalifa ruling family and the Al Jalahma family, which had historically been both allies and rivals within the broader Bani Utbah tribal confederation.

This marriage alliance was part of the complex web of relationships that characterized Gulf politics in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, where intermarriage between prominent families served to strengthen political bonds and resolve historical grievances. Through her marriage to the future ruler, she became part of the ruling household during a crucial period in Bahrain's history, including the discovery of oil and the modernization of the country's administration.

history: Al Jalahma are derived from the Anizah tribe of Northern Central Arabia, one of the ancient Arabian tribes within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites. They were one of the four Utub clans who founded the modern state of Kuwait in 1716, along with Al Bin Ali, Al Sabah and Al Khalifa. The Jalahimas were expelled from Umm Qasr by the Ottomans due to their predatory habits of preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in Shatt al-Arab, after which they migrated to Kuwait. Around the 1760s, the Jalahimas, along with the Al Khalifas, migrated to Zubarah in modern-day Qatar, leaving Al Sabah as the sole proprietors of Kuwait. The two tribes established a free-trade port at Zubarah's harbor, but the Al Khalifas refused to share the economic gains with the Al Jalahma, causing the latter to migrate to Al Ruwais while Al Khalifa monopolized the pearl banks around Qatar. In 1783, putting aside their grievances, the Utub tribes of Al Jalahma and Al Khalifa drove out the Persians from Bahrain in an expedition launched partly due to Persian aggression towards Zubarah in 1782. However, Al Jalahma seceded from the Utub alliance before the annexation was complete, as they considered themselves treated unfairly by the Al Khalifas who did not share their political or territorial gains. This left Al Khalifa in undisputed possession of Bahrain. The Al Jalahma tribe then allied itself with the Wahhabis of Arabia, while the Al Khalifas aligned with the Omanis. In 1811, Said bin Sultan of Muscat attacked the Wahhabi garrisons, and the same year, the famous Al Jalahma leader Rahmah ibn Jabir al-Jalahimah fought a naval battle against the Al Khalifas. The heated confrontations culminated in 1826 when another naval battle ensued, resulting in the deaths of many including pilot Ahmad ibn Salman Al Khalifa, after Rahmah ibn Jabir al-Jalahimah blew up his own ship in close quarters. Today, members of the clan are scattered throughout the Arabian Peninsula, with most Al Jalahmas found in Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, and despite their secession from Bani Utbah, they are still widely regarded as part of the tribal confederation.
modernStatus: Prominent in Bahraini healthcare and government administration, with members serving in high-level positions including the Consultative Council and Ministry of Health

name: Al Jarba
originStory: The Al Jarba emerged as a leading branch within the powerful Shammar tribal confederation during the 18th and 19th centuries. They established their authority over vast territories in northern Arabia and the Jazirah region of Mesopotamia.
description: The Al Jarba family is a prominent branch of the Shammar tribal confederation, historically influential in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They have successfully transitioned from traditional tribal leadership to modern political and business prominence across the Arab world.

notableFigures.name: Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba
notableFigures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
notableFigures.significance: Mother of six children of King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia, including Prince Mishaal and Prince Turki.
notableFigures.biography:
Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba was one of the wives of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud of Saudi Arabia. She belonged to the prominent Al Jarba family of the Iraqi branch of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah was part of traditional tribal alliance-building between the Saudi royal family and major tribal confederations.

Tathi gave birth to six children with King Abdullah, including notable sons Prince Mishaal, who served as governor of the Makkah Province between 2013 and 2015, and Prince Turki, who was a pilot in the Royal Saudi Air Force and served as governor of the Riyadh Province from 2014 to 2015. She also had a daughter Princess Oraib. Through her children, Tathi's lineage became integrated into the Saudi royal family, strengthening ties between the Al Saud and the Shammar confederation.
achievements: Mother of Saudi princes including governors of major provinces

notableFigures.name: Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri
notableFigures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
notableFigures.significance: Another wife of King Abdullah from the Al Jarba family, mother of three royal children including Prince Saad and Prince Sultan.
notableFigures.biography:
Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri was another wife of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud from the prominent Al Jarba family of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah represented the continued alliance between the Saudi royal family and the influential Shammar confederation.

Malka had three children with King Abdullah: Prince Saad, Princess Sahab, and Prince Sultan. Her marriage demonstrates the practice of multiple marriages within the same tribal family to strengthen political and tribal alliances. The Al Jarba family's connection to the Saudi royal house through multiple marriages reflects their significant status within the broader tribal hierarchy of the Arabian Peninsula.
achievements: Mother of three Saudi royal children

notableFigures.name: Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Shammar tribal leader killed in Wahhabi conflicts
notableFigures.significance: His death in 1791 triggered a major migration of Shammar clans from Najd to Iraq under Al-Jarba leadership.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba was a prominent leader of the Shammar tribe during the late 18th century when the tribe faced increasing pressure from Wahhabi expansion in the Arabian Peninsula. He led the tribe during a period of intense conflict with the emerging Saudi state and their Wahhabi allies.

Muslit's death in 1791 during the Wahhabi wars marked a turning point for the Shammar confederation. His killing triggered a major exodus of Shammar clans from their traditional territories in Najd to safer regions in southern Iraq. Under the leadership of the Al-Jarba family, particularly Mutlag Al-Jarba, large numbers of Shammar migrated northward to escape continued Wahhabi pressure and raids. This migration established the foundation for the significant Shammar presence in Iraq that continues to this day, where they became one of the country's largest tribal confederations.
achievements: Led Shammar resistance against early Wahhabi expansion

notableFigures.name: Mutlag Al-Jarba
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Leader of Shammar migration to Iraq
notableFigures.significance: Led the major Shammar exodus from Arabia to Iraq in the 1790s following Wahhabi conflicts.
notableFigures.biography:
Mutlag Al-Jarba was a crucial figure in Shammar tribal history who led the great migration of the tribe from their ancestral lands in Najd to Iraq in the 1790s. Following the death of Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba in 1791, Mutlag took leadership of the Shammar and guided them to safer territories in southern Iraq to escape the advancing Wahhabi forces of the first Saudi state.

Mutlag successfully established Shammar settlements in southern Iraq and organized the tribe's resistance against continued Saudi raids. However, his leadership was cut short when he was killed in 1795 during the Battle of Alabyadh, some sources suggest, as Saudi forces continued their attacks even after the Shammar had relocated. His death marked the end of the initial phase of Shammar settlement in Iraq, but the foundation he laid enabled the tribe to become one of Iraq's most influential tribal confederations under subsequent Al-Jarba leadership.
achievements: Led the great Shammar migration to Iraq
Established Shammar presence in Mesopotamia

notableFigures.name: Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Shammar leader in early 19th century Iraq
notableFigures.significance: Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families in Iraq and successfully defeated the Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805.
notableFigures.biography:
Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba emerged as a prominent leader of the Iraqi Shammar in the early 19th century, following in the footsteps of earlier Al-Jarba chiefs who had led the tribe's migration from Arabia. He took leadership after Faris Al-Jarba and commanded significant influence over the large Shammar population that had settled in the Mesopotamian region.

In 1805, Faris achieved a decisive military victory by defeating the Al-Ubaid tribe, which significantly strengthened Shammar control over their territories in the Jazirah plain between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. By 1836, he ruled over approximately 12,000 Shammar families, making him one of the most powerful tribal leaders in Iraq. His leadership helped consolidate Shammar power in their new homeland and established them as a dominant force in Iraqi tribal politics.
achievements: Defeated Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805
Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families

notableFigures.name: Ahmad Jarba
notableFigures.title: Political Opposition Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Syrian National Council member and founder of Syria's Tomorrow Movement
notableFigures.significance: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement in 2016 and served as a key Syrian opposition leader during the civil war.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad Jarba is a prominent Syrian opposition political leader and member of the Syrian National Council who founded the Syria's Tomorrow Movement in March 2016 in Cairo. The party was backed by Egypt and the United Arab Emirates and cooperates with the National Coalition for Syrian Revolutionary and Opposition Forces, although it maintains independence from the coalition. Jarba also works with the Syrian Democratic Council of the Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria.

The Syria's Tomorrow Movement under Jarba's leadership aims to decentralize Syria and implement pluralist democracy while opposing any partition of the state. The party considers all ethnic groups, including Alawites, to be essential components of the Syrian people and calls for greater inclusion in the opposition. In September 2016, Jarba met with Kurdish leaders to form agreements for governing northeastern Syria, and his movement signed cooperation agreements with various Syrian opposition groups including the National Coordination Committee for Democratic Change.
achievements: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement
Syrian National Council member
Negotiated Syrian opposition agreements

history: The Al Jarba family emerged as a prominent branch of the powerful Shammar tribal confederation, descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the second century CE. Under the leadership of Al Jarba sheikhs, particularly during the late 18th century conflicts with the first Saudi state, a massive exodus of Shammar occurred into Iraq around 1791-1801. This migration was triggered by Wahabi wars and the killing of Sheikh Muslet bin Motlak Al-Jarba in battles against Ibn Saud. Led by Sheikh Mutlag Aljarba and later his successors including Faris bin Mohammad and Sfoug bin Faris Aljarba, the Al Jarba dominated Shammar migrants in Iraq and became part of a tribal coalition under Ottoman auspices to resist Wahabi raids on southern Iraq from 1798-1801. They successfully established control over the Jazirah region between the Tigris and Euphrates, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Al Jarba maintained their position as Sheikhs of Sheikhs of Shammar, with the family producing notable political figures including Ghazi al-Yawar, who was chosen as interim president of Iraq after the overthrow of Saddam Hussein. The family's influence extended beyond Iraq, with branches settling in Syria where they continue to hold leadership positions among the Shammar there.
modernStatus: Continue to hold the position of Sheikh of Sheikhs of Shammar, with Ghazi al-Yawar's uncle serving as current paramount sheikh, and family members holding significant political positions in Iraq and Syria

history: The Al Khalifa belong to the Anizah tribe and profess Sunni Islam. They trace their lineage to Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa, who became the first hakim of Bahrain and progenitor of the ruling family. The family was part of the Utub alliance that migrated from Najd in central Arabia to Kuwait, then ruled Qatar, specifically building and ruling Al Zubarah, before settling in Bahrain. Originally, their ancestors were expelled from Umm Qasr in central Arabia by the Ottomans due to predatory habits of preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in Shatt al-Arab waterway, forcing them to Kuwait in 1716 where they remained until 1766. Around the 1760s, the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma, both belonging to the Utub Federation, migrated to Zubarah in modern-day Qatar. The House of Khalifa moved from Qatar to Bahrain in 1799 after Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa conquered Bahrain from Nasr Al-Madhkur in 1783, following the defeat of Persian control at the 1782 Battle of Zubarah. Ahmed ruled Bahrain as hakim until his death in 1796, spending summers in Bahrain while based in Zubarah. Following his death, his two sons Salman and Abdulla moved to Bahrain and co-ruled it, with Salman settling in Bahrain Island and Abdulla in Muharraq Island. The Al Khalifa split into two branches, Al-Abdulla and Al-Salman, engaging in open conflict between 1842 and 1846. The Al-Salman branch was victorious and enjoyed complete rule of Bahrain, while Abdulla ibn Ahmad Al Khalifa's descendants live in Qatar today, and Salman ibn Ahmad Al Khalifa's descendants live in Bahrain. In 1820, the Al Khalifa were recognized by the United Kingdom as rulers of Bahrain after signing a treaty relationship, eventually becoming a British protectorate. Since 1783, the Al Khalifa have continuously ruled Bahrain, with the current head being King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who became Emir in 1999 and proclaimed himself King in 2002, establishing Bahrain as a constitutional monarchy.
modernStatus: Ruling royal family of Bahrain with King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa as current monarch. Family members occupy key government positions including Prime Minister, Deputy Prime Minister, and various ministerial roles.

history: The House of Maktoum is the ruling royal family of the Emirate of Dubai and one of the six ruling families of the United Arab Emirates. The family is a branch of the Bani Yas clan, a tribal federation that was the dominant power through the region that now forms the UAE, sharing this lineage with the Al Nahyan dynasty of Abu Dhabi. In 1833, about 800 members of the Bani Yas tribe, under the joint leadership of Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said, took over the emirate of Dubai. After Obeid bin Said died of old age in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti took the reins as the sole ruler and established the Al Maktoum dynasty in the emirate. The Al Maktoum dynasty has ruled Dubai continuously since 1833. The family belongs to the Al Bu Falasah tribe, a sub-branch of the Bani Yas. Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum (1912-1990) transformed Dubai into a modern commercial hub, developing infrastructure including Dubai Creek, Dubai Airport (1960), Port Rashid (1972), and Jebel Ali Port (1979). He played a crucial role alongside Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan in establishing the United Arab Emirates federation in 1971, serving as the first Vice President and later Prime Minister. The family has maintained significant business interests, notably owning Godolphin, a private Thoroughbred horseracing stable that competes in major races worldwide. The current ruler, Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, has continued the family's tradition of ambitious development projects, with major infrastructure named after the family including Al Maktoum International Airport.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Dubai Emirate; holds key UAE federal positions including Vice President and Prime Minister; owns major business interests including Godolphin racing stables and oversees Dubai's transformation into global commercial hub

migrationPath.description: Al Bu Falasah migration led by Maktoum bin Butti following disputes with Al Nahyan rulers
