name: Al Mu'ammar
originStory: The Al Mu'ammar family descends from the ancient Banu Tamim tribal confederation of central Arabia. They migrated from the Najd region to coastal Gulf areas during the 18th and 19th centuries.
description: The Al Mu'ammar is a prominent Arabian family originating from the Banu Tamim tribe, with historical roots in the Najd region who migrated to coastal areas of the Arabian Gulf. They have maintained significant business and cultural influence across the GCC region through traditional and modern economic activities.

notableFigures.name: Wasmiyah bint Abdul Rahman Al Mu'ammar
notableFigures.nameAr: وسمية بنت عبد الرحمن آل معمر
notableFigures.title: Mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar
notableFigures.roleDescription: Mother of a senior Saudi royal and governor
notableFigures.significance: Mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, who served as governor of Al-Qassim Province and later Riyadh Province.
notableFigures.biography:
Wasmiyah bint Abdul Rahman Al Mu'ammar was a member of the prominent Al Mu'ammar merchant family. She married Prince Bandar bin Abdulaziz, a son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz. Their eldest son, Prince Faisal, was born in 1945 and went on to have a distinguished career in Saudi government service.

As the mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar, she was connected to the highest levels of Saudi governance through her son's roles as deputy governor and later governor of Asir Province, governor of Al-Qassim Province for over two decades, and eventually governor of Riyadh Province from 2015.

notableFigures.name: Uthman ibn Mu'ammar
notableFigures.title: Emir of Uyayna
notableFigures.roleDescription: 18th century ruler of Uyayna who supported Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's reforms
notableFigures.significance: Emir of Uyayna who implemented the religious reforms of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, including the destruction of venerated tombs, before being forced to expel the reformer in 1744.
notableFigures.biography:
Uthman ibn Mu'ammar was the emir (ruler) of Uyayna in central Arabia during the 18th century, when the town had become the leading settlement of the Nejd region. When the Islamic reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab returned to Uyayna in 1740 and began preaching against what he considered religious innovations, Ibn Mu'ammar was convinced to implement these ideas.

Under Ibn Mu'ammar's authority, the tomb of Zayd ibn al-Khattab, brother of the second Muslim Caliph Omar, was leveled as part of the Wahhabi campaign against the veneration of Muslim saints. However, in 1744, the Banu Khalid tribe, who held considerable influence over Uyayna from their base in eastern Arabia, compelled Ibn Mu'ammar to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab from the town. The reformer subsequently found refuge with the Al Saud clan in nearby Dir'iyyah, leading to the eventual Saudi conquest of the region.
achievements: Implemented Wahhabi religious reforms in Uyayna
Ordered the destruction of venerated tombs

history: The research provided focuses exclusively on Muammar Gaddafi (c. 1942-2011), a Libyan political leader from the Qadhadhfa tribe, which was described as 'a small, relatively uninfluential tribe' that was 'Arab in heritage.' Born near Sirte in Italian Libya to a poor Bedouin Arab family, Gaddafi's father was a goat and camel herder. The family belonged to the traditional nomadic Bedouin culture of Libya's desert regions. Gaddafi came to power through a 1969 military coup and ruled Libya until 2011. However, this research does not establish any connection between Muammar Gaddafi's Libyan Qadhadhfa tribe and the Al Mu'ammar family of the Gulf region that originates from the Banu Tamim confederation. The name similarity appears coincidental, as the research provides no evidence of familial, tribal, or geographical connections between Gaddafi's Libyan lineage and the GCC-based Al Mu'ammar family.
modernStatus: The family maintains business interests across the GCC in real estate, trading, and service sectors, with members holding various commercial and civic positions while preserving their tribal heritage and cultural connections.

history: The Al Mualla family is the ruling royal family of Umm Al Quwain, one of the seven emirates of the UAE, and has traditionally been at the head of the Al Ali tribe. The Al Ali tribe originated in Nejd and initially settled on Sinniyah Island before moving to the mainland after water supplies on the island were exhausted. Sheikh Rashid bin Majid Al Mualla was the first known head of the Al Ali when they settled at Umm Al Quwain and was responsible for the construction of Umm Al Quwain Fort in 1768. The Al Ali tribe was approximately 6,750 strong at the turn of the 19th century, with 1,000 families settled at Umm Al Quwain and others at the inland town of Falaj Al Ali (later known as Falaj Al Mualla), with smaller populations in Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. There was also a Persian group of Al Ali who referred to the Umm Al Quwain section as 'Al Mualla'. The emirate was formed in 1775 when Sheikh Majid Al Mualla established it as an independent sheikhdom. On 8 January 1820, Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid signed the General Maritime Treaty with the United Kingdom, accepting British protectorate status. By 1908, Umm Al Quwain was identified as the major boat-building centre on the coast. On 2 December 1971, Sheikh Ahmad bin Rashid joined the other emirates in forming the United Arab Emirates. The current ruler, Sheikh Saud bin Rashid Al Mualla, has ruled since 2009 and is a member of the UAE Federal Supreme Council.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Umm Al Quwain emirate, members of UAE Federal Supreme Council, involved in government administration and economic development initiatives including tourism, trade, and the UAQ Free Trade Zone

notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan is the brother of Crown Prince Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan and son of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He serves as chairman of the Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court and is a member of the Executive Council. He also holds the position of chairman of the Early Childhood Authority, Abu Dhabi Transport Company, and Etihad Rail, demonstrating his role in key infrastructure and development projects in Abu Dhabi. He is married to Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan and they have three children together: Fatima, Salama, and Zayed.

notableFigures.biography: Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan is a member of the ruling Al Nahyan family and the wife of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Her father, Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Nahyan, was a former UAE deputy prime minister. She married Mohamed bin Zayed in 1980 and they have nine children together – four sons and five daughters – as well as two adopted daughters. She is known for her philanthropic activities and serves as founder and president of the Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan Foundation based in Abu Dhabi. She also chairs the Abu Dhabi Art Host Committee and holds senior positions including chair of the General Women's Union, president of the Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood, and supreme chair of the Family Development Foundation. She is a significant art collector, owning works by Pablo Picasso, Rudolf Ernst, and Damien Hirst.

notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born in 1988 and is one of the sons of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. He married Sheikha Fakhra bint Khalifa Al Nahyan in 2021, connecting him to another branch of the Al Nahyan family. They have two sons together: Mohammed (born 15 August 2022) and Khalifa (born 28 February 2024). As a member of the next generation of the Al Nahyan family, he represents the future leadership of the emirate.

notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, being one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi). He was mentioned as having competed for the position of Crown Prince before it was ultimately given to Khaled bin Mohamed by his father. Instead, Tahnoun was appointed to oversee a sovereign wealth fund worth $1.5 trillion, demonstrating his continued importance in the UAE's financial and economic affairs. He has been involved in various diplomatic and intelligence activities.

notableFigures.significance: One of the senior brothers in the Al Nahyan family and member of the Bani Fatima.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Abdullah bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He is one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi), making him a full brother to Mohamed, Hamdan, Hazza, Tahnoun, and Mansour. As a senior member of the ruling family, he plays an important role in the governance and administration of Abu Dhabi and the UAE.

notableFigures.title: Senior Royal

notableFigures.significance: One of the senior brothers in the Al Nahyan family and member of the Bani Fatima.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Hazza bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He is one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi), making him a full brother to Mohamed, Hamdan, Tahnoun, Mansour, and Abdullah. As a senior member of the ruling family, he plays a role in the governance and administration of the emirate and the federation.

notableFigures.name: Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
notableFigures.title: Senior Royal
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of former President Khalifa bin Zayed
notableFigures.significance: Eldest son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan is the eldest son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Sheikha Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei. He is married to Sheikha bint Saif Al Nahyan and they have three sons and three daughters together. As the son of a former president and member of the senior generation of the Al Nahyan family, he holds an important position within the royal family structure.

notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of former President Khalifa bin Zayed
notableFigures.significance: Son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan is one of the sons of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Sheikha Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei. He is married to Al-Yazia bint Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan and they have three sons together. As a member of the Al Nahyan family and son of a former president, he represents the continuing influence of Khalifa's branch of the family.

notableFigures.name: Zayed bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
notableFigures.title: Royal Family Member
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of President Mohamed bin Zayed
notableFigures.significance: Youngest son of the current UAE president, representing the next generation of Al Nahyan leadership.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Zayed bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 24 October 1995 and is the youngest son of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. He graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in December 2020, following in the military tradition of his family. Named after his grandfather, the founding father of the UAE, he represents the next generation of Al Nahyan leadership.
achievements: Graduated from Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in 2020

notableFigures.name: Issa bin Zayed Al Nahyan
notableFigures.title: Royal Family Member
notableFigures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
notableFigures.significance: Member of the Al Nahyan royal family who was subject to international controversy over torture allegations.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Issa bin Zayed is a member of the Al Nahyan royal family and brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. In April 2009, he became the subject of international controversy when he was allegedly shown in video footage torturing a man. This incident brought negative attention to the Al Nahyan family and raised questions about accountability within the royal family structure.

history: Al Nahyan is the ruling royal family of Abu Dhabi and the UAE, belonging to the House of Al Falahi (Āl Bū Falāḥ), a branch of the Bani Yas tribe. The Bani Yas came to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis. Al Nahyan has ruled Abu Dhabi since 1793, and previously ruled Liwa. The family's genealogical name traces to Nahyan bin Mubarak of the Bani Yas confederation, though the research indicates they are related to the House of Al Falasi from which Dubai's Al Maktoum also descends. The family established their seat of power in Abu Dhabi in 1761 under Sheikh Dhiyab bin Isa. The rule has been marked by significant instability - five rulers were overthrown and eight were killed in coups between 1793 and 1966, many of whom were brothers. Notable rulers include Saqr bin Zayed Al Nahyan (1926-1928) who killed his half-brother Sultan bin Zayed to become ruler, before being assassinated by members of the Al-Bu Shaar section of the Al Manasir tribe. His nephew Shakhbut bin Sultan Al Nahyan ruled from 1928-1966 but was deposed by his family with British assistance due to his refusal to spend petroleum royalties after oil discovery in 1958. Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan then ruled and became the founding president of the UAE in 1971. The current ruler is Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, who became president of the UAE in 2022 after his half-brother Khalifa's death. The family controls massive sovereign wealth funds including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and Mubadala Investment Company with an estimated US$1 trillion in assets, making them the second richest family globally as of 2024.
modernStatus: Ruling family of UAE and Abu Dhabi, controlling massive sovereign wealth funds including ADIA and Mubadala with $1 trillion in assets. Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed serves as UAE President since 2022.

migrationPath.description: Bani Yas migration from Liwa Oasis to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century

name: Al Naimi
originStory: The family originates from the Na'im tribe of the Arabian Peninsula, with their rise to political prominence beginning when they established rule over Ajman in the early 19th century.
legitimacyBasis: Traditional tribal leadership and modern constitutional monarchy
description: The Al Naimi (النعيمي) appears to refer to multiple related entities: the Al Nuaimi ruling royal family of Ajman, UAE, and the broader Na'im Arab tribe found across the UAE and other Arab states. Various prominent individuals bearing this name are found across different Gulf countries in fields including politics, academia, sports, and human rights.

notableFigures.name: Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi
notableFigures.nameAr: علي ماجد علي حسن الماجد النعيمي
notableFigures.title: Bahraini Lawyer and Politician
notableFigures.roleDescription: Member of Bahrain's Council of Representatives
notableFigures.significance: Became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi is a Bahraini lawyer and politician who was sworn into the Council of Representatives on December 12, 2018, representing the Seventh District of the Southern Governorate. He earned a master's degree from the Applied Science University in 2012 and followed it up with a doctorate from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), a division of the Arab League's Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization. His dissertation was a comparative study of the state's responsibility for public servants' mistakes.

In the 2018 Bahraini general election, Al-Naimi ran to represent the Seventh District in the Southern Governorate in the Council of Representatives. He received 1,956 votes for 28.07% in the first round on November 24, necessitating a runoff on December 1, in which he defeated his opponent, Ahmed Al-Tamimi, with 3,267 votes for 52.81%. His election was historically significant as he became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father, Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi, held a ministerial portfolio as Minister of Education.

His career has been marked by controversy, including a 2016 report from the Financial and Administrative Control Department showing his absence for seven unaccounted months from work at the National Institute for Human Rights during which he earned BD17,542 in salaries. During the 2018 Bahrain forged degree scandal, his doctorate's provenance from the IARS came under scrutiny, as the Ministry of Education had issued a statement in 2010 stating that degrees from the institute would not be recognized.
achievements: First person to win election as deputy while father held ministerial position

notableFigures.name: Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi
notableFigures.title: Bahraini Minister of Education
notableFigures.roleDescription: Bahraini academic and government minister
notableFigures.significance: Has served as Bahrain's Minister of Education since 2002.
notableFigures.biography:
Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi is a Bahraini academic who has served as Minister of Education since 2002. He is the father of Ali bin Majid Al-Naimi, who made history in 2018 by becoming the first person to win election as a deputy in Bahrain's House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.

During his long tenure as Minister of Education, Al-Naimi has overseen significant developments in Bahrain's educational sector. His ministry was involved in the 2018 controversy regarding degrees from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), where the ministry had issued a statement in 2010 directing applicants to avoid registering at the institute due to significant reservations about its accreditation.
achievements: Long-serving Minister of Education since 2002

history: The Na'im is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe prominent in the United Arab Emirates and other Gulf countries, divided into three main sections: Al Bu Kharaiban, Khawatir, and Al Bu Shamis. The tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where their expansion came at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. At their peak in 1818, they numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi according to British records, making them arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe followed the Ghafiri tradition and supported the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. Although initially linked to Wahhabi influence in the Buraimi area, the Na'im allied with the Al Bu Falah ruler Khalifah bin Shakhbut in 1848 and Sultan Azzan bin Qais in 1869 to evict the Wahhabis from Buraimi, subsequently occupying many forts around the oasis. However, following the death of Zayed the Great, they came under Saudi influence again, leading to their participation in the Buraimi dispute of the 1950s. The tribe experienced significant decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s. The Al Bu Shamis section became virtually independent and closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, while the Al Bu Kharaiban section provided the rulers of Ajman. During the Buraimi dispute in 1952-1955, some 200 Na'im, Al Bu Shamis, and Bani Kaab fought against British forces, with Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis assisting Saudi forces in occupying Hamasa village. Following the conflict, many Na'im were resettled in Saudi Arabia, while others settled in the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi.
modernStatus: The family includes ruling royalty in Ajman (Al Nuaimi ruling family), former high-ranking government officials like Ali Al-Naimi (former Saudi oil minister), and various professionals across Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE in fields including politics, academia, sports, and human rights.

subTribes.name: Khawatir
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
subTribes.description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

subTribes.name: Al Bu Shamis
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
subTribes.description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

name: Al Nuaimi
currentHead: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III
originStory: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty followers took the coastal settlement of Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis tribe in a short conflict. Rule was established when the Ajman fort fell to Rashid's followers in 1816 or 1817.
legitimacyBasis: Military conquest of coastal settlement from Al Bu Shamis tribe, endorsed by Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi
description: The Al Nuaimi family is the ruling dynasty of the Emirate of Ajman in the UAE, having governed since 1816. They are descendants of the ancient Nuaimi tribal confederation and have successfully transformed their emirate from a small fishing and pearling settlement into a modern commercial center.

notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Ahmed bin Humaid Al Nuaimi is one of the sons of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. He serves as the Chairman of the Economic Department in the Emirate of Ajman, playing a key role in the emirate's economic development and policy-making. As a member of the ruling family, he contributes to the governance and modernization efforts of Ajman under his father's rule.
achievements: Chairman of Economic Department

notableFigures.significance: Son of the current ruler with extensive roles including President of the UAE Football Association.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi IV is one of the sons of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III and Sheikha Fatima bint Zayed. He holds multiple important positions within Ajman and nationally. He serves as Chairman of Ajman Municipality and Planning, overseeing urban development and infrastructure projects in the emirate. Additionally, he is the Chief of Ajman Club and heads R Holding company. He is also the owner of City University College of Ajman, contributing to the emirate's educational sector. At the national level, he serves as President of the UAE Football Association (UAEFA), playing a significant role in the development of football across the United Arab Emirates.

notableFigures.biography: Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi was the sister of the current ruler of Ajman, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. She died on 14 December 2014. Her significance extends beyond Ajman as she was the mother of Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi, who is the ruler of Fujairah, another emirate in the UAE. This family connection represents important inter-emirate relationships within the UAE's ruling families, as her son rules one emirate while her brother rules another. Her only son's position as ruler of Fujairah demonstrates the interconnected nature of the UAE's royal families.
achievements: Mother of Fujairah's ruler
Connected Al Nuaimi family to Al Sharqi ruling family

notableFigures.title: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former UAE Foreign Minister and diplomat
notableFigures.significance: Former Foreign Minister of the UAE and member of the Al Nuaimi ruling family of Ajman.
notableFigures.biography: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967. He started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs, where he worked in various capacities until 1975. In 1975, he was named as the director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976, he became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as the minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.
achievements: UAE Minister of State for Foreign Affairs (1977-1990)
Received Order of the Two Niles from Sudan (2018)
Received Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan (2024)
Served as undersecretary for foreign affairs

history: The Na'im is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe prominent in the United Arab Emirates and other Gulf countries, divided into three main sections: Al Bu Kharaiban, Khawatir, and Al Bu Shamis. The tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where their expansion came at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. At their peak in 1818, they numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi according to British records, making them arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe followed the Ghafiri tradition and supported the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. Although initially linked to Wahhabi influence in the Buraimi area, the Na'im allied with the Al Bu Falah ruler Khalifah bin Shakhbut in 1848 and Sultan Azzan bin Qais in 1869 to evict the Wahhabis from Buraimi, subsequently occupying many forts around the oasis. However, following the death of Zayed the Great, they came under Saudi influence again, leading to their participation in the Buraimi dispute of the 1950s. The tribe experienced significant decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s. The Al Bu Shamis section became virtually independent and closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, while the Al Bu Kharaiban section provided the rulers of Ajman. During the Buraimi dispute in 1952-1955, some 200 Na'im, Al Bu Shamis, and Bani Kaab fought against British forces, with Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis assisting Saudi forces in occupying Hamasa village. Following the conflict, many Na'im were resettled in Saudi Arabia, while others settled in the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Ajman Emirate, members of UAE Federal Supreme Council, involved in government administration, banking (Ajman Bank), and charitable foundations

subTribes.name: Khawatir
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
subTribes.description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

subTribes.name: Al Bu Shamis
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
subTribes.description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

name: Al Qasimi
originStory: The Al Qasimi originated from Hawala Arab tribes and established their power base in Julfar (Ras Al Khaimah) in the early 18th century. They quickly expanded to control both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, becoming the dominant maritime force in the lower Arabian Gulf.
description: The Al Qasimi are a prominent ruling family of the UAE, historically controlling the emirates of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They rose to power as a major maritime force in the 18th-19th centuries, controlling Gulf trade routes before adapting to modern governance and business.

notableFigures.name: Lubna Khalid Al Qasimi
notableFigures.nameAr: لبنى بنت خالد بن سلطان القاسمي
notableFigures.title: Sheikha, Minister, University President
notableFigures.roleDescription: First female minister in UAE, current President of Zayed University
notableFigures.significance: She was the first woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE and served in multiple high-ranking government positions.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Lubna bint Khalid bin Sultan Al Qasimi was born on 4 February 1962 and is an Emirati politician and member of the ruling family of Sharjah, niece to Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. She graduated from California State University, Chico with a bachelor's degree in Computer Science and has an Executive MBA from the American University of Sharjah.

After returning to the UAE in 1981, she worked as a programmer for software company Datamation, then became Dubai branch manager for the General Information Authority responsible for automating the federal government. She later worked for seven years in the Dubai Ports Authority before entering ministerial roles. She served as Minister of State for Tolerance, Minister of State for International Cooperation, and Minister of Economic and Planning of the UAE, making history as the first woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE.

Since 2014, she has served as President of Zayed University. She has received numerous honors including being named the 36th most powerful woman in the world by Forbes and receiving an honorary Dame Commander of The Most Excellent Order of the British Empire in 2013.
achievements: First woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE
36th most powerful woman in the world by Forbes
Honorary Dame Commander of The Most Excellent Order of the British Empire (2013)
Lifetime Achievement Award at Asia HRD Congress 2012

notableFigures.name: Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh, Deputy Ruler of Sharjah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Deputy Ruler of Sharjah and President of University of Sharjah
notableFigures.significance: He serves as Deputy Ruler of Sharjah and holds numerous leadership positions across government and private sectors.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi is a member of the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah and currently serves as Deputy Ruler of Sharjah. He holds a Bachelor of Science degree in Business Administration from Arkansas State University and earned a Master of Science in Computer Information Systems from University of Detroit Mercy.

In December 2021, he was appointed President of the University of Sharjah by the ruler Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. He serves as Deputy Chairman of The Executive Council, chairman of the Sharjah National Oil Corporation, chairman of Sharjah Media Council, and chairman for Sharjah Petroleum Council. In the private sector, he has chaired Basma Group since 2005, Tilal Properties LLC since 2014, and Arada since 2017, among other leadership positions.

He founded the online news website Sharjah 24 in 2015 and launched OMNES Media in 2017. He is married to his cousin Sheikha Bodour bint Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi, daughter of the ruler of Sharjah. He has received several awards including The Most Outstanding Media Personality Award from the League of Arab States in 2016.
achievements: The Most Outstanding Media Personality Award for 2016 from the League of Arab States
Elected as the Humanitarian Envoy for The Big Heart Foundation (2017)
Jawaher Medal of Honour (2023)

notableFigures.name: Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh, 9th Ruler of Sharjah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Ruler of Sharjah and founding father of the UAE
notableFigures.significance: He was a founding father of the United Arab Emirates and the 9th ruler of Sharjah until his assassination in 1972.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi was born in Sharjah in 1931 and served as the 9th ruler of the Emirate of Sharjah from 1965 until his murder in 1972. He became ruler following the exile of his Arab nationalist cousin Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, who was removed with unanimous consent of the ruling family under British pressure. His status as ruler was confirmed by William Luce, the British Political Resident of the Persian Gulf, on 25 June 1965.

As a quiet and unassuming man, Khalid established Sharjah's first formal police force and played a key role in the negotiations that led to the foundation of the United Arab Emirates on 2 December 1971, making him a founding father of the nation. He was responsible for the demolition of Sharjah Fort in an attempt to erase the memory of his predecessor Saqr, though his brother Sultan convinced him to stop the demolition partway through.

On 24 January 1972, Saqr returned from exile in Egypt with mercenaries and seized power in a coup attempt, invading the Ruler's palace at approximately 2:30 PM. Despite being besieged by the Union Defence Force and Saqr's eventual surrender to UAE Minister of Defence Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Sheikh Khalid was killed in the action on 25 January 1972. He was succeeded by his brother Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.
achievements: Founding father of the United Arab Emirates
Established Sharjah's first formal police force

notableFigures.name: Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh, Former Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah
notableFigures.significance: He served as Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah for 45 years before being removed in 2003 due to political differences.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi was born in 1940 and is the eldest son of Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi, who ruled Ras Al Khaimah from 1948 until 2010. He was named Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah in 1958 at age 18 and served in this capacity for 45 years until his removal in June 2003. He completed his elementary schooling in Ras Al Khaimah, secondary education in Cairo, and higher education at Loughborough University in England.

As Crown Prince, he oversaw day-to-day government operations and served as principal contact with foreign governments, leading diplomatic missions to the United States during the Nixon, Reagan and Clinton Administrations. In 1999, President Clinton personally received him in the White House. He served as Chairman of the Board of several major institutions including the National Bank of Ras Al Khaimah, RAK National Oil Company, and RAK Cement Company, promoting economic development including airport and port expansion.

After 37 years in office, he was removed as Crown Prince on June 14, 2003, by his father and replaced by his younger half-brother Sheikh Saud bin Saqr al Qasimi. His removal caused street protests with thousands of demonstrators, requiring Abu Dhabi to send armored vehicles to restore order. Media reports attributed his removal to his opposition to the Iraq War and his vocal hostility to US intervention, which conflicted with UAE federal policy of strengthening cooperation with the US.
achievements: Served as Crown Prince for 45 years (1958-2003)
Led high-level diplomatic missions to the United States
Received by President Clinton in the White House (1999)
Promoted economic development including expansion of International Airport and Saqr Port

history: The Al Qasimi are a Huwala Arab dynasty of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province. During the 18th century, they established themselves as rulers in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, with Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi ruling from 1722-1747. The Qawasem embraced the Wahhabi movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they emerged as a dominant maritime power controlling both shores of the Persian Gulf, with territories in Ras Al Khaimah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their control of Gulf trade routes led to conflicts with Oman and Britain, resulting in British military campaigns in 1809 and 1819. The British labeled them 'pirates,' though current ruler Sultan bin Mohammed Al Qasimi contested this characterization in his book 'The Myth of Arab Piracy in the Gulf.' After the 1820 General Maritime Treaty, the family adapted to the new political reality, eventually becoming founding members of the UAE in 1971, with the family splitting to rule both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah as separate emirates.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates in the UAE, with extensive business interests, cultural institutions, and international diplomatic presence

history: The Al Qasimi (RAK) are a branch of the Qawasim tribal confederation that established themselves in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah in the early 18th century, growing to become a significant maritime force with holdings on both the Persian and Arabian coasts. According to research, Sheikh Rahma Al Qasimi became the first documented ruler of Ras Al Khaimah in 1708. The family built a powerful maritime empire that frequently came into conflict with British shipping, leading to British campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. After the 1820 General Maritime Treaty, they continued to rule under British protection. The family maintained control through various political changes, including periods where Ras Al Khaimah was incorporated into Sharjah (1900-1921). Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi ruled for 62 years (1948-2010), making Ras Al Khaimah the last emirate to join the UAE in 1972. Under current ruler Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, who took power in 2010, the family has diversified the emirate's economy through industrial development, tourism, and business ventures like RAK Ceramics. The family faced internal succession disputes when Sheikh Saud replaced his older half-brother Khalid as crown prince in 2003.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah emirate, involved in major business ventures including RAK Ceramics, real estate development, and economic diversification initiatives

history: The Al Qasimi (Qawasim) are a confederation of Sunni tribes that emerged as a dominant maritime power in the Persian Gulf during the 18th century. According to their genealogy, they are Huwala of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over Lengeh on the Hormozgan coast. During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Wahhabi movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they controlled territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah on the southern shore, and Qishm, Bandar Abbas and Lingeh on the northern shore. Their dominance led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire over trade routes and naval supremacy. Beginning in 1804, increased Qawasim naval attacks on trading ships led the British to label them 'pirates' and mount two major military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty and subsequent 1853 Perpetual Maritime Truce established British control over the region, transforming the 'Pirate Coast' into the Trucial States. Under Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi (1781-1866), who ruled from 1803-1866 with interruptions, the Qawasim maintained their confederation structure while adapting to British oversight. The tribe engaged in extensive conflicts with Abu Dhabi in the 1830s and maintained commercial ties reaching the Red Sea and Somali ports of Berbera and Zeila. Today, the Al Qasimi remain the only royal family to rule two emirates simultaneously - Sharjah under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi and Ras Al Khaimah under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, making them one of the longest-reigning dynasties in the Arabian Peninsula.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Sharjah emirate with extensive involvement in education, cultural preservation, media, and business through various government and private sector positions.

name: Al Rashid (House of Rashid)
originStory: The Al Rashid dynasty was established by Abdullah bin Rashid around 1836 when he consolidated power over the Shammar tribal confederation in the Ha'il oasis. The family rose from tribal leadership to create one of the most significant emirates in 19th-century Arabia.
description: The Al Rashid, or House of Rashid, was a powerful ruling dynasty that controlled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in northern Arabia from 1836 to 1921. Originating from the Shammar tribe, they were the primary rivals to the House of Saud before being conquered and integrated into the modern Saudi state.

history: The research provided focuses on historical figures with similar names but from different contexts - Harun al-Rashid (8th-9th century Abbasid Caliph), and modern members of the Al Maktoum family of Dubai including Rashid bin Mohammed Al Maktoum and Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. However, none of this research directly relates to the Al Rashid (House of Rashid) family that ruled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1836-1921. The existing data indicates this family originated from the Shammar tribal confederation and was established by Abdullah bin Rashid around 1836 in the Ha'il oasis in northern Arabia. The research provided does not contain information about the specific Al Rashid dynasty of Ha'il, their migration patterns, business ventures, or notable figures beyond what is already in the existing data. The Al Maktoum family research shows a different lineage entirely, being rulers of Dubai rather than the Shammar region.
modernStatus: Members of the Al Rashid family maintain traditional leadership roles within the Shammar tribe and hold various positions in modern Saudi Arabia's government and business sectors, though they no longer wield political power as a ruling dynasty.

notableFigures.title: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon
notableFigures.roleDescription: Lebanese politician and former Prime Minister
notableFigures.significance: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon in 1946 and held various ministerial positions.
notableFigures.biography:
Saadi Al Munla was born on November 4, 1890, in Tripoli, Lebanon, into a Sunni family. He received a law degree and worked as a lawyer by profession. He was a protégé of Rashid Karami and became a member of the Independence Party headed by Abdul Hamid Karami. He then became a member of the Lebanese parliament.

In 1945, he was appointed minister to the cabinet led by Prime Minister Sami Solh. Munla and two other cabinet members who were also members of the Independence Party, Ahmed Asad and Jamil Talhouk, resigned from office in May 1946. On May 22, 1946, Munla was appointed Prime Minister under President Bishara Khoury, replacing Sami Solh in the post. During his premiership, Munla also assumed the position of economy minister.

Munla remained in office until December 14, 1946, when Riad Solh succeeded him as prime minister. He died in December 1975, having served his country in various political capacities throughout his career.

Served as Minister of Economy
Member of Lebanese Parliament

history: The research reveals Al Saadi as a surname used across different regions and contexts, but provides no direct evidence connecting these individuals to the UAE Al Saadi family or their Bani Yas origins. The Wikipedia entries cover: Al-Saadi Gaddafi (Libyan, son of Muammar Gaddafi), Laith Al-Saadi (American blues musician of Iraqi-American heritage), and Saadi Al Munla (Lebanese politician from a Sunni family in Tripoli). The Arabic Wikipedia mentions Ali Al-Saadi, a Lebanese footballer. Most significantly, the research on the Dhawahir tribe indicates that one of their subsections, the Bani Saad, lived in Jimi in the Al Ain area and were part of the broader tribal confederation allied with the Al Nahyan. This suggests the Al Saadi family mentioned in the existing data may derive from this Bani Saad branch of the Dhawahir tribe rather than directly from Bani Yas, though the research does not explicitly confirm this connection to the UAE Al Saadi merchant family.
modernStatus: The family maintains business interests in trading, real estate, and commercial ventures across Abu Dhabi and the UAE, with several members holding positions in private sector companies and maintaining traditional merchant activities.

notableFigures.biography:
Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan was a prominent member of the Al Sabhan family who became connected to the Rashidi dynasty through marriage. She was previously married to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid before marrying Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, the reigning Rashidi emir. Through her marriage to Abdulaziz, she became the mother of Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, who would later become the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar between 1908 and 1920.

Her son Saud was born around 1898, and when his father was killed in the Battle of Rawdat Muhanna in 1906, the Al Sabhan family played a crucial role in protecting and supporting the young prince. Her family's influence in Rashidi politics became evident when her brother Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan took Saud to safety in Medina and later helped install him as emir in 1908.
achievements: Mother of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
Connected Al Sabhan family to Rashidi dynasty

notableFigures.name: Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan
notableFigures.title: Political conspirator and later minister
notableFigures.roleDescription: Conspirator in assassination of regent and later minister to Emir Saud
notableFigures.significance: Participated in the assassination of regent Zamil bin Salem and later became minister under Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz.
notableFigures.biography:
Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan was a member of the influential Al Sabhan family who played a significant role in the later period of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. In 1914, he conspired with his cousin Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid to assassinate the regent Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan. This assassination was motivated by disagreements over foreign policy, particularly Zamil's preference for maintaining relations with Ibn Saud, which conflicted with the emir's pro-Ottoman stance.

After the successful conspiracy and Zamil's death, Saud Al Sabhan became the minister to Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid. In this position, he served the young emir during the final years of the Rashidi state, as it faced increasing pressure from Ibn Saud's expanding Saudi kingdom. His role as minister made him one of the key Al Sabhan family members in the administration of the emirate during its declining years.
achievements: Participated in political conspiracy that changed emirate leadership
Served as minister to the last effective Rashidi emir

notableFigures.name: Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan
notableFigures.title: Queen consort of Saudi Arabia
notableFigures.roleDescription: Widow of Rashidi emir who became wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud
notableFigures.significance: Former widow of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid who became the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud.
notableFigures.biography:
Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan was originally married to Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, the tenth and last effective emir of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. After her husband was assassinated by his cousin Abdullah bin Talal in 1920, she became one of his widows during the final collapse of the Rashidi state.

Following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921, Norah remarried into the victorious Saudi royal family, becoming the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud). This marriage represented the integration of prominent Rashidi and Al Sabhan family members into the new Saudi state structure, and was likely part of Ibn Saud's policy of consolidating his rule by incorporating former rival elites through marriage alliances.
achievements: Became queen consort to the founder of modern Saudi Arabia
Represented Al Sabhan family integration into Saudi state

notableFigures.name: Zamil bin Sabhan
notableFigures.title: Early supporter of Rashidi dynasty
notableFigures.roleDescription: Close friend and supporter of the founding Rashidi princes
notableFigures.significance: Early ally who supported the Rashidi brothers in their rise to power in Ha'il.
notableFigures.biography:
Zamil bin Sabhan was an early and influential supporter of the Rashidi dynasty during its founding period in the 1830s. He was described as a cousin and close friend of the founding Rashidi princes Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid and his brother Prince Ubayd Al Rashid, who led a revolt against the ruler of Ha'il, Muhammad bin Ali of the Jaafar al-Shammari lineage.

Zamil provided crucial support to both brothers during their successful takeover of Ha'il and the establishment of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1836. His backing was particularly important when Abdullah demanded from his brother an ahd (covenant) according to which succession to the office of emir would remain in Abdullah's line. This early support established the Al Sabhan family as key allies and eventually regents of the Rashidi state.
achievements: Supported the founding of the Rashidi dynasty
Helped establish the succession covenant

notableFigures.name: Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan
notableFigures.title: Temporary Emir of Ha'il
notableFigures.roleDescription: Appointed by Ibn Saud as temporary ruler after Saudi conquest
notableFigures.significance: First post-conquest ruler of Ha'il appointed by Ibn Saud to ensure loyalty from local tribes.
notableFigures.biography:
Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan was appointed as the temporary emir of Ha'il by King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud) following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921. After Ibn Saud gave orders to destroy the historic Barzan Palace and ordered the leaders of both the Rashidi dynasty and Al Sabhan dynasty to relocate from Ha'il to Riyadh, he strategically appointed Ibrahim from the Al Sabhan family to govern the city.

This appointment was a calculated political move by Ibn Saud to ensure the loyalty of the Ha'il people and the Shammar tribe by placing a respected local figure from an established family in charge. Ibrahim's selection represented Ibn Saud's policy of incorporating former regional elites into his administration to maintain stability and legitimacy in newly conquered territories. His role as temporary emir helped facilitate the transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule in the northern Arabian region.
achievements: First Saudi-appointed ruler of Ha'il
Facilitated transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule

history: The Al Sabhan family emerged as a powerful political dynasty within the Anaza tribal confederation, playing crucial roles in Arabian Peninsula politics during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Historical records show they served as regents and advisors to the Rashidi emirate of Ha'il, with Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan acting as regent for the young Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid from 1908-1909. The family maintained significant political influence through strategic marriages and alliances - notably, Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan was married to Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, while other family members like Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan and Nura bint Sabhan later married King Abdulaziz Al Saud after the fall of the Rashidi dynasty. The family produced notable female leaders including Fatima bint Zamel Al Sabhan, who managed the treasury and influenced policy during the Rashidi period. In modern times, the Al Sabhan have transitioned into diplomatic and governmental roles, exemplified by Thamer bin Sabhan Al-Sabhan, who has served as Saudi Arabia's Minister of State for Gulf Affairs since 2016 and was the first Saudi ambassador to Iraq in over 25 years. The family's historical significance is recognized in academic research, with historian Dalal bint Mukhled Al-Harbi documenting their contributions in her study 'Fatimah Al-Sabhan: Her Life and Role in the Emirate of the Rasheed Family.'

history: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe whose existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Classical Arab genealogists describe Anizah's eponymous ancestor as a great uncle of these related tribes, indicating common patrilineal descent. Early historians recorded two main branches: one nomadic, living in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and another sedentary in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, Anizah settled in the Hijaz region, specifically Khaybar, at the end of the 10th century AD, with Ali bin al-Mugrab Al-Uyuni documenting their expulsion of the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from Khaybar's rulership. During the Ottoman era, Anizah became prominent masters of oasis towns in northwestern Arabia, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while maintaining seasonal migrations between winter settlements and summer pastures in southern Syria. The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs, followed by the Wuld Ali in Hawran, and the Ruwallah in early 19th century. One branch centered around Al-Jouf and Wadi Sirhan became so powerful it developed into the independent Ruwallah tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of Shammar. In Nejd, 19th-century historian Ibn La'bun recorded Anizah family settlements from the 14th century, with major Al-Qassim settlement in the 15th century. Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal gained fame leading Anizah clans to victory at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, though he died in the battle. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted Anizah's traditional nomadic lifestyle, with most members eventually settling and taking citizenship in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, Syria, Jordan, and other states. Notable ruling families claiming Anizah descent include Kuwait's Al-Sabah and Bahrain's Al-Khalifa, while Saudi Arabia's Al-Saud trace ancestry to the related Bani Hanifa tribe within the broader Rabiʿa confederation.
modernStatus: Ruling dynasty of Saudi Arabia with extensive political control and business interests, particularly in oil through Saudi Aramco. Key ministries and governorships held by family members.

subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Political confederation of multiple clans that migrated together from Najd. Most clans claim Anizah lineage with some exceptions. Formation was based on shared migration and political cooperation rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: The Bani Utbah is an Arab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, formed when a group of clans from Najd migrated to Eastern Arabia in the late 17th and early 18th centuries. Most Utub clans trace their lineage to the Anizah tribe, with some exceptions like Al Bin Ali tracing to Banu Sulaym. The confederation includes prominent ruling families like Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa, and successfully conquered Bahrain in 1783, establishing lasting rule there.

relations.context: Joined with Bakr ibn Wa'il under an alliance called al-Lahazim in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods

The Ruwallah became the arch-enemy of the large tribe of Shammar, who inhabited roughly the same area and dominated Nejd in the late 19th century. A 19th century oral poetic epic tells of rivalry between heroes from both tribes.
The 14th-century historian Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari notes that the Anazah were one of the tribes allied with the Al Fadl, along with Khath'am, Adwan, and Dhafeer.

Sheikha Sara bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1985 as the eldest daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah, and Sheikha Fatima bint Thani Al Maktoum. She is a graduate of the American University of Sharjah, demonstrating the family's commitment to higher education.

As a member of the ruling family of Fujairah, she represents the younger generation of Emirati royalty who have received international education while maintaining their cultural heritage and family traditions.

Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1987 as the second son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He holds a PhD in economics from London, reflecting the family's emphasis on advanced education and expertise.

He currently serves as the head of the media department of Fujairah, playing an important role in the emirate's communications and public relations efforts. His academic background in economics and his media responsibilities position him as a key figure in Fujairah's modern development and international outreach.

Sheikha Shamsa bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1988 as a daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. She is a graduate of Zayed University in Dubai, one of the UAE's leading higher education institutions.

She currently heads various charities in Fujairah, demonstrating the royal family's commitment to social responsibility and community welfare. Her charitable work contributes to the social development of the emirate and reflects the traditional values of Arabian leadership in caring for the community.

Sheikha Madiyah bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1989 as a daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah, and Sheikha Fatima bint Thani Al Maktoum. She has pursued a career as a fashion designer, representing the creative and entrepreneurial spirit of the younger generation of Emirati royalty.

Her work in fashion design showcases the modern aspirations of the Al Sharqi family while maintaining their cultural identity. She represents the diversification of interests and careers among contemporary Emirati royal family members.
achievements: Fashion designer

notableFigures.name: Maktoum bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Military officer and Sandhurst graduate

notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Maktoum bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1991 as the youngest son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He is a graduate of the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, the prestigious British military academy that has trained many members of royal families and military leaders worldwide.

His military education at Sandhurst reflects the family's tradition of service and leadership, as well as their strong ties with British institutions. As the youngest male member of the immediate ruling family, he represents the continuation of the Al Sharqi dynasty's commitment to professional excellence and international education.
achievements: Graduate of Royal Military Academy Sandhurst

notableFigures.name: Hamad bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Grandson of the ruler of Fujairah
notableFigures.significance: Eldest son of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on December 29, 2009, as the eldest son of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. He is the grandson of the current ruler of Fujairah and represents the future generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.

As the eldest son of the Crown Prince, he is positioned in the line of succession for the emirate. His lineage connects him to both the Al Sharqi family of Fujairah and the Al Maktoum family of Dubai, strengthening inter-emirate family ties within the UAE.

notableFigures.name: Aisha bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
notableFigures.title: Sheikha
notableFigures.roleDescription: Granddaughter of the ruler of Fujairah
notableFigures.significance: Daughter of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Aisha bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on November 1, 2011, as the eldest daughter of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. She is the granddaughter of the current ruler of Fujairah.

As a member of the younger generation of the Al Sharqi family, she represents the continuation of the dynasty and the strong family ties between the ruling families of Fujairah and Dubai through her mother's lineage.

notableFigures.name: Fatima bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi

notableFigures.roleDescription: Granddaughter of the ruler of Fujairah
notableFigures.significance: Daughter of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.

Sheikha Fatima bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on March 11, 2014, as a daughter of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. She is named after her great-grandmother, Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi.

As a member of the fourth generation of the modern Al Sharqi dynasty, she represents the future of the ruling family and maintains the strong inter-emirate connections through her maternal lineage to the Al Maktoum family of Dubai.

Sheikh Rashid bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on December 15, 2015, as a son of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. He is the grandson of the current ruler of Fujairah.

His name honors the family tradition, being named after his uncle Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi. As a member of the youngest generation of the Al Sharqi family, he represents the continuation of the dynasty into the future.

As the youngest member of the immediate royal family mentioned in the records, she represents the newest generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty and the continuation of the strong family ties between Fujairah and Dubai through her maternal lineage.

history: The Sharqiyin (Arabic: الشرقيون, singular Al Sharqi الشرقي) emerged as the dominant tribe along the East coast of the Trucial States by the early 19th century, when they were the second most numerous tribe in the area after the Bani Yas. They settled extensively from Kalba to Dibba along the East Coast, as well as in the strategic Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. By the turn of the 20th century, they numbered some 7,000 strong. The tribe is organized into three notable sections: the Hafaitat (from which the ruling family of Fujairah derives), the Yammahi, and the Hamudiyin. Originally dependents of Sharjah under Al Qasimi rule, the Sharqiyin controlled the vital trade route through Wadi Ham, guarded by their strategic Al Bithnah Fort. The Sharqiyin's quest for independence began in earnest in 1866 when their headman Abdullah bin Khamis Al Sharqi first withheld tribute to Sharjah following the death of Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi. Multiple rebellions followed in 1876 and 1879, led by Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi of the Hafaitat section. Despite British disapproval and various military actions by Sharjah, including the 1876 assault that killed 36 Sharqiyin and the temporary exile of Hamad bin Abdullah to Muscat in 1879, the tribe persisted in their independence efforts. The crucial turning point came in 1884 when Hamad bin Abdullah seized the strategic Bithnah Fort, effectively cutting off Sharjah's access to the entire east coast. When Saqr bin Khalid of Sharjah failed to act decisively, he lost effective control over the Shamaliyah. Hamad bin Abdullah finally declared Fujairah's independence from Sharjah in 1901, gaining recognition from neighboring Trucial States and Muscat, though not from the British until 1952. The delay in British recognition was primarily due to their need for oil exploration concessions, finally granted when Petroleum Concessions Limited required a recognized ruler to sign agreements. A 1968 census showed 90% of Fujairah's tribal population remained Sharqiyin, cementing their dominance in the region they had fought decades to control.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Fujairah emirate, with the current ruler Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi serving as a member of the UAE Supreme Council since 1975

name: Al Thani
originStory: The Al Thani originated from the Tamim tribe in the Najd region of central Arabia, migrating to Qatar around 1744 under the leadership of Thani bin Mohammed. They settled initially in Fuwayrit before establishing control over the broader peninsula.
description: The Al Thani family has ruled Qatar as the sovereign ruling dynasty since the mid-19th century, transforming from Bedouin tribal leaders to modern monarchs governing one of the world's wealthiest nations. Originally from the Tamim tribe of Najd, they established dominance over the Qatar Peninsula through strategic alliances and successful resistance to foreign powers.

notableFigures.roleDescription: Deputy Emir and unofficial heir presumptive to the Emir of Qatar
notableFigures.significance: Current Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive, appointed by his half-brother Emir Tamim in 2014.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani was born on 9 February 1988 in Doha, Qatar, as the son of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani and his third wife Sheikha Noora bint Khalid Al Thani. He graduated with a Bachelor of Science in Foreign Service from Georgetown University in 2010, demonstrating the family's commitment to international education and diplomacy.

From 20 December 2011 to 10 November 2014, Abdullah served as chief of the Amiri Diwan of the State of Qatar, gaining experience in government administration. On 11 November 2014, his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani appointed him to the post of Deputy Emir, unofficially making him heir to the throne of Qatar, though it is expected that Emir Tamim will eventually appoint one of his own sons as crown prince.

In his current roles, Abdullah serves as Chairman of the Board of Trustees of QatarEnergy since 4 November 2018, Chairman of Board of Regents of Qatar University, and Vice-chairman of the Supreme Council for Economic Affairs and Investment. He married Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah at Al Wajbah Palace on 22 March 2012.

notableFigures.name: Jawaher bint Hamad Al Thani
notableFigures.nameAr: جواهر بنت حمد بن سحيم آل ثاني
notableFigures.title: Sheikha, First Wife and Consort of the Emir
notableFigures.roleDescription: First wife and consort of Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani
notableFigures.significance: Current consort of the Emir of Qatar and prominent member of the royal family, representing Qatar in official state functions.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Jawaher bint Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani was born in Doha in 1984 to Sheikh Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani, a government minister and member of the Qatari royal family. She is a granddaughter of former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, making her a member of the royal family by birth. Her paternal grandfather, Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, served as Qatar's foreign minister.

She married her first cousin, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, on 8 January 2005 at Al Wajbah Palace, before he became Emir. They have four children together: Sheikha Al Mayassa (born 2006), Sheikh Hamad (born 2008), Sheikha Aisha (born 2010), and Sheikh Jassim (born 2012). When her husband succeeded as Emir in 2013, she became his consort.

As consort, Sheikha Jawaher has accompanied her husband on official state visits, including to Spain (2022), the Netherlands (2024), and attended major international events such as Queen Elizabeth II's funeral (2022) and King Charles III's coronation (2023). She serves as royal patron for Qatar University's female graduates and received a Master of Arts degree from Qatar University in 2021. She was awarded the Spanish Order of Isabella the Catholic in 2022.

Royal patron of Qatar University graduates

history: The Al Thani family originated from the Adnanite Banu Tamim tribe, tracing their lineage back to Mudar ibn Nizar. According to historical records, they migrated from the Najdi town of Ushaiger, northwest of Riyadh, and settled at the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before moving to Qatar around the 1720s-1740s. Their first settlement in Qatar was in the southern town of Sikak, from where they moved northwest to Zubarah, Al Ruwais, and then to Fuwayrit. They eventually settled in Doha in the 19th century under Mohammed bin Thani, with the family taking its name from his father Thani bin Mohammed. The family consists of three main branches: the Bani Ali, Bani Hamad, and Bani Khalid, with an estimated 20,000 members by the early 1990s. From tribal leaders, they evolved into Qatar's ruling dynasty, with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani becoming the first recognized ruler in 1851. The family has maintained unbroken rule since the mid-19th century, transitioning from traditional tribal governance to modern constitutional monarchy. Under successive emirs, Qatar transformed from a pearl-diving economy to a major oil and gas producer. The current emir, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, represents the latest generation of this ruling house, continuing the family's political dominance while modernizing the country's economy and infrastructure.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Qatar with extensive international business interests, including significant real estate holdings in London's Mayfair district, ownership of Paris Saint-Germain FC, and leadership of the Qatar Investment Authority

name: Al ash-Sheikh
originStory: The family originates from Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who established the Wahhabi reform movement in 18th century Najd. Their alliance with the Al Saud family in 1744 created the religious-political foundation of the Saudi state.
description: The Al ash-Sheikh family are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of the Wahhabi movement, and serve as Saudi Arabia's premier religious authority. They have maintained their role as the kingdom's chief Islamic scholars and jurists for over 275 years through their historic alliance with the Al Saud family.

notableFigures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Muhammad Abd al-Wahhab was the son of the founder Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and became leader of the Saudi religious establishment following his father's death in 1791. He endorsed further Al Saud expansion and wrote a number of tracts against Shia belief, continuing his father's religious mission. His leadership came during a critical period when the Saudi state was expanding but also facing growing Ottoman opposition. The Ottoman forces from Egypt eventually destroyed the Al Saud state in 1818, which was also a disaster for the Al ash-Sheikh family, though Abd Allah had died by this time.

notableFigures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif Al ash-Sheikh was a descendant of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab who led the ulema during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. When the Al Saud were defeated by the Al Rashid in 1891 and went into exile, he decided to side with the Al-Rashid and moved to Ha'il, indicating that preservation of the Wahhabi cause took precedence over the family compact with the Al Saud. However, when Abdul Aziz Al Saud returned from exile in 1902 and re-established the Saudi state around Riyadh, Abd Allah changed sides again and re-joined the Al Saud. Abdul Aziz accepted this change of heart and cemented the relationship with the Al ash-Sheikh through matrimonial links, including marrying Abd Allah's daughter. He was the father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, who became one of Abdul Aziz's wives and mother of King Faisal.
achievements: Led religious establishment during Al Saud restoration
Strengthened Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage ties
Father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, mother of King Faisal

notableFigures.name: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh
notableFigures.nameAr: عبد الله بن محمد آل الشيخ
notableFigures.title: Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura
notableFigures.roleDescription: Minister of Justice (1992-2009) and Chairman of Consultative Assembly since 2009
notableFigures.significance: Served as Saudi Minister of Justice for 17 years and implemented major judicial reforms including the creation of specialized courts.
notableFigures.biography: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh was born in Diriyah in 1948 and is a member of the noted Al ash-Sheikh family of religious scholars. He was educated by his father, Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh, the former Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia. He graduated with a bachelor's degree in Sharia from the Shariah College in Riyadh in 1975, then studied at Al-Azhar University in Cairo before returning to earn his PhD in Fiqh from Imam Mohammed bin Saudi University in 1987. After obtaining his doctorate, he became a lecturer and professor until his appointment as Minister of Justice in November 1992, replacing Mohammed bin Jubair. During his tenure, he drew up the judiciary law of 2007 on the King's instructions, creating specialized courts and a Supreme Court. He was replaced as justice minister in February 2009 in a cabinet reshuffle aimed at replacing conservative incumbents with more progressive candidates, and was appointed Chairman of the Majlis ash-Shura the same year.

Created judiciary law of 2007 establishing specialized courts
Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura since 2009

notableFigures.biography: Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh was born in 1884, the daughter of Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh, a principal religious teacher and adviser to the Emir of Nejd. She was one of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's eighth generation direct descendants. Tarfa married Emir Abdulaziz in 1902 immediately after he captured Riyadh, becoming his third wife. This marriage was a strategic move to strengthen the links between the Al Sauds and the Al Sheikhs. Her sisters also married into the Al Saud family - Munira married Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman, and Sara married his full-brother Saad bin Abdul Rahman. Tarfa and Abdulaziz's first child, Noura, was born in 1904, followed by their son Faisal in April 1906. Tragically, Tarfa died in October 1906 when Faisal was just six months old. Her son Faisal would later become King of Saudi Arabia in 1964.
achievements: Mother of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia
Strengthened Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage
Eighth generation descendant of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab

history: The Al ash-Sheikh family are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Tamimi al-Najdi (1703-1792), founder of the Wahhabi movement and member of the Banu Tamim tribe. Born in Nejd in 1703, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab moved to Diriyah in the 1740s where he formed a historic alliance with Muhammad ibn Saud in 1744, creating the religious-political foundation of what would become Saudi Arabia. This pact established a power-sharing arrangement where the Al Saud would maintain political authority while the Al ash-Sheikh would lead religious affairs and legitimize royal rule through their moral authority. The family suffered major setbacks when Ottoman forces executed many members in 1818, including Sulayman ibn Abd Allah, and exiled others to Egypt. However, they survived and continued their religious leadership role through the Second Saudi State and into the modern kingdom established in 1932. The family has maintained extensive intermarriage with the Al Saud, including King Faisal's mother Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh, cementing their position as Saudi Arabia's premier religious family, second in prestige only to the royal family. While their dominance has somewhat diminished in recent decades due to an influx of scholars from other families, they continue to hold key religious positions including the Grand Mufti and various ministerial roles.
modernStatus: Continue to dominate Saudi Arabia's religious establishment with members serving as Grand Mufti, ministers, judges, and senior religious scholars, while maintaining extensive ties to the Al Saud through intermarriage

migrationPath.description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab moved to Diriyah where he allied with Muhammad ibn Saud
