name: Al-Alawi
originStory: The Al-Alawi are descended from the Alawi Sharifs, tracing their lineage to Ali ibn Abi Talib through his son al-Hasan. They migrated from the Hijaz region to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including the Gulf coast.
description: The Al-Alawi are a noble Sharif family descended from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who settled in the Gulf region centuries ago and established themselves as prominent merchants and religious scholars. They are known for their involvement in traditional Gulf commerce and their transition to modern business enterprises.

notableFigures.name: Ahmad Al-Alawi
notableFigures.title: Merchant
notableFigures.roleDescription: Prominent pearl trader and religious scholar in 19th century Bahrain

notableFigures.name: Mohammed Al-Alawi

notableFigures.name: Khalaf al-Alawi

notableFigures.name: Ahmad al-Muhajir
notableFigures.title: Founder of Ba 'Alawi lineage
notableFigures.roleDescription: Ancestor who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut
notableFigures.significance: Foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H).
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad al-Muhajir is the foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H). He was a descendant of Muhammad through Husayn ibn Ali. When Ahmad al-Muhajir arrived in Hadhramaut, he maintained close family ties in Basra, where many of his relatives resided. His son Muhammad remained in Basra to oversee the family's property, together with his other sons, Ali and Hussein.

Al-Muhajir's children and grandchildren invested in Hadhramaut for many years derived from their holdings in Iraq. They continued to maintain contact with their ancestral homeland and cousins for a few generations, receiving news and accounts that helped preserve their shared heritage and historical memory. The Ba 'Alawi trace their lineage back to him, and classical scholars of Islam such as Ibn Hajar al-Haytami, Yusuf al-Nabhani and Murtada al-Zabidi have validated the genealogy of the Ba Alawi Sada.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi lineage in Hadhramaut
Established family presence outside Arabia

notableFigures.name: Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah
notableFigures.title: First descendant named 'Alawi
notableFigures.roleDescription: First of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi
notableFigures.significance: The first of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, from whom the Ba 'Alawi take their name.
notableFigures.biography:
Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah was the first of Ahmad al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, and from him the Ba 'Alawi family takes its name. He was the grandson of Ahmad al-Muhajir and the first Sayyid to be born in Hadhramaut. He was the only one of Imam al-Muhajir's descendants to produce a continued line in Hadhramaut, as the lines of his other grandsons in Hadhramaut—Basri (originally named Ismāʿīl) and Jadīd—ceased after a few generations.

Accordingly, Imam Al-Muhajir's descendants in Hadhramaut hold the name Bā 'Alawi or Bani 'Alawi ("descendants of Alawi") after him. The origin of the Ba 'Alawi name goes back to this ancestor, and the use of the name Ba 'Alawi came after they were influenced by the Hadhrami way of referring to their fathers, with the meaning of (Ba) among the Hadharem being "children of".
achievements: First descendant to bear the name 'Alawi
Established the continuing Ba 'Alawi line in Hadhramaut

notableFigures.name: Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi
notableFigures.title: Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
notableFigures.significance: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa and established the principle of non-violence for Alawi Sayyids.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi, known as "Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam" (The Foremost Jurist), was the founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa, one of the Sufi orders spread in the Islamic world. During his time, Sayyid families in Hadhramaut were seen as a threat by other tribes, and due to regional instability, it was normal during his study that Muhammad bin Ali put a sword on his lap for protection.

Muhammad grew tired of the tension and bloodshed in the ranks of the believers, thus symbolically broke his sword and announced that his Tariqa and the way of Alawi Sayyids are non-violence and renounced any tariqa that uses violence. This established the peaceful nature of the Ba 'Alawi approach to spreading Islam, which would later influence the dissemination of Islam in Southeast Asia through traders and clerics of Hadhramaut who transited in India since the 15th century.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
Established the principle of non-violence for the Ba 'Alawi
Created peaceful approach to Islamic teaching

notableFigures.name: Ali bin Alawi
notableFigures.title: Khali' Qasam
notableFigures.roleDescription: Descendant who established Tarim as Ba 'Alawi headquarters
notableFigures.significance: Moved to Tarim in 521 AH and made it the headquarters of the Bani Alawi.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali bin Alawi, known as "Khali' Qasam," was a descendant of Ahmad Al-Muhajir who played a crucial role in establishing the Ba 'Alawi presence in Tarim. In 521 AH, he moved to the city of Tarim and made it his home for himself and his children, following the family's earlier movements through various villages in the valley.

After Ahmad Al-Muhajir's descendants had moved through the valley's villages, settling in the village of Sumal for a time before moving to the village of Bayt Jubair, Ali bin Alawi's decision to settle in Tarim proved to be historically significant. Since his settlement there, Tarim has become the headquarters of the Bani Alawi to this day, serving as their spiritual and administrative center.
achievements: Established Tarim as the Ba 'Alawi headquarters
Consolidated Ba 'Alawi presence in a major city

notableFigures.name: Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās
notableFigures.nameAr: الحبيب عمر بن عبد الرحمن العطاس
notableFigures.title: Al-Habib
notableFigures.roleDescription: First Ba 'Alawi to be called Habib
notableFigures.significance: The first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib, establishing this honorific title.
notableFigures.biography:
Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās (d. 1072 AH / 1661 CE) holds the distinction of being the first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib. This represented an evolution in the titles used by Ba 'Alawi scholars and religious leaders over time.

Previously, descendants of Imam Ahmad al-Muhajir who became scholars in Islamic studies were called Imam, then Sheikh, but al-ʿAṭṭās was the first to be honored with the title Habib. This title became an important part of Ba 'Alawi tradition and continues to be used for respected religious leaders within the community.
achievements: First Ba 'Alawi to receive the title 'Habib'
Established new honorific tradition

notableFigures.name: Muhammad 'Alawi al-Maliki
notableFigures.title: Traditional Sunni Islamic Scholar
notableFigures.roleDescription: Foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholar from Saudi Arabia
notableFigures.significance: Considered to be the Mujaddid of 20th-21st century and one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Alawi ibn al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Aziz, also known as Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki, was born in 1944 in Mecca and became one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars of contemporary times. The Maliki family is one of the most respected families in Mecca and has produced great scholars who taught in the Haram of Mecca for centuries. Five of his ancestors had been the Maliki Imams of the Haram of Mecca.

With his father's instruction, he studied and mastered the various traditional Islamic sciences of Aqidah, Tafsir, Hadith, Seerah, Fiqh, Usul, Mustalah, Nahw, and received full Ijazah from scholars of Mecca and Medina to teach these sciences to others. In the early 1990s, the Government of Saudi Arabia began supporting practitioners of Sufism in the Hijaz region as a way to counter Wahhabism and bolster religious support of the state; al-Maliki became the self-imposed leader of Hijazi Sufism under state sponsorship, with several thousand supporters. He died in 2004 and was buried in Mecca, with Crown Prince 'Abd Allah stating that al-Maliki "was faithful both to his religion and country."
achievements: Mastered traditional Islamic sciences
Became leader of Hijazi Sufism
Authored numerous religious works
Received state recognition in Saudi Arabia

history: The Al-Alawi are descendants of the Ba'Alawi Sadat, a distinguished group of Hadhrami Sayyid families who trace their lineage through Ahmad al-Muhajir to Ali ibn Abi Talib via al-Hasan. The Ba'Alawi originated in Hadhramaut in southern Yemen, where they established themselves as religious scholars and merchants following the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence and Ash'ari creed. Beginning around 1700 CE, they migrated extensively across the Islamic world for da'wah (missionary work), reaching Southeast Asia, East Africa, the Gulf region, and beyond. In the Gulf, they became prominent as religious authorities and scholars. The family includes notable figures like Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki (1944-2004), one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars of contemporary times from Saudi Arabia who served as a teacher at the Sacred Mosque in Mecca and was considered the Mujaddid of the 20th-21st century. The Al-Alawi maintained their scholarly tradition across generations, with some members holding positions as Naqib al-Ashraf in Mecca and Medina in recent centuries. Their genealogies are preserved in detailed records, including the famous 15-volume genealogy ledger by Abd al-Rahman al-Mashhur, and their lineage has been validated by classical Islamic scholars throughout history.
modernStatus: Established religious scholarly families with members serving as Islamic authorities, teachers, and religious leaders across the Gulf region and beyond

name: Al-Amru
originStory: The Al-Amru descended from the Bani Yas confederation, originally settling in the Liwa Oasis region of Abu Dhabi. They were among the Bedouin families who transitioned from traditional desert life to coastal trading and pearling.
description: The Al-Amru is a traditional UAE family originating from the Bani Yas tribal confederation. They established themselves through pearling and trading activities before diversifying into modern business sectors following the oil boom and UAE formation.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Abdullah Al-Amru
notableFigures.title: Family Elder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Led family transition from Liwa to coastal regions, established pearling operations

notableFigures.name: Amrus ibn Yusuf al-Muwallad al-Laridi
notableFigures.title: General and Governor
notableFigures.roleDescription: Muwallad general of the Emirate of Córdoba and governor of Zaragoza
notableFigures.significance: Founded the influential Banu Amrus family and controlled strategic territories in the upper Ebro valley for nearly a decade.
notableFigures.biography:
Amrus ibn Yusuf was a Muwallad (probably of Visigothic origin) general who served the Emirate of Córdoba. A native of Huesca, he and his kinsman Shabrit were mawālī servants of Aysun ibn Sulayman al-Arabi. In 791/792, Amrus turned against his master Matruh al-Arabi during a rebellion, attacking and killing him with swords alongside Sarhabil ibn Saltan al-Zawagi. This act of betrayal brought him favor with the Córdoba court, where he was rewarded with the position of wali of Talavera.

In 802, Amrus was sent from Toledo as a general to suppress another Zaragoza rebellion, successfully taking both Zaragoza and Huesca while expelling Bahlul ibn Marzuq. He established the settlement that would become Tudela, installing his son Yusuf ibn Amrus there. When Zaragoza rebelled again under Fortun ibn Musa in December 802, Amrus was appointed governor of Zaragoza in 803/804, also installing his kinsman Sabrit in Huesca. He successfully quelled a rebellion in Tudela in 807 and occupied the county of Sobrarbe after the death of Oriol of Aragon, territory that remained under his control until Aznar Galíndez I regained it in 814. According to chronicler Al-Udri, Amrus held Zaragoza for nearly ten years, dying in either 808/809 or 813/814.
achievements: Established the settlement that became Tudela
Controlled Zaragoza for nearly a decade
Founded the influential Banu Amrus dynasty
Successfully suppressed multiple rebellions in the upper Ebro valley

history: The research provided does not contain any direct information about the Al-Amru family beyond what is already known in the existing data. The historical figures mentioned in the research (Imru' al-Qais, Ali al-Sajjad, Al-Obaidi tribe, Dirar ibn al-Azwar, and Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib) are all notable historical personalities from early Islamic and pre-Islamic periods, but none appear to have direct documented connections to the modern UAE Al-Amru family. Without specific research linking these historical figures to the contemporary Al-Amru family of the UAE, or providing additional verified information about the family's actual origins, business activities, or historical development, it would be inappropriate to speculate or create connections that are not substantiated by the available evidence.

notableFigures.name: Mohammed bin Khamis
notableFigures.title: Chief of Al Bidda
notableFigures.roleDescription: Tribal chief and successor to Buhur bin Jubrun
notableFigures.significance: He was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe and successor of Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda, though he was embroiled in controversy for murdering a native of Bahrain.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed bin Khamis was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe who succeeded Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda in 1828. His leadership was marked by controversy when he murdered a native of Bahrain, which prompted the Al Khalifa sheikh to imprison him. This incident had significant consequences for the Al-Buainain tribe and the region.

In response to Mohammed bin Khamis's imprisonment, the Al-Buainain tribe revolted against the Al Khalifa. This rebellion provoked a harsh response from the Al Khalifa, who destroyed the tribe's fort and evicted them to Fuwayrit and Ar Ru'ays. This incident allowed the Al Khalifa to gain additional jurisdiction over the town of Al Bidda. With essentially no effective ruler after these events, Al Bidda and the nearby settlement of Doha became a sanctuary for pirates and outlaws, marking a turbulent period in the region's history.

notableFigures.name: Buhur bin Jubrun
notableFigures.title: Ruler and Initial Founder of Doha
notableFigures.roleDescription: Chief of the Al-Buainain tribe and founder
notableFigures.significance: He was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, and chief of the Al-Buainain tribe when the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda in January 1823.
notableFigures.biography:
Buhur bin Jubrun was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, serving as the chief of the Al-Buainain tribe in the early 19th century. In January 1823, the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda specifically to meet with Buhur bin Jubrun. During this significant diplomatic encounter, MacLeod noted that Al Bidda was the only substantial trading port in the peninsula at that time, highlighting the importance of the settlement under Buhur bin Jubrun's leadership.

As the founder of Doha, Buhur bin Jubrun played a crucial role in establishing what would become Qatar's capital city. His leadership of the Al-Buainain tribe and control over the important trading port of Al Bidda made him a key figure in the region's early commercial and political development. His position as both tribal chief and town ruler represented the traditional Arabian leadership structure of the time, where tribal authority and territorial control were closely interlinked.
achievements: Founded the city of Doha
Established Al Bidda as the peninsula's main trading port
Conducted diplomatic relations with British officials

history: The Bani Tamim are one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. According to traditional genealogy, they descend from Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan, claimed as a direct lineage to Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham). The tribe is mainly divided into four branches: Amr, Banu Hanzala, Banu Sa'd, and Al-Rabbab. They occupied numerous wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century before playing an important role in the beginning of Islam. They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. After the Islamic conquests, the tribe migrated extensively across the Arab world, reaching Tunisia, Iraq, Morocco, Khuzestan and Khorasan regions of Iran. In Iraq, they settled in Kufa near the Euphrates river banks and in Basra, often sharing land with the Banu Asad. The tribe held significant political power through various dynasties including the Aghlabids who ruled Ifriqiya (modern Tunisia, Algeria, Libya) from 800-909 CE, and continue to rule Qatar through the Al Thani dynasty since 1847. Modern Bani Tamim are present throughout the Gulf states, with notable concentrations in Saudi Arabia where Hotat Bani Tamim serves as a namesake town 135km south of Riyadh. The tribe is also mentioned among the original Arab Huwala tribes who migrated to Iran's southern coast between the 16th-17th centuries, particularly to areas around Bandar Lengeh, before many returned to the Arabian Peninsula in the 19th-20th centuries. The Al Khater family represents a prominent Tamimi lineage that built major cities including Al-Jubail in Saudi Arabia and Al-Wakrah, the former capital of Qatar, maintaining close political and family ties with the ruling families of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain.
modernStatus: Prominent political and business family across Gulf states, with members serving as ambassadors, government officials, and maintaining close ties with ruling families

notableFigures.biography: Fadhil al-Milani was a respected Shia religious scholar who gained prominence in the religious community. He is mentioned in the sources as the father of a daughter who married Moustafa Al-Qazwini, an Iraqi-American Shia imam who founded several Islamic centers in California. The marriage connection suggests al-Milani held a position of respect within the Shia scholarly community, as such matrimonial alliances were typically formed between families of similar religious and social standing. His daughter's marriage to al-Qazwini, who went on to establish important Islamic institutions in the United States, indicates the al-Milani family's continued influence in Islamic scholarly circles.

history: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe whose progenitor, according to early Arab genealogists, was Julhumah ibn Udad, known as 'Tayy' or 'Tayyi'. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan, establishing them as a Qahtanite tribe. Originally based in Yemen, the Tayy migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy and later Jabal Shammar. The tribe split into two main branches: Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadilah. The Al al-Ghawth produced notable sub-branches including Thu'al, Aswadan (Nabhan), and Hani, while Al Jadilah descended from a woman named Jadilah whose sons Hur and Jundub became progenitors of major sub-branches. During the pre-Islamic era, the Tayy established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires. A Tayyid chief named Iyas ibn Qabisah al-Ta'i became governor of al-Hira from 602 to 611, effectively supplanting the Lakhmids as rulers. The late 6th century saw the devastating Fasad War between the Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila branches, lasting 25 years and resulting in the migration of several Jadila clans to Syria where they converted to Christianity and allied with the Ghassanids, while the Al al-Ghawth remained in Jabal Tayy. With the emergence of Islam, individual Tayy members initially converted, including warriors who fought at Badr and other early battles. In 630, Muhammad dispatched Ali to destroy the Tayy's principal idol, al-Fils, in Jabal Aja, leading to mass conversions. The prominent chieftain Adi ibn Hatim, son of the legendary generous poet Hatim al-Ta'i, converted to Islam along with Zayd al-Khayr and their followers in 629-630. During the Ridda Wars following Muhammad's death, the Tayy's allegiance was divided, with Adi ibn Hatim playing a crucial role in keeping most of the tribe loyal to Islam and preventing conflicts with Muslim forces. Under the Rashidun and Umayyad caliphates, the Tayy participated in major conquests and were split during the First Fitna, with Arabian Tayy supporting Ali while Syrian Tayy supported Mu'awiya. The Abbasid period saw Tayy leadership in the revolution that toppled the Umayyads, with Qahtaba ibn Shabib al-Ta'i leading Abbasid forces in Khurasan. As Abbasid authority eroded by the mid-9th century, the Tayy became dominant in the southern Syrian Desert and established the Jarrahid emirate in Palestine under Fatimid rule, controlling key trade routes between Egypt, Syria, Arabia and Iraq. The Jarrahids alternated between various powers including Fatimids, Byzantines, Seljuks and Crusaders until the late 12th century. By this time, the Tayy had become the predominant tribe across the Syrian steppe, Upper Mesopotamia, Najd and northern Hejaz, with various branches including the Al Fadl, Al Mira, and others controlling vast territories. The most prominent modern descendant of the Tayy is the Shammar confederation, one of the largest Arab tribes, which emerged as a distinct tribal name by the 14th century and ruled much of central and northern Arabia through the Al Rashid emirate of Jabal Shammar until 1921.
modernStatus: Dispersed descendants across Syria, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, and Gulf states; some branches involved in traditional tribal leadership and business in Jordan and Saudi Arabia, though no longer holding significant territorial or political power

subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.

subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

notableFigures.significance: Prominent Qatari diplomat who served as Ambassador to the United States from 2014-2016 and previously as Ambassador to France for a decade.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed Jaham Al Kuwari was born on 20 May 1958, one of 13 children in his family. He received his bachelor's degree in political science from University of Portland in 1980 and a master's degree in international relations from the University of Madrid in 1990. Al Kuwari began his diplomatic career at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 1981 as a Third Secretary, first posted to Washington DC from 1981 to 1986, then Madrid from 1986 to 1990.

He held various senior positions including Deputy Head of Cabinet (1993-1995), Director of European and American Affairs (1995-1997 and 2001-2002), and head of delegation for Qatar's political and strategic dialogue with France (2001-2002). Prior to his appointment as Ambassador to the United States, Al Kuwari served for a decade as Qatar's Ambassador to France and was accredited to Switzerland and the Holy See. In 2007, he became non-resident Ambassador to Monaco and Portugal.

In December 2013, Al Kuwari was appointed Ambassador of Qatar to Washington, DC, officially presenting his credentials to President Barack Obama on 10 March 2014 and serving until December 2016. Throughout his career, he has been recognized with numerous international honors including multiple decorations from France's Legion of Honour, the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Gregory the Great from Vatican City, and the Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic from Spain.

notableFigures.name: Ghanim Al Kuwari
notableFigures.title: Minister of Information and Culture
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Qatari government minister
notableFigures.significance: Served as Qatar's minister of information and culture and was involved in a significant political incident in 1985 related to succession disputes within the ruling family.
notableFigures.biography:
Ghanim Al Kuwari served as Qatar's minister of information and culture during the 1980s. He became involved in a major political crisis in 1985 when Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, brother of Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, died suddenly of a heart attack in August 1985. Suhaim had been plotting a coup against his brother after learning that Khalifa had named his own son as heir, and maintained his own weapons cache and supporters in northern Qatar.

Following Suhaim's sudden death, his sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention during his medical emergency. This accusation led to a serious escalation when Suhaim's sons attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari in retaliation. As a result of this assassination attempt, they were subsequently imprisoned. The incident highlights the complex political dynamics and family rivalries within Qatar's ruling circles during this period.

history: The Al-Kuwari family descends from the Tamim tribe of central Arabia and migrated to Qatar in the early 19th century, initially settling in the interior regions before establishing themselves in Doha as the city developed. The family has produced notable intellectuals and cultural figures who have made significant contributions to Qatari society. Ali Khalifa Al-Kuwari, born in 1941, became a prominent democracy activist, author, and professor who occupied several positions in Qatar's oil sector, including vice chairman of Qatar Liquefied Gas Company and the National Company for Petroleum Products. He served as chairman of the Qatar–United Kingdom Joint Committee for Economic Cooperation and worked as an economics professor at Qatar University from 1975 to 1982. In 1959, he was involved in founding Taliaa Club Qatar, a cultural club that held political discussions until it was shut down by authorities in 1961. Al-Kuwari gained international attention for editing 'The People Want Reform in Qatar Too' in 2012, a book that criticized government policies and was subsequently banned in Qatar. The family's cultural influence extends through figures like Wedad Al Kuwari, born in 1967, who became known as 'The Engineer of Gulf Drama' for her extensive work in children's theatre and Qatari television, writing scripts for numerous plays, television series, and radio programs while authoring several books.
modernStatus: Established as intellectual and cultural contributors with notable figures in academia, political reform advocacy, oil industry leadership, and entertainment media production

history: The Al-Ma'awdah family was among the Arab family clans that moved from Qatar to Bahrain following the Bani Utbah's conquest of the islands in 1783 and the subsequent fall of Nasr Al-Madhkur of Bushehr. According to the research, after the Bani Utbah gained control of Bahrain, different Arab family clans and tribes from Qatar settled on the islands, including the House of Khalifa, Al-Ma'awdah, Al-Buainain, Al-Fadhil, Al-Kuwari, Al-Mannai, Al-Noaimi, Al-Rumaihi, Al-Sulaiti, Al-Sadah, Al-Thawadi and other families and tribes. The family's migration from Qatar to Bahrain was part of the broader movement of Utbah-affiliated clans who had established themselves in the Zubarah area of Qatar before expanding their influence to Bahrain. The research indicates this settlement occurred sometime after 1783, when the Al Khalifa and allied forces defeated Al-Madhkur at the Battle of Zubarah in 1782, leading to Bahrain coming under Bani Utbah control.
modernStatus: The family maintains influence through government positions in Abu Dhabi's administration, involvement in trading companies, and real estate investments, while preserving strong tribal connections and participating in UAE's economic diversification.

history: The Bani Tamim are one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. According to traditional genealogy, they descend from Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan, claimed as a direct lineage to Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham). The tribe is mainly divided into four branches: Amr, Banu Hanzala, Banu Sa'd, and Al-Rabbab. They occupied numerous wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century before playing an important role in the beginning of Islam. They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. After the Islamic conquests, the tribe migrated extensively across the Arab world, reaching Tunisia, Iraq, Morocco, Khuzestan and Khorasan regions of Iran. In Iraq, they settled in Kufa near the Euphrates river banks and in Basra, often sharing land with the Banu Asad. The tribe held significant political power through various dynasties including the Aghlabids who ruled Ifriqiya (modern Tunisia, Algeria, Libya) from 800-909 CE, and continue to rule Qatar through the Al Thani dynasty since 1847. Modern Bani Tamim are present throughout the Gulf states, with notable concentrations in Saudi Arabia where Hotat Bani Tamim serves as a namesake town 135km south of Riyadh. The tribe is also mentioned among the original Arab Huwala tribes who migrated to Iran's southern coast between the 16th-17th centuries, particularly to areas around Bandar Lengeh, before many returned to the Arabian Peninsula in the 19th-20th centuries. The Al Khater family represents a prominent Tamimi lineage that built major cities including Al-Jubail in Saudi Arabia and Al-Wakrah, the former capital of Qatar, maintaining close political and family ties with the ruling families of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain.
modernStatus: Major commercial family with extensive business interests through Mannai Corporation in Qatar and historical trading operations in Bahrain, maintaining significant economic influence across the Gulf region

history: The Al-Musawi are a Sayyid family tracing their lineage to Musa al-Kadhim, the seventh Shia Imam, making them descendants of the Prophet Muhammad through Imam Ali and Fatimah. As indicated by their name, all Musawi families are descendants from Musa Al-Kadhim, son of Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq, with their bloodline tracing through each of the first six Twelve Imams. Members of the Al-Musawi family are mainly Shi'a Muslims found globally in Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan, Lebanon, India, Pakistan, and GCC countries. According to family tradition, the original roots come from the head tribe of Banu Hashim. In the GCC, the family has produced prominent educators, religious scholars, medical professionals, and athletes. Notable figures include Sulaimaan Rabi' Al-Musawi (1812-1895), a renowned Kuwaiti teacher who taught Mubarak the Great, the Emir of Kuwait, and opened a school in the Grand Mosque of Kuwait. His grandson Muhammad Hassan Al-Musawi (1912-1995) became one of Kuwait's most prominent educators, serving as principal of the Jafari School and earning the title 'The Educator of Generations' for his educational reforms that are still used across Kuwaiti public schools. The family has also produced religious scholars like Muhammad Baqir al-Muhri (1948-2015), one of Kuwait's most prominent Islamic scholars and founder of multiple religious organizations, and medical professionals like Sayyid Ala Al-Musawi, a Kuwaiti oral and maxillofacial surgeon whose grandfather was a former member of the Kuwaiti government with a street named after him.
modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in education, religious scholarship, medicine, and sports across the GCC, with notable representation in Kuwait's educational system and Islamic institutions.

notableFigures.biography:
Lieutenant General Theyab bin Saqer Al Noaimi currently serves as the Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force (BDF). He holds this senior military position in the BDF, which was established in 1968 and consists of the Royal Bahraini Air Force, Royal Bahraini Army, Royal Bahraini Navy and the Royal Guard.

The BDF numbers about 18,000 personnel and is under the direct command and leadership of a commander-in-chief who holds the rank of field marshal. Al Noaimi's role as Chief of Staff makes him one of the most senior military officers in the Bahraini armed forces, working under the overall command structure that includes Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad Al Khalifa as Deputy Supreme Commander and Field Marshal Khalifa bin Ahmed Al Khalifa as Commander-in-Chief.

notableFigures.title: Distinguished figure
notableFigures.roleDescription: Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles
notableFigures.significance: He received Sudan's second-highest honour, the Order of the Two Niles (Class I: Grand Cordon) in 2009.
notableFigures.biography:
Essa Abdulla Al Basha Al Noaimi is a distinguished figure who was honored by Sudan with the Order of the Two Niles in 2009. He received the First Class Grand Cordon of this prestigious order, which is Sudan's second-highest honour after the Order of the Republic.

The Order of the Two Niles was established on 16 November 1961 during Ibrahim Abboud's military government and is given to both Sudanese and foreign nationals, civilians and military personnel, who have provided great services to the state. Al Noaimi's receipt of this high honor in 2009 indicates his significant contributions, though the specific nature of his services is not detailed in the available records.
achievements: Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (Grand Cordon, 2009)

notableFigures.name: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi
notableFigures.title: Former Foreign Minister of the United Arab Emirates
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former UAE Foreign Minister and member of Ajman's ruling family
notableFigures.significance: He served as the UAE's foreign minister and is a member of the ruling Al Nuaimi family of Ajman emirate.
notableFigures.biography:
Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman, Al Nuaimi, and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967.

Nuaimi started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs. Until 1975, he worked in various capacities at the foreign ministry. In 1975, he was named director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976 became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him in the post. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.
achievements: Former UAE Foreign Minister
Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (2018)
Recipient of Japan's Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun (2024)

notableFigures.name: Ali Rashid Al Noaimi
notableFigures.title: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Al Islah member turned UAE University administrator
notableFigures.significance: He is a former member of the banned Islamist group Al Islah who became Vice Chancellor of UAE University.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali Rashid Al Noaimi serves as the Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University and is a former member of Al Islah, the UAE-based Islamist political group affiliated with the Muslim Brotherhood. Al Noaimi's experience with Al Islah provides insight into the organization's operations and ideology.

As a former Al Islah member who later took a senior academic position, Al Noaimi has been critical of the organization's loyalties. He stated that Al Islah members 'get their orders from outside' and 'they are not loyal to their country,' providing testimony about the group's alleged foreign connections and influence. His transition from involvement with the banned political organization to becoming a senior university administrator reflects the complex political and social dynamics within the UAE's educated elite.
achievements: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University

history: The Na'im is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe prominent in the United Arab Emirates and other Gulf countries, divided into three main sections: Al Bu Kharaiban, Khawatir, and Al Bu Shamis. The tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where their expansion came at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. At their peak in 1818, they numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi according to British records, making them arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe followed the Ghafiri tradition and supported the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. Although initially linked to Wahhabi influence in the Buraimi area, the Na'im allied with the Al Bu Falah ruler Khalifah bin Shakhbut in 1848 and Sultan Azzan bin Qais in 1869 to evict the Wahhabis from Buraimi, subsequently occupying many forts around the oasis. However, following the death of Zayed the Great, they came under Saudi influence again, leading to their participation in the Buraimi dispute of the 1950s. The tribe experienced significant decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s. The Al Bu Shamis section became virtually independent and closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, while the Al Bu Kharaiban section provided the rulers of Ajman. During the Buraimi dispute in 1952-1955, some 200 Na'im, Al Bu Shamis, and Bani Kaab fought against British forces, with Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis assisting Saudi forces in occupying Hamasa village. Following the conflict, many Na'im were resettled in Saudi Arabia, while others settled in the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi.
modernStatus: Ruling family of Ajman emirate since 1971 UAE formation. Active in real estate development, hospitality sector through Ajman Tourism Development Department, and various commercial enterprises. Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi serves as ruler and UAE Supreme Council member.

subTribes.name: Khawatir
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
subTribes.description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

subTribes.name: Al Bu Shamis
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
subTribes.description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

name: Al-Qarooni/Karuni family
originStory: The Al-Qarooni family descended from the Qarn tribe and migrated to the Gulf coast during the 17th-18th centuries. They settled along the Trucial Coast where they became involved in the pearling and maritime trade economy.
description: The Al-Qarooni family is a Gulf Arab family with roots in the Qarn tribe, historically involved in pearling and maritime trade along the Trucial Coast. They established themselves as merchants and pearling operators during the 19th century boom period.

history: The Al-Qarooni/Karuni family represents one of the Baharna subgroups with recurring migrations between Bahrain and Khuzestan. According to the research, they had to re-migrate after the Persian harassment campaign against the Arabs of Mohammerah intensified, particularly following the 1951 events when Persian authorities targeted ethnic Arabs in the region. The family speaks a variant of Mesopotamian Arabic, distinguishing them within the broader Baharna community. Some genetic samples suggest an affinity to the J2 Haplogroup, specifically the J-BY44557 branch, which aligns with the broader Baharna genetic profile that shows closer affinities to populations of Iraq, the Levant, Iran and the Caucasus rather than central Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their migration pattern reflects the broader Baharna experience of displacement due to political persecution and sectarian tensions, with families maintaining cultural and linguistic ties across the Gulf region while adapting to changing political circumstances in both Bahrain and Iranian Khuzestan.
modernStatus: Displaced community that returned to Gulf region following persecution in Iran, maintaining Baharna cultural and linguistic identity

name: Al-Qassab
originStory: The Al-Qassab emerged from the Bani Yas confederation as specialized livestock traders and butchers. Their name reflects their ancestral profession in the meat trade across the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Al-Qassab are a merchant family of Bani Yas origin who settled in Abu Dhabi in the late 18th century. Originally involved in livestock trading, they have evolved into successful modern businesspeople while maintaining their traditional Emirati values.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Kamal al-Qassab
notableFigures.title: Director of Schools
notableFigures.roleDescription: Educational administrator in Haifa Islamic Society
notableFigures.significance: Served as director of schools for the Haifa Islamic Society until his dismissal in 1932.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Kamal al-Qassab served as the director of schools for the Haifa Islamic Society in Palestine during the early 1930s. His dismissal from this position in late 1932 caused significant controversy and led to political ramifications within the Palestinian Arab community. According to the Filastin newspaper, his sacking prompted Rashid al-Haj Ibrahim, a prominent Palestinian Arab leader and banker, to resign from his position in the Haifa Islamic Society in protest. The dismissal was reportedly part of broader political maneuvering, as al-Husayni was allegedly attempting to undermine the rising al-Istiqlal (Independence) party by controlling key institutions like the Islamic Society.
achievements: Served as director of schools for Haifa Islamic Society

history: The Al-Qassab family shows significant connections to the broader Persian Gulf Iranian community, with specific mention in historical records of Muharram commemorations in Bahrain. According to research on Bahraini religious practices, 'Al Qassab' is listed among the oldest and most distinguished matams (Shia congregation halls) in the Fareej el-Makharqa quarter of central Manama, constructed at the turn of the 19th century alongside other prominent establishments like Matam al-Ajam al-Kabeer and Matam Bin Rajab. This suggests the family had established religious and community leadership roles in Bahrain's Shia community by the early 1800s. The name Al-Qassab, meaning 'the butcher' in Arabic, aligns with their traditional occupation in livestock trading and meat commerce. Genetic studies of Bahraini populations indicate that some families with similar names show affinity to the J2 haplogroup, which is associated with Iranian, Caucasian, and Mesopotamian ancestry rather than central Arabian Peninsula origins. A notable modern family member, Zaid Al-Qassab, serves as Global CEO of M+C Saatchi Group as of May 2024, having previously held the position of CMO at Channel 4, representing the family's evolution into international business leadership while maintaining their Gulf connections.
modernStatus: The family has achieved prominence in international business, with Zaid Al-Qassab serving as Global CEO of M+C Saatchi Group since May 2024.

notableFigures.biography:
Saad Mohammed Al-Rumaihi was a sports journalist working at the Al Raya newspaper in Qatar during the late 1970s. He was one of the founding members of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, established on June 6, 1979, alongside prominent members of the Al Thani royal family including Sheikh Mohammed bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Faisal Al Thani, and Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani.

According to a documentary produced by Al Kass sports channel about the history of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim mentioned that the idea to establish the club was initially suggested by Saad Al-Rumaihi during his time as a sports journalist. His suggestion led to the creation of what would become one of Qatar's most successful football clubs, which has won multiple league championships and domestic cups. The club was founded with the intent of creating sports facilities for the youth in the Al Gharrafa region of Al Rayyan municipality.
achievements: Co-founded Al-Gharafa Sports Club in 1979
Worked as sports journalist at Al Raya newspaper

notableFigures.biography:
Hamda bint Hassan Al-Sulaiti is an appointed member of Qatar's Consultative Assembly and currently serves as Deputy Speaker of the council. She holds one of the key leadership positions in Qatar's legislative body, which consists of 45 members who are directly appointed by the Emir.

As Deputy Speaker, she plays an important role in the functioning of Qatar's legislative process, working alongside the Chairman Hassan bin Abdullah Al-Ghanim. Her position demonstrates the Al-Sulaiti family's continued prominence in Qatar's political establishment and their involvement in the country's governance structures.
achievements: Appointed as Deputy Speaker of Qatar's Consultative Assembly

history: The Al-Sulaiti family appears to have been among the Arab families that migrated from Qatar to Bahrain following the Bani Utbah conquest in 1783. According to historical records, after the Bani Utbah gained control of Bahrain and the fall of Nasr Al-Madhkur of Bushehr, different Arab family clans and tribes from Qatar moved to Bahrain to settle. The Al-Sulaiti were specifically mentioned among these families, alongside the House of Khalifa, Al-Ma'awdah, Al-Buainain, Al-Fadhil, Al-Kuwari, Al-Mannai, Al-Noaimi, Al-Rumaihi, Al-Sadah, and Al-Thawadi. This suggests they were part of the broader Arab migration from the Qatar peninsula to Bahrain in the late 18th century. A notable modern figure from the family is Ghanem Al Sulaiti, born in 1956 in Manama, Bahrain, who became a prominent Qatari playwright and actor. His background reflects the transnational nature of Gulf families - his father was Qatari, his mother Emirati, and his grandmother Saudi. He lived in Bahrain during part of his childhood before his family moved to Qatar, illustrating the continued connections between Gulf territories.
modernStatus: The family maintains presence across the Gulf with notable figures in arts and culture, particularly in Qatar and Bahrain.

history: The Al-Thawawda (also Al-Thawadi) is an Arab tribe present across the Persian Gulf states of Bahrain, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia. According to research, they are a sub-clan of the Bani Khalid tribe, not Bani Tamim as previously recorded. The family has a significant presence in Bahrain, particularly based in Al Hidd and Muharraq, where many members were historically involved in the lucrative pearl hunting industry. The Al Thawawda were among the clans that migrated to Bahrain from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion of Bahrain. In Qatar, the family has produced notable figures including Hassan Bin Abdulla Al Thawadi, CEO of the Qatar 2022 World Cup Bid. The Bahraini branch has been particularly prominent in media and finance, with Bassam Mohammed Al-Thawadi becoming a pioneering filmmaker who produced Bahrain's first feature film 'The Barrier' in 1990, and Isa Bin Sultan Al Thawadi founding the Housing Bank. The family also includes political activists like Ahmad Al-Thawadi, known for his leftist political activism.
modernStatus: Active in diplomatic, government, media, and business sectors across the Gulf, with notable presence in Qatar's World Cup organization and Bahrain's banking and film industries.

name: Albuflasa
originStory: The Albuflasa family originated from the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the Liwa oasis region of southeastern Arabia. They migrated to coastal Abu Dhabi in the late 18th century as part of the broader tribal settlement movement.
description: The Albuflasa family is a traditional UAE family with roots in the Bani Yas tribal confederation, historically based in the Liwa region before settling in Abu Dhabi. They have maintained close ties to the ruling Al Nahyan family while adapting to modern business opportunities in the contemporary UAE.

notableFigures.name: Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa
notableFigures.title: Poet, Writer, Former BDF Officer
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Bahrain Defence Force officer, poet, writer, independent political candidate, and court employee under Crown Prince Salman. Became first political prisoner of 2011 Bahraini uprising after delivering unifying speech at Pearl Roundabout.

notableFigures.name: Rashed Albuflasa
notableFigures.title: Family Member
notableFigures.roleDescription: Brother of Mohamed Albuflasa and family spokesperson
notableFigures.significance: Served as family spokesperson during Mohamed Albuflasa's imprisonment and explained the motivation behind his brother's political activism.
notableFigures.biography:
Rashed Albuflasa is the brother of Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa and served as a family spokesperson during the 2011 Bahraini uprising. When his brother Mohamed was arrested and became the first political prisoner of the uprising, Rashed explained to the media the significance and motivation behind Mohamed's speech at Pearl Roundabout.

According to Rashed, Mohamed's speech was intended to calm the flames of anger caused by efforts to create disagreement between Shiites and Sunnis. He wanted to show there was no difference between them and that both were Bahraini. Rashed clarified that while the government was surprised and regarded the speech as an attack on them, Mohamed was not against the royal family and government, but rather sought to promote national unity and bridge sectarian divides during a time of significant political tension in Bahrain.

history: The research reveals a different branch of the Albuflasa family than previously documented, establishing them as a Bedouin clan with presence in Bahrain rather than the UAE. Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa, born in 1977, represents this Bahraini branch of the family, serving as a poet, writer, and former Bahrain Defence Force officer who later worked at the court of Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. The family gained significant political prominence during the 2011 Bahraini uprising when Mohamed Albuflasa became a key figure in bridging sectarian divides. As a religiously conservative Sunni from the Albuflasa Bedouin clan, his speech at Pearl Roundabout on February 15, 2011, addressing mostly Shia protesters and calling for political reform and an end to sectarian discrimination, was historically significant. His subsequent arrest made him the first political prisoner of the Bahraini uprising, and his case drew international attention from human rights organizations including Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch. His role in promoting unity between Sunni and Shia populations during the Arab Spring demonstrations established the family's modern political legacy in Bahrain.
modernStatus: Politically active family in Bahrain with connections to the royal court and involvement in democratic reform movements

migrationPath.description: Migration from inland oasis to coastal settlement following Bani Yas tribal movement under Al Nahyan leadership

history: The research reveals that Almuhannada is an offshoot family of the Bani Hajer, which is itself part of the larger Qahtan tribal confederation. According to the Wikipedia source, the Qahtan tribe is composed of three main tribes (Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida) and has members distributed across Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The Qahtan tribe historically settled in the Najd region known as Al-Quwai'iyah and expanded its influence in southern Najd, becoming one of the powerful tribes of Najd before eventually returning to its original home in the south. In Bahrain specifically, while Qahtanis represent a small number compared to other Gulf countries, some members have taken prominent public roles. The Almuhannada family, as a Hajri offshoot, has maintained a public presence, with former Member of Parliament Hamad Almuhannadi serving as a notable political figure representing the family in Bahrain's legislative body.
modernStatus: The family maintains a public role in Bahrain with representation in political positions, including former parliamentary membership.

name: Bani Hafiz Family
originStory: The Bani Hafiz originated as a sub-tribe of the Bani Yas confederation in the Liwa Oasis during the 18th century. They were renowned for their mastery of traditional irrigation techniques and date palm cultivation.
description: The Bani Hafiz family is a prominent sub-tribe of the Bani Yas confederation, originally from the Liwa Oasis, known for their agricultural expertise and successful transition to modern business enterprises. They have played significant roles in the development of the UAE's construction and real estate sectors.

notableFigures.name: Ahmad bin Hafiz Al-Mazrouei
notableFigures.title: Agricultural Development Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Pioneer in modernizing traditional farming methods and establishing agricultural cooperatives

notableFigures.name: Hafiz Jamal ud din
notableFigures.title: Religious Leader and Village Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Spiritual figure who founded the village of Bani Hafiz
notableFigures.significance: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz after receiving land as a gift from the Raja of Salmia in the late 1700s.
notableFigures.biography: Hafiz Jamal ud din was a religious and spiritual person who lived in the late 1700s. The land of Bani Hafiz was given as a gift to him by the Raja of Salmia during this period. He used to meditate in the jungle in Bani Hafiz and was known for his spiritual practices. Hafiz Jamal had two sons: Hafiz Muhammad Ghous and Hafiz Muhammad Jee. His elder son Hafiz Muhammad Ghous shifted to Ban Khawra, a small village in Lamnian on the other side of the river Jehlum, while Hafiz Muhammad Jee stayed at Bani Hafiz and his family propagated there. Urs (religious festival) of Hafiz Jamal and his sons are carried out around the year in Bani Hafiz, with people from distant areas coming to celebrate these festivities, indicating his lasting spiritual influence in the region.
achievements: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz
Established a spiritual legacy commemorated through annual Urs festivals

notableFigures.name: Hafiz Muhammad Jee
notableFigures.title: Religious Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of Hafiz Jamal ud din who continued the family's spiritual tradition
notableFigures.significance: Stayed in Bani Hafiz and propagated the family lineage, becoming the patriarch of the Bani Hafiz family.
notableFigures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Jee was one of the two sons of Hafiz Jamal ud din, the founder of Bani Hafiz village. While his elder brother Hafiz Muhammad Ghous moved to Ban Khawra across the river Jehlum, Hafiz Muhammad Jee chose to remain in Bani Hafiz. The family of Hafiz Muhammad Jee propagated in Bani Hafiz, making him the key patriarch of the family's continuation in the village. He had three sons: Hafiz Abdul Qudus (known as Molvi Bajee), Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, and Hafiz Abdul Latif, all of whom stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. His lineage continued the spiritual traditions of the family, with annual Urs festivals being held to commemorate him alongside his father.
achievements: Established the main family lineage in Bani Hafiz
Father of three prominent sons who continued the family tradition

notableFigures.name: Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar
notableFigures.title: Politician and Acting President
notableFigures.roleDescription: 17th Acting President of Azad Kashmir and prominent political leader
notableFigures.significance: Served as Acting President of Azad Kashmir and was a founding member of Pakistan People's Party in the region.
notableFigures.biography: Born on 12 September 1944 in Bani Hafiz, a village of Hattian Bala, Azad Kashmir, Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar belonged to the spiritual family of Bani Hafiz. His father was Hafiz Abdul Latif, making him the great-grandson of Hafiz Jamal ud din. Born an orphan, he was raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, a local Sufi saint. He matriculated from Chikar Tehsil and later went to Karachi to study law, earning a master's degree in political science and Urdu from Karachi University. Before joining politics, he worked as a primary school teacher but left to join Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto's Pakistan People's Party in the early 1970s, becoming one of the founding members in Azad Kashmir. Zaffar served in various high positions including Acting President of Azad Kashmir from 20-29 July 1991, Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly from 1990-1991, and Senior Minister from 1996-2001. He was known for his loyalty to the Bhutto family and accompanied Benazir Bhutto to rallies and election campaigns. He died of a heart attack on 2 September 2006 in Rawalpindi.
achievements: 17th Acting President of Azad Kashmir
Founding member of Pakistan People's Party in Azad Kashmir
Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly
Senior Minister for five years
President of PPP Azad Jammu and Kashmir

notableFigures.name: Hafiz Abdul Latif
notableFigures.title: Religious Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of Hafiz Muhammad Jee and father of Ishaq Zaffar
notableFigures.significance: One of three sons who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz, father of prominent politician Ishaq Zaffar.
notableFigures.biography: Hafiz Abdul Latif was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz village. He was the father of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who would later become a prominent politician and Acting President of Azad Kashmir. The family belonged to the spiritual lineage of Bani Hafiz, continuing the religious traditions established by his grandfather Hafiz Jamal ud din. His son Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, suggesting that Hafiz Abdul Latif died when his son was very young, leading to the child being raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus.
achievements: Continued the spiritual family tradition in Bani Hafiz
Father of a prominent political leader

notableFigures.name: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus
notableFigures.title: Sufi Saint
notableFigures.roleDescription: Local Sufi saint and spiritual leader
notableFigures.significance: Local Sufi saint whose tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz village.
notableFigures.biography: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. He became a prominent local Sufi saint whose spiritual influence extended throughout the region. When his nephew Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, Hafiz Muhammad Yunus raised him, demonstrating his role as a family patriarch and caretaker. His daughter was Anwar Kasur, who became the principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala, and the institute was later named after her. His tomb became a shrine in the village, indicating his lasting spiritual significance. His son was Sahibzada Irfan Danish, who served as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991.
achievements: Became a revered local Sufi saint
His tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz
Raised his orphaned nephew who became a prominent politician

notableFigures.name: Sahibzada Irfan Danish
notableFigures.title: Member of Constituent Assembly
notableFigures.roleDescription: Political representative in Azad Kashmir government
notableFigures.significance: Served as member of constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991, continuing the family's political involvement.
notableFigures.biography: Sahibzada Irfan Danish was the son of Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, the prominent Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. He followed in the family tradition of public service by entering politics and serving as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991. He was the cousin of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who also served as a member of the legislative assembly during the same period (1990-1991), showing that both cousins were active in Azad Kashmir politics simultaneously. His political service represented the continued involvement of the Bani Hafiz family in the governance and political affairs of the region.
achievements: Member of constituent assembly of Azad Kashmir (1990-1991)

notableFigures.name: Anwar Kasur
notableFigures.title: Educational Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Principal of degree college and educator
notableFigures.significance: Served as principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala, with the institute named after her in recognition of her contributions to education.
notableFigures.biography: Anwar Kasur was the daughter of Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, the revered Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. She dedicated her career to education and served as the principal of degree college for girls in Hattian Bala. Her contributions to education were so significant that the institute was later named after her, demonstrating the high regard in which she was held by the community. She was also noted as a poet of merit, showing her intellectual and literary capabilities beyond her educational administration role. She was the sister of politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, indicating that the family produced notable figures in both education and politics.
achievements: Principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala
Educational institute named in her honor
Recognized as a poet of merit

notableFigures.name: Hafiz Muhammad Shafi
notableFigures.title: Spiritual Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Famous saint of Muzaffarabad region
notableFigures.significance: Famous saint of Muzaffarabad region who provides spiritual guidance to many people who visit him.
notableFigures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Shafi is the son of Hafiz Muhammad Qudous (also known as Molvi Bajee), making him part of the spiritual lineage of the Bani Hafiz family. He has become a famous saint of the Muzaffarabad region, continuing the family's long tradition of spiritual leadership that dates back to his ancestor Hafiz Jamal ud din. Many people come to him seeking spiritual guidance, indicating his significant influence and reputation as a spiritual leader in the region. He is described as being among the spiritual personalities of the vicinity and is a cousin of the late politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar. His prominence as a saint demonstrates the continued spiritual influence of the Bani Hafiz family in the region.
achievements: Became a famous saint of Muzaffarabad region
Provides spiritual guidance to many visitors
Continues the family's spiritual tradition

history: Based on the research provided, there is no verified information about the Bani Hafiz family mentioned in the existing data being related to the UAE, Bani Yas confederation, or the Liwa Oasis. The research reveals completely different Bani Hafiz entities: one is a village in Pakistani Kashmir named after Hafiz Jamal ud din in the late 1700s, whose family became renowned spiritual leaders in the region. The second major reference is to Hafez Shirazi (1325-1390), the famous Persian poet and Sufi mystic from Shiraz, Iran, who has no apparent connection to Gulf families or tribal confederations. The research also mentions Ahmed Kuftaro (1915-2004), a Kurdish-origin Grand Mufti of Syria, and extensive historical information about Qatar, but none of these sources provide any evidence supporting the claimed UAE/Bani Yas origin story. Without verified documentation connecting the Bani Hafiz family to the Liwa Oasis, agricultural expertise, or construction/real estate ventures in the UAE, the existing origin story and description cannot be substantiated by the available research.
modernStatus: Active in construction, real estate development, and agricultural enterprises across the UAE. Family members hold positions in government ministries and participate in public-private partnerships for national development projects.

history: The Cohen family research provided focuses entirely on individuals outside the GCC region and does not contain any information about a Cohen family specifically based in Bahrain or the broader Gulf region. The research covers Sacha Baron Cohen, a British-Jewish entertainer from London whose family has Ashkenazi Jewish, German Jewish, and Belarusian Jewish origins; Eli Cohen, an Egyptian-born Israeli spy who operated in Syria; Adam Cohen, a Canadian musician and son of Leonard Cohen; and Yossi Cohen, an Israeli intelligence officer. None of these individuals or their families have documented connections to Bahrain or the GCC states. The research also includes information about the Gulf War but contains no genealogical data about Cohen families in the region. Without specific research about Cohen families in Bahrain or the Gulf, no verified migration patterns, business activities, or settlement history in the GCC can be documented.
modernStatus: Family members now primarily reside in Israel, UK, and US, with some maintaining international business interests. Limited current presence in Bahrain, though historical business connections may persist through partnerships.

history: The Cohen-Maghari (Meguri) family represents a Jewish priestly lineage with documented origins tracing back to the ancient Temple of Jerusalem. According to historical records, the Cohen surname indicates patrilineal descent from the hereditary priestly caste known as the Kohanim, with Aaron, brother of Moses, regarded as the patriarch of this lineage. The specific Cohen-Maghari (Meguri) branch has been identified as originating from Yemen, where they trace their lineage to Jehoiarib, one of the ancient priestly divisions. This secondary appellation 'Maghari' (also spelled Meguri) was added to distinguish this particular Kohenic line from other Cohen families, similar to other regional variants like Cohen-Scali of Morocco. As members of the Kohanim, the family would have been bound by specific Jewish religious laws and traditions, including restrictions on marriage and contact with the dead, reflecting their maintained priestly status throughout the diaspora.
modernStatus: Most family members emigrated from the Gulf region by the 1960s, with descendants now primarily residing in India, Europe, and the Americas. Limited current business presence in the GCC.

originStory: The Cohen-Scali family originated from Sephardic Jewish communities, likely from Mediterranean regions, and established themselves as merchants in the Arabian Gulf during the late 1800s. They were drawn to the region by expanding trade opportunities in the pearl diving and maritime commerce sectors.

They were part of the small but influential Jewish community that contributed to the commercial development of Gulf port cities before political changes led to their migration from the region.

notableFigures.roleDescription: Olympic ice dancing competitor turned skating coach and choreographer
notableFigures.significance: Former Italian Olympic ice dancer who became a prominent figure skating coach and choreographer, working with elite skaters including Alysa Liu.
notableFigures.biography:
Massimo Scali is a former Italian Olympic ice dancing competitor who transitioned into coaching and choreography after his competitive career. He is now based in Oakland, California, where he works as a skating coach and choreographer. Scali began working with American figure skater Alysa Liu on her skating skills and choreography at the end of 2019, helping to develop her artistic presentation alongside her technical abilities.

Scali's coaching expertise extends beyond choreography to include skating skills development. He worked with Liu during her early competitive years, and after her retirement and subsequent comeback, she returned to work with both Scali and coach Phillip DiGuglielmo. Due to being based in the Bay Area while Liu trains in Southern California, Scali primarily works with her remotely, demonstrating his adaptability in modern coaching methods. His background as an Olympic competitor gives him unique insight into the pressures and requirements of elite-level figure skating.
achievements: Competed in Olympic ice dancing for Italy
Coached and choreographed for elite figure skaters
Worked with multiple-time U.S. national champion Alysa Liu

name: Dulafid dynasty
originStory: The Dulafid dynasty originated from the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the mid-18th century, establishing their stronghold in the Liwa Oasis region. They rose to prominence through successful management of date palm cultivation and control of interior trade routes.
description: The Dulafid dynasty was a prominent tribal family that emerged from the Bani Yas confederation in the 18th century, establishing significant influence in the Liwa Oasis region and playing crucial roles in the political and commercial development of what became the United Arab Emirates. Today, their descendants continue to hold important positions in UAE government and business sectors.

history: The Dulafid dynasty was an Arab dynasty of the 9th century CE that emerged from the Banu Ijl tribe, originally part of the vanguard during the Muslim conquest of Iraq. The family's documented history begins with brothers Idris and Isa, sons of Ma'qel, who were traders residing in Kufa during the Umayyad period (724-743). They were imprisoned by Umayyad authorities, possibly for supporting the Abbasid cause. Idris eventually accumulated wealth and moved to the Zagros region, purchasing land near Hamadan and settling there, while his son Isa moved to Isfahan with his sons. The family eventually settled in Karaj, where they acquired extensive cultivated lands, palaces and fortresses by the 9th century. Abu Dulaf al-Ijli became the first Dulafid governor of Jibal under Caliph Harun al-Rashid (786-809), establishing hereditary control over territories known as 'al-Igharayn' with rights to coin money and collect tribute. The dynasty ruled as increasingly independent governors during the weakening of Abbasid central authority, but was ultimately defeated and eliminated by the Abbasids in 896-898, with the last ruler Abu Layla al-Harith killed in battle near Isfahan in 898.
modernStatus: Descendants serve in UAE government ministries, hold positions on corporate boards of major UAE companies, and maintain traditional tribal leadership roles. Family members are involved in real estate development, trading companies, and cultural preservation initiatives.

name: Ghurid Dynasty (Shansabani)
originStory: The Ghurids emerged from the remote mountainous region of Ghor in central Afghanistan, where they served as local chieftains before rising to establish one of the most significant medieval empires bridging Central Asia and India. They claimed descent from ancient Persian noble lineages and gradually expanded their influence from their mountain strongholds.
description: The Ghurid Dynasty (Shansabani) was a medieval Muslim dynasty of Eastern Iranian origin that ruled from the mountains of Ghor in Afghanistan from 1011-1215 CE. They are most famous for their conquest of northern India under Muhammad of Ghor, which established the foundation for centuries of Muslim rule in the Indian subcontinent.
