notableFigures.significance: CEO of Cars Taxi, Dubai's largest taxi fleet provider, and son of Mashreq Bank founder Abdulla bin Ahmad Al Ghurair.
notableFigures.biography:
Badr Al Ghurair is the Chief Executive Officer of Cars Taxi, which operates as the largest taxi fleet provider in Dubai and is part of Al Ghurair's mobility sector. In his role, Badr oversees the ongoing operations of all divisions within the company. Under his leadership, Cars Taxi became notable as the first taxi company in the UAE to incorporate hybrid taxis into their fleet, demonstrating innovation in sustainable transportation.

Badr is the son of Abdulla bin Ahmad Al Ghurair, the founder and chairman of Mashreq Bank, positioning him as part of the third generation of the Al Ghurair business family. His role in the mobility sector represents the family's diversification beyond their traditional areas of banking, construction, and manufacturing into modern transportation services.
achievements: CEO of Dubai's largest taxi fleet provider
First to incorporate hybrid taxis in UAE taxi fleet

history: The Al Ghurair family traces its origins to the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the Najd region of Central Arabia. According to historical records, the family's business history began in the 1930s when Ahmad Al Ghurair and his son Saif were pearl divers in Dubai. A stormy boat trip to India in the 1930s to sell dates left Saif blind in one eye. In 1960, as their business grew successful, Ahmad Al Ghurair founded the Al Ghurair Group to oversee the family's expanding interests. The group built the UAE's first cement factory, flour mill, and sugar refinery, and founded the Bank of Oman (later Mashreq Bank) in 1967. In the 1990s, the family business split into two complementary companies: Saif Al Ghurair retained the Al Ghurair Group focusing on manufacturing, real estate, and investments, while his brother Abdulla created Al Ghurair Investment concentrating on finance, food, and construction. Today, the family controls diverse business empires spanning banking, manufacturing, real estate, and retail, with notable figures including Abdulla Al Ghurair (founder of Mashreq Bank, net worth $4.8 billion), and his son Abdul Aziz Al Ghurair who serves as CEO of Mashreq and chairman of Al Ghurair Investment, and was former Speaker of the UAE Federal National Council (2007-2011).
modernStatus: Controls major UAE business conglomerates Al Ghurair Group and Al Ghurair Investment, with significant ownership in Mashreq Bank and diverse holdings in manufacturing, real estate, and retail sectors

history: The Al Futtaim family established itself as one of the UAE's most prominent merchant dynasties, with the business empire founded in the 1930s and expanding rapidly through the 1940s and 1950s. The family built an integrated commercial, industrial, and services organization that became a major conglomerate. In 2000, a significant family split occurred when Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum mediated a settlement between cousins Abdulla Al Futtaim and Majid Al Futtaim, dividing the assets, liabilities and operations of the larger Al Futtaim Group. Abdulla Al Futtaim retained control of the automotive and retail business (Al-Futtaim Group), while Majid Al Futtaim took the property development business, founding Majid Al Futtaim Group in 1992. Both branches have become major regional conglomerates, with Al-Futtaim Group ranking third and Majid Al Futtaim Group ranking sixth in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in 2022.
modernStatus: Split into two major conglomerates: Al-Futtaim Group (led by Abdulla Al Futtaim family) and Majid Al Futtaim Group (founded by late Majid Al Futtaim, now led by his son Tariq). Both rank among the top Arab family businesses regionally.

name: Al Habtoor
originStory: The Al Habtoor family descended from the Bani Yas tribal confederation and migrated from the interior oases to Dubai in the early 19th century. They initially established themselves through pearl diving and coastal trading before evolving into major merchants and industrialists.
description: The Al Habtoor family is a prominent Emirati merchant family that rose from traditional pearl diving and trading origins to become one of the UAE's most successful business dynasties. Founded by Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor, their conglomerate spans construction, hospitality, automotive, and real estate sectors across the Middle East.

notableFigures.title: Chairman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder and Chairman of Al Habtoor Group, leading businessman and philanthropist

notableFigures.name: Mohammed Al Habtoor
notableFigures.title: Donor and Philanthropist
notableFigures.roleDescription: Major donor to educational institutions
notableFigures.significance: Made significant donation to renovate the Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in 2007.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed Al Habtoor is a notable philanthropist who has made significant contributions to educational institutions. In 2007, he made a substantial donation that enabled the renovation of the Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in Jacksonville, Illinois. The museum, housed in Whipple Hall on the college campus, is dedicated to former congressman and alumnus Paul Findley and contains artifacts related to Findley's political career, his interest in Abraham Lincoln, and his involvement in human rights and Middle East issues.

Al Habtoor's donation was instrumental in preserving and enhancing this important historical collection, which includes Lincoln's 1837 law office sofa, Findley's congressional desk, WWI and campaign memorabilia, and gifts from seven U.S. presidents and international leaders. The museum serves as an educational resource that is open for tours and visits by appointment, continuing to benefit students and visitors through Al Habtoor's generosity.
achievements: Donated funds for renovation of Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in 2007

notableFigures.name: Rashid Al-Habtoor
notableFigures.title: UAE Businessman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Business executive and hotel industry investor
notableFigures.significance: Submitted a major bid for the acquisition of Leela Hotels & Resorts in India in 2019.
notableFigures.biography:
Rashid Al-Habtoor is a prominent UAE businessman who has been involved in significant international business transactions, particularly in the hospitality sector. In January 2019, he emerged as one of the major suitors for the acquisition of Leela Hotels & Resorts, a prestigious Indian luxury hotel chain. Al-Habtoor, working jointly with business partner Abhishek Verma, submitted a substantial bid of US$600 million for the hotel group.

The bid was part of a competitive acquisition process that included other major suitors such as ITC Group and ultimately Brookfield Asset Management, which eventually won the acquisition. Despite the significant value of Al-Habtoor's offer, questions were raised about the conduct of JM Financial, the asset reconstruction company managing the sale transaction, regarding how various bids were entertained and evaluated. This high-profile attempt to acquire the Indian hotel chain demonstrated Al-Habtoor's active involvement in international hospitality investments and his capacity for major business transactions.
achievements: Submitted $600 million bid for Leela Hotels & Resorts acquisition in 2019

history: The Al Habtoor family, while previously understood to have Bani Yas tribal origins, has evolved into one of the UAE's most prominent business dynasties under the leadership of Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor. The family's business empire, the Al Habtoor Group, spans multiple sectors including construction, hospitality, automotive, and real estate development. Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor has built an extensive international business portfolio, with notable ventures including Al Habtoor Motors (established 1983) serving as distributor for luxury automotive brands like Bentley, McLaren, and Mitsubishi, and major hospitality investments including the acquisition of The President Abraham Lincoln Hotel in Springfield, Illinois for $9.3 million in 2014. The family has also developed significant infrastructure projects in Dubai, including the Al Habtoor Polo Club (opened 2016) featuring world-class equestrian facilities and the massive mixed-use Habtoor City development comprising three luxury hotels and residential towers. Beyond business ventures, the family has established a significant philanthropic presence through the Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor Foundation, launched in January 2013, which has conducted extensive humanitarian campaigns supporting Syrian and Palestinian refugees in Lebanon, distributing thousands of aid packages and supporting various Islamic educational and religious initiatives across the region.
modernStatus: Leading UAE business conglomerate with international operations in construction, hospitality, automotive, and real estate, led by multi-billionaire chairman Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor

They have been influential traders in Dubai since the 19th century, successfully transitioning from traditional pearl trading to modern commercial enterprises.

notableFigures.roleDescription: Prominent businesswoman and senior executive in family business
notableFigures.significance: Leading Emirati businesswoman who ranked among the world's most powerful Arab women and serves in senior roles across the family business empire.
notableFigures.biography:
Dr. Amina Al Rustamani is a prominent Emirati businesswoman who currently serves as Director and Board Member of AW Rostamani Group and Chief Executive Officer of AWR Properties. She earned her bachelor's, master's and doctoral degrees in electrical engineering from The George Washington University in 1993, 1996, and 2001, respectively. She started her career in Dubai in 2001 as an electrical engineer with TECOM Investments (now TECOM Group), where she was involved in the development of Dubai Design District and Dubai Wholesale City.

In February 2018, Al Rustamani left TECOM Group to take up the position as Chief Operating Officer of the AW Rostamani Group while also serving as CEO of AWR Properties, the real estate arm of the family business. She has achieved significant recognition for her business leadership, ranking number 9 in CEO Middle East's fifth annual list of the world's most powerful Arab women in 2015 and being picked as the Advertising Person of the Year by Dubai Lynx for 2015. She was also listed as number 20 in Forbes 'Middle East Power Businesswomen 2021.'

Beyond the family business, she serves on multiple high-profile boards including Dubai Healthcare City Authority, Noor Bank, and HSBC Bank Middle East. Her previous board positions included Dubai Media Incorporated, National Media Council, and Emirates Central Cooling Systems Corporation (Empower), and she served as Chairperson of the Dubai Design and Fashion Council.
achievements: Ranked #9 in CEO Middle East's most powerful Arab women list (2015)
Named Advertising Person of the Year by Dubai Lynx (2015)

history: The Al Rostamani family is a prominent Emirati merchant family of Persian origin, with their business empire established in Dubai in the 1950s. According to Gulf News records, Abdullah Al Rostamani was one of the three UAE businessmen who purchased Gulf News in 1984 alongside Obaid Humaid Al Tayer and Juma Al Majid, forming Al Nisr Publishing. The AW Rostamani Group was officially established in 1954 by Al Rostamani brothers Abdullah and Abdul Wahid, sons of a local pearl trader. They began their business journey in 1951 on the shore of Dubai Creek, opening Dubai's first bookshop, Al Ahliya Library, in 1954, importing books from Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon. The family successfully transitioned from traditional pearl trading to modern commercial enterprises, building a diversified business empire spanning automotive, real estate, retail, logistics, lighting, travel and agritech sectors. By 1968, they had established Arabian Automobiles as exclusive distributors of Nissan, INFINITI, and Renault. The company employs over 4,000 workers and operates across the GCC region. Abdullah Al Rostamani died in 2006, with his position on the Gulf News board held by a family nominee. Today, the family is led by Khalid Abdul Wahid Al Rostamani as Chairman, and Dr. Amina Al Rustamani serves as Director and Board Member of AW Rostamani Group and CEO of AWR Properties, reflecting the family's continued prominence in UAE business circles.
modernStatus: Major business conglomerate operating AW Rostamani Group with over 4,000 employees across automotive, real estate, retail, logistics, lighting, travel and agritech sectors, with operations throughout the GCC region

Ahmad Al Tayer is an Emirati political figure who has served in government positions in the United Arab Emirates. He appears among the notable politicians listed in the compilation of prominent Emiratis who have contributed to the UAE's political landscape.

As part of the political establishment, Al Tayer represents the involvement of notable merchant families in the governance and development of the UAE. His inclusion in the list of prominent Emirati politicians indicates his role in the country's political and administrative affairs.

history: The Al Tayer family established themselves as prominent Dubai merchants, with the modern Al Tayer Group being founded in 1979 as a privately held holding company. The family's business empire expanded significantly under the leadership of Obaid Humaid Al Tayer, who served as UAE Finance Minister and became chairman of the Al Tayer Group. In 1984, Obaid Al Tayer, along with partners Abdullah Al Rostamani and Juma Al Majid, purchased Gulf News and formed Al Nisr Publishing, transforming it into one of the region's leading English-language newspapers. The Al Tayer Group diversified into multiple sectors including automotive (representing Land Rover, Jaguar, Ford, Lincoln, Ferrari, and Maserati since 1982), luxury retail through Al Tayer Insignia (operating nearly 200 stores across 6 Middle Eastern countries representing brands like Bulgari, Yves Saint Laurent, Prada, and Armani), real estate, engineering, and media. The family's retail division achieved notable success by opening the first Bloomingdale's store outside the United States in Dubai Mall in 2010, and launching the luxury e-commerce platform Ounass.com in 2016. Currently employing nearly 9,000 people, the Al Tayer Group represents one of the UAE's most diversified business conglomerates, with the family maintaining significant influence in Dubai's business landscape through their media ownership, government connections, and extensive retail and automotive operations across the Gulf region.
modernStatus: Leading business family controlling the Al Tayer Group (automotive, retail, real estate) and Al Nisr Publishing (Gulf News), with extensive operations across 6 Middle Eastern countries and nearly 9,000 employees

name: Al Mulla
originStory: The Al Mulla family descended from the Banu Tamim tribe and established themselves as pearl merchants and traders along the Arabian Gulf coast during the 18th century. Their name became synonymous with successful commercial ventures as they built trading networks connecting the Gulf with India and Persia.
description: The Al Mulla family is a prominent merchant dynasty originating from the Banu Tamim tribe, with extensive business interests across the Gulf region. Known for their successful transition from traditional pearling and trading to modern diversified business operations spanning construction, automotive, and financial services.

notableFigures.name: Early Al Mulla Patriarch
notableFigures.title: Pearl Merchant
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of family's Gulf coast trading operations

notableFigures.name: Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla
notableFigures.title: Secretary of State and Business Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder and patriarch of Al Mulla Group
notableFigures.significance: Founded Al Mulla Group in 1938, establishing one of Kuwait's most prominent business dynasties.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla served as Secretary of State and established Al Mulla Group in 1938 by setting up an electrical and domestic appliance store in Kuwait. He soon acquired the GEC franchise from the United Kingdom, marking the beginning of what would become one of Kuwait's largest diversified business groups. Under his leadership, the company expanded into various sectors including automotive, with the creation of Bader Al Mulla and Brothers Company in 1947, which acquired distribution rights for Chrysler Middle East, Plymouth and Dodge Middle East vehicles in Kuwait.

Al Mulla continued to expand the business into marine products, HVAC contracting, travel and industrial equipment distribution. His entrepreneurial vision laid the foundation for what would eventually become a business empire with operations in 8 countries and over 15,000 employees. Following his death in 1955, his business legacy was continued by his sons, with his eldest son Bader taking over as chairman.
achievements: Founded Al Mulla Group in 1938
Acquired GEC franchise from the United Kingdom
Established Bader Al Mulla and Brothers Company in 1947
Secured distribution rights for major American automotive brands in Kuwait

notableFigures.name: Bader Al Mulla
notableFigures.title: Chairman of Al Mulla Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Eldest son and successor to founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla
notableFigures.significance: Led Al Mulla Group as chairman from 1955-1969, continuing his father's business legacy.
notableFigures.biography:
Bader Al Mulla was the eldest son of Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla, founder of Al Mulla Group. Following his father's death in 1955, Bader assumed leadership of the family business as chairman, a position he held until 1969. During his tenure, he oversaw the continued expansion of the Al Mulla Group's operations across various sectors including automotive, marine products, HVAC contracting, and industrial equipment distribution.

After stepping down as chairman in 1969, Bader was succeeded by his brothers Najeeb and Anwar, who took on the roles of Chairman and Deputy Chairman & CEO respectively. The company bearing his name, Bader Al Mulla and Brothers Company, had been established in 1947 and was instrumental in securing major automotive distribution rights in Kuwait. His leadership helped consolidate the business foundation laid by his father during the crucial post-independence period of Kuwait's economic development.
achievements: Served as Chairman of Al Mulla Group from 1955-1969
Continued expansion of family business operations
Maintained major automotive distribution partnerships

notableFigures.name: Najeeb Al Mulla
notableFigures.title: Chairman of Al Mulla Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Second-generation leader who became Chairman in 1969
notableFigures.significance: Led Al Mulla Group during a period of significant expansion and modernization in the 1970s.
notableFigures.biography:
Najeeb Al Mulla was one of the sons of founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla and brother to Bader and Anwar Al Mulla. In 1969, he became Chairman of Al Mulla Group, taking over from his elder brother Bader, while his brother Anwar assumed the role of Deputy Chairman & CEO. This marked a new phase of shared leadership within the family business.

Under Najeeb's chairmanship, the company underwent significant transformation and expansion. In 1972, three years after he took over, the Maseelah Trading Company was founded, which acquired the distribution rights for Mitsubishi Motor Vehicles in Kuwait, leading to dramatic growth in the group's automotive business. In 1978, Najeeb oversaw radical changes to the company structure, including the appointment of a multinational board instead of a board consisting only of family members. During this period, the Group entered additional businesses such as environmental systems, vehicle rental and leasing, fire protection, office equipment, security services, engineering products, consumer appliances and insurance consultancy.

notableFigures.biography:
Anwar Al Mulla was one of the sons of founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla and brother to Bader and Najeeb Al Mulla. In 1969, when leadership of the family business transitioned from his elder brother Bader, Anwar took on the role of Deputy Chairman & CEO while his brother Najeeb became Chairman. This dual leadership structure marked a new era for the Al Mulla Group.

As Deputy Chairman & CEO, Anwar was instrumental in the operational management and strategic expansion of the company. He worked alongside his brother Najeeb during a period of significant growth and modernization. Under their joint leadership, the company established Maseelah Trading Company in 1972 and secured important distribution rights for Mitsubishi Motor Vehicles in Kuwait. Anwar also played a key role in the 1978 restructuring that introduced a multinational board and expanded the company into numerous new sectors including environmental systems, vehicle rental and leasing, fire protection, office equipment, security services, engineering products, consumer appliances and insurance consultancy.

notableFigures.name: Isa Bin Abdullatif Al Serkal
notableFigures.title: British Residency Agent
notableFigures.roleDescription: British Native Residency Agent in Sharjah during the early-mid 20th century
notableFigures.significance: Served as the key British representative and intermediary in Sharjah, playing a crucial role in regional politics and negotiations including the establishment of Sharjah Airport.
notableFigures.biography:
Isa bin Abdullatif Al Serkal served as the British Residency Agent in Sharjah during a critical period of regional development in the early-to-mid 20th century. He acted as the primary intermediary between the British government and local rulers, representing British interests while managing complex tribal and political relationships. His role was particularly significant during the turbulent period involving Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi, the headman of Al Heera, when Al Serkal's cousin, Ibrahim bin Rajab, was killed by a shot fired in Sharjah on 11 October 1925. The attack was believed to have been aimed at Al Serkal himself, highlighting the dangerous nature of his position.

Al Serkal played a crucial role in the negotiations for the establishment of Sharjah Airport in the 1930s. He was a co-signatory to the historic agreement made on 22 June 1932 between the British government and Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi, the Ruler of Sharjah, which established the first airport in the Trucial States. The agreement stipulated that Imperial Airways would only deal with the Sheikh through the Residency Agent, emphasizing Al Serkal's central role in this groundbreaking aviation development. His position required him to balance British imperial interests with local sensitivities, often in challenging circumstances involving tribal conflicts, debt disputes, and territorial disagreements among the various rulers of the Trucial Coast.
achievements: Co-signed the historic 1932 agreement establishing Sharjah Airport, the first airport in the Trucial States
Served as key British intermediary in complex tribal and political negotiations
Represented British subjects' interests in debt disputes involving local merchants

history: The Al Serkal family emerged from the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the Liwa Oasis region during the 18th century, establishing themselves as merchants and traders who eventually migrated to coastal areas to capitalize on maritime commerce and the pearl diving industry. The family has successfully transitioned from traditional Gulf commerce to contemporary business ventures while maintaining their influential position in UAE society. The family's modern prominence is exemplified by Abdelmonem Bin Eisa Alserkal, whose grandfather Nasser bin Abdullatif Alserkal (1917-1990) was involved in setting up Dubai State Telephone Company Limited, the UAE's first telecommunication firm. His father, Eisa bin Nasser Alserkal, is a businessman involved in international trade. Abdelmonem has become a prominent patron of the arts, founding Alserkal Avenue in 2008, an arts district housed in a former industrial district in the Al Quoz area of Dubai. The family has also demonstrated international cultural engagement, with the Al-Serkal Mosque in Phnom Penh, Cambodia being a gift from the Al Serkal family in 1968, later replaced by a new building gifted by the United Arab Emirates in 2014. The family continues to hold significant business positions, with Abdelmonem serving as a board member of Etisalat Group and being recognized internationally for his cultural contributions.
modernStatus: Active in telecommunications (Etisalat Group board membership), cultural patronage (Alserkal Avenue, Alserkal Arts Foundation), and international trade, with significant recognition in arts patronage including French Ordre des Arts et des Lettres

notableFigures.name: Ali Hassan al-Majid
notableFigures.title: Colonel General
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer and politician, Defense Minister and Interior Minister
notableFigures.significance: Notorious Iraqi military officer known as 'Chemical Ali' for his use of chemical weapons against Kurdish populations during the Al-Anfal campaign.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali Hassan al-Majid al-Tikriti was born around 1941 in al-Awja near Tikrit, a first cousin of Saddam Hussein from the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. He worked as a motorcycle messenger and driver in the Iraqi Army from 1959 until the Ba'ath Party seized power in 1968, after which he gained entry into the Military Academy and was commissioned as an Infantry officer. His rise was aided by his cousin Saddam, initially becoming an aide to Defense Minister Hammadi Shihab in the early 1970s, then head of the government's Security Office.

During the late stages of the Iran-Iraq War, al-Majid served as Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party from March 1987 to April 1989, effectively making him Saddam's proconsul in northern Iraq. In this role, he ordered the indiscriminate use of chemical weapons against Kurdish targets during the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign, earning him the nickname 'Chemical Ali.' The campaign resulted in the destruction of 4,000 villages and the killing of an estimated 180,000 Kurds. He later served as military governor of occupied Kuwait during the Gulf War and held various ministerial posts including Defense Minister and Interior Minister.

Following the 2003 invasion of Iraq, al-Majid was captured by American forces on 17 August 2003 in Basra. He was tried by the Iraqi Special Tribunal and received four death sentences for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes. He was executed by hanging on 25 January 2010, having been convicted for his role in the Kurdish genocide, killing Shia Muslims, and the Halabja chemical attack.
achievements: Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party (1987-1989)
Military Governor of Kuwait during Gulf War (1990)
Defense Minister of Iraq
Interior Minister of Iraq

notableFigures.name: Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid
notableFigures.title: Head of Republican Guard
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer, first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
notableFigures.significance: Head of Iraq's Republican Guard who defected with his brother in 1995 but was killed upon their return in 1996.
notableFigures.biography:
Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid was born on 1 June 1960, the first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Iraqi president Saddam Hussein. He was married to Rana Hussein, Saddam's daughter, and was the brother of Hussein Kamel al-Majid, who was married to another of Saddam's daughters, Raghad Hussein. For a time, he served as head of the Republican Guard until he was removed from the position in 1986 in favor of Saddam Hussein's son, Qusay Hussein.

Due to his close resemblance to the Iraqi leader, Saddam Kamel played the part of Saddam Hussein in the propagandistic film 'The Long Days,' which depicted Saddam Hussein's early life and rise to power. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Hussein Kamel and their wives, fleeing to Jordan. His brother provided information to UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs during their exile.

In 1996, believing Saddam's assurances that they had been pardoned for their defection, the brothers returned to Iraq. However, shortly after their return on 23 February 1996, both Saddam Kamel and his brother Hussein Kamel were killed in a prolonged gun battle. According to Iraqi authorities, they were killed by other members of their clan who exercised personal vengeance upon the brothers, possibly as a way for Saddam to circumvent his promise of pardon by masking the executions as a family quarrel.
achievements: Head of Republican Guard
Actor in propaganda film 'The Long Days'
Part-time actor

notableFigures.name: Hussein Kamel al-Majid
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi official, son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
notableFigures.significance: Brother of Saddam Kamel who defected in 1995 and provided intelligence about Iraq's WMD programs to Western agencies before being killed in 1996.
notableFigures.biography:
Hussein Kamel al-Majid was the brother of Saddam Kamel al-Majid and was married to Raghad Hussein, one of Saddam Hussein's daughters. He held a significant position within the Iraqi government and had access to sensitive information about the country's weapons programs. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Saddam Kamel and their wives, seeking refuge in Jordan.

During his time in exile, Hussein Kamel provided valuable information to UNSCOM (United Nations Special Commission), the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs. His testimony and intelligence were considered crucial for understanding the extent and nature of Iraq's biological, chemical, and nuclear weapons development efforts during the 1980s and early 1990s.

In 1996, Hussein Kamel and his brother returned to Iraq after receiving assurances from Saddam Hussein that they would be pardoned for their defection. However, on 23 February 1996, shortly after their return, both brothers were killed in what Iraqi authorities described as a gun battle with clan members who considered them traitors. The killings were officially presented as family vengeance, though many believed it was orchestrated by Saddam as a way to eliminate the defectors while maintaining plausible deniability.

notableFigures.name: Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti
notableFigures.title: President of Iraq
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician and revolutionary, President of Iraq from 1979 to 2003
notableFigures.significance: Dominant Iraqi political leader for 35 years who ruled as President from 1979 until his overthrow in 2003, known for domestic modernization efforts and regional conflicts.
notableFigures.biography:
Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti was born on 28 April 1937 in al-Awja, a small village near Tikrit, to a Sunni Arab family from the Al-Bejat clan of the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. His father, Hussein Abd al-Majid, was from the Al-Majid branch of the Al-Bejat clan. After a difficult childhood, he was raised by his uncle Khairallah Talfah and joined the revolutionary Ba'ath Party in 1957 at age 20. He participated in a failed assassination attempt against Iraqi leader Abdul Karim Qasim in 1959, after which he fled to Egypt where he completed his education.

Saddam rose to power through the Ba'ath Party, becoming vice president in 1968 under Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr and formally assuming the presidency in 1979. During his rule, he implemented significant domestic reforms including nationalization of oil resources, infrastructure development, healthcare and education expansion, and women's rights advancement. Iraq experienced substantial economic growth in the 1970s, with the Iraqi dinar worth more than 3 US dollars at its peak. He also invested heavily in modernizing the country's infrastructure, bringing electricity to nearly every city and establishing one of the Middle East's most advanced healthcare systems.

However, Saddam's presidency was marked by three major military conflicts: the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), the invasion of Kuwait leading to the Gulf War (1991), and ongoing conflicts with Kurdish populations including the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign. His regime was characterized by authoritarian rule, with extensive use of surveillance, torture, and repression against political opponents. Following the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq, he was captured, tried for crimes against humanity, and executed by hanging on 30 December 2006.
achievements: President of Iraq (1979-2003)
Vice President of Iraq (1968-1979)
Nationalized Iraq's oil industry (1972)
Established universal free education system
Developed modern healthcare infrastructure
Promoted women's rights and education
Received UNESCO award for healthcare system development

Instead, the research focuses on Iraqi political figures from the Al-Majid family, specifically Ali Hassan al-Majid (known as 'Chemical Ali') and the extended family of Saddam Hussein. Ali Hassan al-Majid (c. 1941-2010) was born in al-Awja near Tikrit and belonged to the Bejat clan of the Al-Bu Nasir tribe, the same tribal group as Saddam Hussein. He rose from working as a motorcycle messenger in the Iraqi Army to become a powerful military officer and politician under Saddam Hussein's regime. The Iraqi Al-Majid family gained notoriety through their political and military roles during Ba'ath Party rule, particularly Ali Hassan's involvement in the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign against Kurdish populations in the 1980s, which earned him the nickname 'Chemical Ali.' The research also references modern UAE figures like Sheikh Majid bin Mohammed Al Maktoum of the ruling Al Maktoum family of Dubai, and mentions the Majid Al Futtaim Group, a major UAE business conglomerate, but provides no connection to the merchant Al Majid family described in the existing data.

notableFigures.roleDescription: One of the most famous early Emirati writers
notableFigures.significance: Recognized as one of the most famous Emirati writers alongside Mubarak Al Oqaili and Ahmed bin Sulayem.
notableFigures.biography:
Salem bin Ali al Owais (1887-1959) was one of the most prominent early Emirati writers and poets. He lived during a transformative period in UAE history, spanning the late pearling era through the early oil discoveries. Al Owais was part of a literary tradition that helped establish Emirati cultural identity through written works.

Along with Mubarak Al Oqaili (1880-1954) and Ahmed bin Sulayem (1905-1976), al Owais formed part of the foundational generation of Emirati literature. His work contributed to the early development of written Emirati culture during a time when the region was transitioning from a pearling-based economy to one that would eventually be transformed by oil wealth. His literary contributions helped preserve and develop Emirati cultural heritage during this crucial period of change.

notableFigures.significance: Served as president of both the Indian National Congress (1927) and Muslim League (1918, 1920) during India's independence movement.
notableFigures.biography: Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari (1880-1936) was a prominent surgeon and political leader during India's independence movement. He served as president of the Muslim League in both 1918 and 1920, demonstrating his early leadership in Muslim political representation. Later, he became president of the Indian National Congress in 1927, showing his broader nationalist credentials and ability to bridge communal divides during a critical period in Indian politics. His dual leadership roles in both major political organizations of the time made him a significant figure in the struggle for Indian independence. As a medical professional, he combined his surgical career with political activism, representing the educated Muslim elite's participation in the nationalist movement.

notableFigures.name: Mohammad Hamid Ansari
notableFigures.title: Vice President of India
notableFigures.roleDescription: Diplomat and Vice President of India (2007-2017)
notableFigures.significance: Served as Vice President of India for two terms (2007-2017) with the longest vice presidential tenure in Indian history.
notableFigures.biography: Mohammad Hamid Ansari was born on April 1, 1937, in Kolkata to Mohammad Abdul Aziz Ansari and Aasiya Begum. Though his ancestral home is in Yusufpur, Ghazipur, Uttar Pradesh, he spent his formative years in Kolkata. He comes from a family of Ansari sheikhs descended from the Sufi saint Abdullah Ansari of Herat, and is the grand-nephew of former Indian National Congress president Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari. Joining the Indian Foreign Service in 1961, Ansari served as ambassador to several countries including the UAE (1976-1980), Australia (1985-1989), Afghanistan (1989-1990), Iran (1990-1992), and Saudi Arabia (1995-1999). He also served as India's permanent representative to the United Nations from 1993 to 1995. After his diplomatic career, he became vice-chancellor of Aligarh Muslim University (2000-2002) and chairman of the National Commission for Minorities (2006-2007). Elected Vice President of India on August 10, 2007, he was re-elected in 2012, serving two full terms until 2017. His tenure is tied with Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan for the longest vice presidential tenure in Indian history.

notableFigures.name: Mukhtar Ansari
notableFigures.title: Politician and Gangster
notableFigures.roleDescription: Member of Legislative Assembly from Mau constituency
notableFigures.significance: Five-time MLA from Mau constituency who combined criminal activities with electoral politics in Uttar Pradesh.
notableFigures.biography: Mukhtar Ansari (June 30, 1963 – March 28, 2024) was an Indian gangster, convicted murderer, and politician based in Uttar Pradesh. His paternal grandfather was Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari, the surgeon and early president of the Indian National Congress and Muslim League. His father was Subhan Ullah Ansari, a Communist party leader. The family claims patrilineal lineage from the 11th century Sufi saint Abdullah Ansari of Herat in Afghanistan. Ansari entered politics around 1995 through a student union and became an MLA in 1996, eventually being elected five times from the Mau constituency. He joined the Bahujan Samaj Party in 2007 along with his brother Afzal, but was expelled in 2010 after continued involvement in criminal activities. He then formed his own party, Quami Ekta Dal, in 2010 before rejoining the BSP in 2016. Throughout his political career, he faced numerous criminal cases and spent considerable time in jail from 2005 onwards. He died of a heart attack on March 28, 2024, while in prison at age 60, with his family alleging he was poisoned.
achievements: Five-time MLA from Mau constituency

notableFigures.roleDescription: American actor, comedian, and filmmaker
notableFigures.significance: First Asian American actor to win a Golden Globe for acting on television for his role in Master of None.
notableFigures.biography: Aziz Ismail Ansari was born on February 23, 1983, in Columbia, South Carolina, to Indian immigrants from Tamil Nadu. His parents are Tamil Muslims, though Ansari himself is non-religious. He graduated from NYU Stern School of Business in 2004 with a Bachelor of Business degree in marketing. Ansari began performing comedy in New York City while a student, frequently performing at the Upright Citizens Brigade Theatre. He co-created and starred in the MTV sketch comedy show Human Giant before landing his breakthrough role as Tom Haverford on NBC's Parks and Recreation (2009-2015). In 2015, he co-created and starred in Netflix's critically acclaimed series Master of None, for which he won several awards including two Emmys and a Golden Globe - making him the first Asian American actor to win a Golden Globe for acting on television. As a stand-up comedian, he has released multiple comedy specials and continues to tour. His first book, Modern Romance: An Investigation, was released in 2015, and he was included in Time's 100 most influential people list in 2016.

notableFigures.name: Yassamin Ansari
notableFigures.title: U.S. Representative
notableFigures.roleDescription: U.S. Representative for Arizona's 3rd congressional district
notableFigures.significance: First Iranian American elected to public office in Arizona and youngest female member of Congress.
notableFigures.biography: Yassamin Ansari was born on April 7, 1992, in Seattle, Washington, to parents who immigrated to the United States after fleeing Iran following the Islamic Revolution. She grew up in Scottsdale, Arizona, and attended Chaparral High School, where she organized with the Arizona Democratic Party for Barack Obama's 2008 campaign. She attended Stanford University, receiving a bachelor's degree in international relations, and later earned a master's degree from St. John's College, Cambridge as a Gates Cambridge Scholar. After graduation, she worked in the UN Secretary-General's office under Ban Ki-moon and António Guterres, focusing on climate policy including the Paris Agreement. She was elected to the Phoenix City Council in 2021, becoming the youngest person elected to the council and the first Iranian American elected to public office in Arizona. During her tenure, she created an Office of Heat Response and Mitigation and promoted climate action initiatives. In 2024, she was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives for Arizona's 3rd congressional district, becoming the youngest female member of Congress.
achievements: First Iranian American elected to public office in Arizona

history: The Al Ansari family traces its ancient lineage to the Ansar of Medina, specifically to the companion Abu Ayyub al-Ansari (Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb ibn Tha'laba) from the Banu Najjar tribe of the Khazraj. According to Islamic historical sources, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari was a close companion of Prophet Muhammad who hosted him after the Hijra in 622 CE and became the waqif (endower) of Al-Masjid an-Nabawi by purchasing the land on behalf of the Prophet. The descendants of Abu Ayyub can be found throughout the Arabian Peninsula, with two of his sons, Khalid and Ayyub, remaining in Arabia and their descendants later migrating to Gulf countries including Qatar and Bahrain. The research shows evidence of distinguished Ansari lineage continuing through Islamic history, with notable scholars like the 15th-16th century Zakariya al-Ansari, who served as chief judge (qadi al-qudat) in Egypt and was considered a renewer of the faith (mujaddid) for the 9th Islamic century. In modern times, the Al Ansari name appears in various contexts including diplomatic service, as evidenced by Mohammad Hamid Ansari who served as India's Vice President and had extensive diplomatic experience in Gulf countries including the UAE, and in sports, with athletes like Hajar al-Ansari representing Bahrain in football and other sports. The family's merchant tradition mentioned in existing data aligns with the historical pattern of Ansari descendants establishing trading networks across the Islamic world.
modernStatus: Established as prominent merchants with Al Ansari Exchange being one of the Middle East's largest remittance companies, with family members in various professional fields including diplomacy and sports

notableFigures.title: Dr.
notableFigures.roleDescription: Political analyst, researcher and opinion writer
notableFigures.significance: Renowned Emirati political analyst and researcher specializing in Middle Eastern politics and security issues.
notableFigures.biography:
Salem Al Ketbi is a prominent Emirati political analyst, researcher and opinion writer from a Bedouin background. He holds a Ph.D. in Public Law and Political Science from Hassan II University, Casablanca, for his thesis on political and religious propaganda and leadership through social media in the Arab World.

Al Ketbi has written extensively for major newspapers and media outlets including Gulf News, The Jerusalem Post, Al Arabiya, and many others in both English and Arabic. He focuses on UAE national security, Iranian foreign policy, terrorist organizations and extremist groups. In 2017, he published a political biography of Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan titled 'Fakhr al-Oroba' which won the Arab Women Media Network award for Book of the Year.

He is a member of several prestigious organizations including Chatham House, the International Federation of Journalists, and various press clubs. In 2019, he ran for the Federal National Council election on a platform of education, women and youth empowerment. Al Ketbi has spoken at international forums and is recognized as an expert on Middle Eastern politics.
achievements: Won Arab Women Media Network award for Book of the Year in 2017

Published historical research on UAE and Palestinian relations
Spoke at 2017 MEDays international forum

history: The Al Ketbi family demonstrates the intersection of traditional Bedouin heritage and modern political influence in the UAE. Most prominently represented by Sheikha Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi, born in Al-Hayer in the Al Ain region around 1943 to a religious Bedouin family, the Al Ketbi rose to extraordinary prominence through her marriage to Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan in 1960. As his third wife and most influential spouse, she became known as the 'Mother of the Nation' and bore eight children who form the most powerful bloc within the Al Nahyan ruling family, including current UAE President Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Her sons, known as 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima), include key figures like Crown Prince Khaled bin Mohamed, Deputy PM Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed, and Foreign Minister Abdullah bin Zayed. The family has leveraged this political prominence into significant influence across UAE institutions, with Al Ketbi members holding positions in sovereign wealth funds, oil companies, and think tanks. Contemporary figures like Dr. Ebtesam Al-Ketbi have established intellectual prominence, founding The Emirates Policy Center and becoming the first Arab woman to lead a major think tank. The Al Ketbi story exemplifies how traditional families have adapted to become central players in the modern Gulf political economy.
modernStatus: Central to UAE political power through the Al Nahyan ruling family, with members holding key government positions, sovereign wealth fund leadership, and intellectual influence through think tanks

notableFigures.significance: She serves as Minister of State in the UAE and was ranked 13th on Forbes Middle East's list of most powerful Arab women in the Middle East.
notableFigures.biography:
Maitha Salem Al Shamsi is a politician and government minister in the United Arab Emirates who serves as Minister of State. She completed her Bachelor of Arts in 1980 from King Saud University in Saudi Arabia and her master's degree in 1988 from Alexandria University in Egypt. She also completed a post doctorate in Sociology and has written several books including 'Expatriate Migration to the Gulf Cooperation Council States: Current Problems and Future Prospects', 'the Mother of the Nation: Fatima bint Mubarak', and 'Principles and Accomplishments'.

In 2008 she joined the Federal Government of the United Arab Emirates as the Minister of State and helped establish the Sorbonne University in Abu Dhabi. She has held numerous leadership positions including serving on the board of trustees of Abu Dhabi University, sitting on UNESCO's Scientific Committee for the Arab States on Education and Scientific Research, and serving as chairperson of the Marriage Fund until February 2016. She currently serves as chairperson of the Family Development Foundation, the General Women's Union in United Arab Emirates, and the Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood. In 2017, she pledged seven million dollars to support Rohingya refugees at a United Nations conference in Geneva.

notableFigures.name: Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh of Al Heera
notableFigures.roleDescription: Influential and controversial tribal leader in early 20th century Trucial States
notableFigures.significance: He was a highly influential and divisive figure in regional politics during the early 20th century, described by British officials as 'a stormy petrel of the Trucial Coast, a man feared by everyone in and around Sharjah'.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi was the Sheikh, or head man, of the township of Al Heera, today a suburb of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates. A highly influential and often divisive figure in regional politics during the early 20th century in the Trucial States, he was frequently at odds with other rulers and often in trouble with the British administration. By 1920, facing enormous debts of Rs 21,560 from his town's struggling pearling fleet, he led a desperate attack on Ajman on 15 June 1920, occupying its fort for six days before being forced out by British intervention.

His conflicts led to multiple exiles, including a period with the Shihuh of the Rus Al Jibal mountains and later a four-year exile to Aden (returning after three years) following accusations of involvement in the 1925 killing of Ibrahim bin Rajab in Sharjah. Despite his turbulent relationships, Al Shamsi played a crucial role in the establishment of Sharjah Airport, with his influential backing among Bedouin tribes being instrumental in Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi's successful negotiations with the British for the airport in 1932. He ruled Al Heera until his death on 10 August 1942, when the township reverted to rule by Sharjah.
achievements: Instrumental in concluding negotiations for Sharjah Airport establishment
Maintained significant influence among Bedouin tribes including the Manasir and Bani Qitab

history: The Al Shamsi family demonstrates remarkable diversity in contemporary UAE society, with prominent figures across government, diplomacy, sports, and business. The research reveals several notable Al Shamsi individuals but does not provide clear documentation of their common tribal or ethnic origins. Amna bint Abdullah Al Dahak Al Shamsi serves as Minister of Climate Change and Environment since 2024, holding a PhD in Computer Engineering from Khalifa University and previously serving as UAE Government spokesperson during COVID-19. The diplomatic branch includes Abdul Aziz Nasser Al Shamsi, born in Ajman in 1956, who has served as UAE Ambassador to Italy since 2008 and previously held ambassadorial roles in Brazil, Argentina, Chile, and France, as well as serving as UAE Permanent Representative to the UN. The family also includes Mohammed Al Shamsi, a professional footballer born in 1997 who plays as goalkeeper for Al Wahda FC. Interestingly, Ali Mohammed Hammad Al Shamsi appears as director of UAE intelligence services in connection with high-level diplomatic activities. The research also notes that the Al Bu Shamis tribe historically controlled parts of Ajman before the Al Nuaimi takeover in the early 19th century, though the connection between this historical tribe and the contemporary Al Shamsi individuals is not definitively established in the available sources.

notableFigures.biography:
Jaber Khoory is a businessman who operates the Jaber Khoory Household Appliances Company located in the Irani Souk area of Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates. His business establishment gained international attention when it served as a clue location during the seventh leg of The Amazing Race 23 in 2013, where teams competing in the reality show had to find their next clue at his company.

The inclusion of his business in this internationally televised program highlights the Al Khoory family's established presence in the UAE's commercial sector. His company's location in the Irani Souk suggests connections to the broader Iranian merchant community that has historically played a significant role in the UAE's trade and commerce development.

history: The research reveals significant evidence that Al Khoory may have connections to Iranian/Persian origins rather than exclusively Bani Yas tribal origins. The Wikipedia entry on Khouri/Khoory indicates this surname traditionally means 'priest' in Levantine Arabic and is found among Arab Christians in the Middle East, particularly in the Levant. However, the research also documents extensive Iranian presence in the UAE, with Persians being among the first major groups of foreign settlers in the region dating back to the 1810s, and over 500 Persian Gulf Iranians residing in Abu Dhabi by the early 20th century. Most significantly, the research shows a direct Al-Khoory connection to the UAE: Dr. Syekh Mohammad MT Al-Khoory, Chairman of the Asian Muslim Charity Foundation (AMCF) Dubai, who funded the Subulussalam Al-Khoory Mosque at Muhammadiyah University of Makassar in Indonesia. Additionally, during Season 23 of The Amazing Race, teams visited 'Jaber Khoory Household Appliances Company' in Abu Dhabi's Irani Souk, suggesting the family's established commercial presence in areas with historical Iranian connections. The existing data's attribution to Bani Yas origins may require reassessment in light of the surname's Levantine Christian origins and the documented Iranian commercial presence in the UAE.
modernStatus: Active in Dubai's commercial sector with documented business operations in Abu Dhabi's Irani Souk, and involved in international charitable work through the Asian Muslim Charity Foundation

notableFigures.name: Hikmat Mizban Ibrahim al-Azzawi
notableFigures.title: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Finance
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician and economist who served under Saddam Hussein's government
notableFigures.significance: Served as Deputy Prime Minister and twice as Minister of Finance under Saddam Hussein's Ba'athist government.
notableFigures.biography:
Hikmat Mizban Ibrahim al-Azzawi was born in 1933 and originally trained as an economist. He was arrested in 1960 for protesting against the government of Iraqi General Abd al-Karim Qasim. He joined the Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party in 1968 and was named undersecretary of state for Commerce. His career faced significant turbulence when he was fired from all his official positions in 1982 and demoted to a low administrative job, being expelled from the party until 1985 when he became Governor of the Central Bank of Iraq. In April 1987, he was publicly humiliated and severely dismissed for refusing to transfer a large sum of money at the demand of one of Saddam's uncles.

President Saddam Hussein forced him back into the position of Minister of Finance in 1995. In July 1999, he was given the honorary title of Deputy Prime Minister. After the United States-led Invasion of Iraq in 2003, he was listed as number 45 (8 of Diamonds) on the Iraqi most-wanted playing cards. He was peacefully detained from his house in Baghdad on April 19, 2003 by Iraqi police and handed over to the US military. He died in prison on January 27, 2012, at age 79, due to lack of his heart medications, with his health deteriorating because of aging and cancer.
achievements: Served as Deputy Prime Minister of Iraq

notableFigures.name: Dia Azzawi
notableFigures.title: Artist and Architect
notableFigures.roleDescription: Contemporary Iraqi artist known for incorporating Arabic calligraphy and ancient Mesopotamian art
notableFigures.significance: A prominent contemporary artist whose work includes references to Arabic calligraphy, Sumerian and Assyrian art, and has inspired many contemporary calligraffiti artists.
notableFigures.biography:
Dia Azzawi was born in 1939 and became one of Iraq's most notable contemporary artists. His work is described as contemporary art that includes references to Arabic calligraphy, as well as Sumerian and Assyrian art. Azzawi has been a significant inspiration for many contemporary calligraffiti artists, helping to bridge traditional Iraqi artistic heritage with modern artistic expression.

Azzawi was one of the founding members of Al-Ru'yah al-Jadida (The New Vision) art group, established in 1968 alongside artists Ismail Fatah Al Turk, Muhammed Muhr al-Din, Hashimi al-Samarchi, Salih Jumai'e, and Rafa al-Nasiri. This group promoted the idea of freedom of creativity within a framework of heritage and represented a free art style where artists believed they needed to be true to their own era. He was also a founding member of the One Dimension Group, established around 1971, which sought a new artistic identity drawn from within Iraqi culture and heritage, blending Arabic motifs, especially calligraphy, and geometric shapes into contemporary and abstract artworks. Additionally, he collaborated with Mahmoud Obaidi, an Iraqi-Canadian artist, in building the structure that houses the Nabu Museum in Lebanon.
achievements: Co-founder of Al-Ru'yah al-Jadida (The New Vision) art group in 1968

However, none of these references provide information about the Al Azzawi family as described in the existing data - as merchants of Azd tribal origin who established themselves across the Gulf region in pearling and date commerce. notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Islamic scholar, poet, and mosque Imam
notableFigures.significance: A prominent Iraqi Islamic scholar and leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad during the Ottoman period.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Muhammad Sa'id al-Suwaidi was born in Karkh, Baghdad, in 1728 to the Iraqi al-Suwaidi family, which was famous for its knowledge, jurisprudence, and literature. His father, Sheikh Abdullah bin Hussein al-Suwaidi, was the author of many books, including 'Al-Nafha al-Miskiyya' on the Meccan journey. He studied under his father as well as other scholars such as Sheikh Abd al-Qadir al-Makki al-Harithi and Sheikh Ali al-Ansari, and would temporarily visit Cairo's madrasas.

As a member and leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad, Ottoman Iraq, he became a significant religious figure. His house in Karkh was a notable landmark in Baghdad and was later inherited by his son, Muhammad Amin, who used it as a madrasa where he would teach. The upper floor overlooking the road served as a majlis. When the structure weakened, it was renovated and became known as al-Suwaidi Madrasa, remaining one of the famous schools in Karkh until Muhammad Amin's death in 1830. He died in 1808 and was buried in the Sheikh Ma'ruf Cemetery.
achievements: Leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad
Established influential madrasa that operated until 1830
Part of scholarly family tradition in Islamic jurisprudence and literature

notableFigures.name: Salemin bin Nasir Al-Suwaidi
notableFigures.title: Chief
notableFigures.roleDescription: Chief of the Sudan tribe (Suwaidi) in Al Bidda
notableFigures.significance: Led the Sudan tribe in Al Bidda and dealt with British authorities during incidents involving pirates and territorial disputes.
notableFigures.biography:
Salemin bin Nasir Al-Suwaidi served as chief of the Sudan tribe (Suwaidi) in Al Bidda during the mid-19th century. In November 1839, when an outlaw from Abu Dhabi named Ghuleta took refuge in Al Bidda, British naval commander A. H. Nott demanded that Al-Suwaidi take Ghuleta into custody and warned him of consequences in case of non-compliance. Al-Suwaidi obliged the British request in February 1840 and also arrested the pirate Jasim bin Jabir and his associates.

Despite his compliance, the British demanded a fine of 300 German krones in compensation for damages incurred by pirates off the coast of Al Bidda. In February 1841, British naval squadrons arrived and threatened consequences if he declined to pay. Al-Suwaidi ultimately declined on the basis that he was uninvolved in bin Jabir's actions. On 26 February, the British fired on Al Bidda, striking a fort and several houses. Al-Suwaidi then paid the fine in full following threats of further action by the British.
achievements: Led the Sudan tribe during British colonial period
Managed tribal relations with British authorities
Maintained tribal leadership during territorial conflicts

notableFigures.name: Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Sheikh of the Sudan tribe and father-in-law of Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
notableFigures.significance: Connected the Al Suwaidi family to the ruling Al Nahyan family through his daughter's marriage to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan.
notableFigures.biography:
Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi served as Sheikh of the Sudan tribe during the late 19th century and played a significant role in tribal politics of the region. He became closely connected to the ruling Al Nahyan family when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan married his daughter, making him the father-in-law of one of Abu Dhabi's most influential rulers.

Sultan bin Nasir was instrumental in Zayed's political schemes, particularly the plan to establish a fort at Al Zorah in Ajman in 1895. It was with the Sudan tribe under Sultan bin Nasir that Zayed conceived this idea to consolidate and build a bridgehead into the northern emirates. In 1897, a section of the Sudan under Sultan bin Nasser requested permission to settle Al Zorah with Zayed's support, which was initially granted by the British Resident, though the scheme was later abandoned due to opposition from other rulers.
achievements: Sheikh of the Sudan tribe
Father-in-law to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
Key ally in Abu Dhabi's northern expansion plans

notableFigures.name: Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi
notableFigures.title: Headman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Headman of the Sudan tribe in Bida'a, Qatar
notableFigures.significance: Led the Sudan tribe in Qatar during conflicts with British forces over piracy accusations.
notableFigures.biography:
Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi served as headman of the Sudan tribe in Bida'a (present-day Doha) during the early 19th century. In February 1841, he became involved in a significant incident when the Sudan were suspected of harboring the pirate Jasim bin Jabir, known as Raqraqi, in Bida'a. This led to British naval intervention as they sought to punish those they believed were supporting piracy in the Persian Gulf.

When a British squadron arrived to confront him, Salimayn initially resisted their demands. However, after the British fired several shots at the town as a show of force, he was compelled to comply with their ultimatum. He paid a substantial fine in cash and jewelry to the British authorities and handed over Raqraqi's ship, which was subsequently set on fire by the British forces. This incident represents the challenges faced by tribal leaders in Qatar during the period of British maritime dominance in the Gulf.
achievements: Headman of Sudan tribe in Qatar
Navigated tribal leadership during British colonial pressures
Managed resolution of piracy accusations against his tribe

notableFigures.name: Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Political leader and opponent in Abu Dhabi succession disputes
notableFigures.significance: Played a key role in Abu Dhabi political affairs and succession disputes in 1928.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi was a prominent political figure in Abu Dhabi during the early 20th century, particularly influential in matters of succession and tribal politics. He represented the Sudan tribe's interests in the highest levels of Abu Dhabi's political hierarchy and often served as a voice for tribal concerns in the ruling family's deliberations.

In 1928, Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi played a crucial role in Abu Dhabi political affairs when he led the opposition to Shakhbut bin Sultan becoming ruler. When this succession was proposed to the family and leading notables, Al Suwaidi organized resistance and instead supported Hazza bin Sultan for the position. His opposition demonstrated the significant political influence that the Sudan tribe, and the Al Suwaidi family in particular, wielded in Abu Dhabi's internal affairs during this critical period of the emirate's development.
achievements: Led opposition in Abu Dhabi succession dispute of 1928
Represented Sudan tribe interests in high-level politics
Influential voice in Abu Dhabi's political decision-making

history: The research reveals multiple contemporary Al Suwaidi family members but provides limited information about the family's historical origins or migration patterns. Notable modern figures include Khawla Ahmed Khalifa Alsuwaidi, a poet and master calligrapher who is married to Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the UAE's National Security Advisor. The family has produced several prominent individuals: poet Ousha bint Khalifa Al Suwaidi (1920-2018), known as 'Fatah al-Arab' (Girl of the Arabs), who became one of the leading figures in Emirati Nabati poetry and was the sister of politician Ahmed Khalifa Al Suwaidi. The research documents a controversial case involving three Al Suwaidi sisters - Asma, Mariam, and Alyaziyah Khalifa al-Suwaidi - who were detained by UAE authorities in February 2015 for campaigning for the release of their brother Issa al-Suwaidi, who had been sentenced to 10 years in prison in 2013 for alleged links to Al Islah, an Islamist organization considered by the UAE government as a terrorist group. The family appears to have both establishment connections through marriage to the ruling family and members who have faced political persecution, suggesting internal diversity in political alignment within the broader Al Suwaidi clan.
modernStatus: Mixed political status with some members connected to ruling elite through marriage while others have faced political persecution

notableFigures.title: Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club
notableFigures.roleDescription: First chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after the 1974 merger
notableFigures.significance: He served as the first chairman of Al Ain Sports Club following the merger with Al Tadhamun Club in 1974.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed Salem Al Dhaheri became a significant figure in UAE sports history when he was appointed as the first chairman of the board of directors of Al Ain Sports Club in 1974. This appointment came after the historic merger between Al Ain Club and Al Tadhamun Club on 10 November 1974, which consolidated the football scene in Al Ain city. The merger was an important moment in UAE football, bringing together two clubs that had been formed when some members broke away from the original Al Ain club in 1971.

Under Al Dhaheri's leadership, the newly merged Al Ain Sports Club began its journey toward becoming one of the most successful football clubs in the United Arab Emirates. His role as chairman positioned him at the forefront of the club's early development during a crucial period in UAE football history. The club went on to win its first Abu Dhabi League title in 1974, the same year as the merger, establishing a foundation for future success that would see Al Ain become the most trophy-laden club in UAE football.
achievements: First Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after 1974 merger
Led the club during its early successful period

notableFigures.name: Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri
notableFigures.title: Film Producer and Entrepreneur
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder and CEO of Film Gate Productions and Al Kalema Productions
notableFigures.significance: He is a prominent Emirati film producer who has contributed significantly to the development of the UAE film industry and received international recognition for his work.
notableFigures.biography:
Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri was born on 14 January 1975 in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates. He pursued higher education in the United States, earning a Master of Business Administration (MBA) from the New York Institute of Technology (2007–2009) and a Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Filmmaking from the New York Film Academy (2010–2012). He also received a certificate in Advanced Project Management from Stanford University, demonstrating his commitment to professional development in both business and creative fields.

Al Dhaheri launched Al Kalema Production in January 2012 and Film Gate Production in January 2018, establishing himself as a significant figure in the UAE's growing film industry. His companies have produced a variety of films, TV series, and documentaries that reflect Emirati culture, with productions appearing at prestigious international festivals including the Cannes Film Festival, Dubai International Film Festival, and Abu Dhabi Film Festival. His notable works include 'The Platform' (2020–2021), 'Dreams Drawn by Dust' (2022), 'Scattered Barriers' (2021), 'House of Life' (2022), 'Swim 62' (2023), and 'Abu Dhabi Success Story' (2024). His achievements have been recognized at numerous international festivals, including winning first prize for Best Documentary Film at the Gulf Film Festival in Riyadh (2024) for 'Swim 62', and multiple awards at the New York Film Awards.
achievements: Founded Al Kalema Production (2012) and Film Gate Production (2018)
Won Best Documentary Film at Gulf Film Festival for 'Swim 62' (2024)

notableFigures.name: Nasser al-Dhaheri
notableFigures.title: Writer and Journalist
notableFigures.roleDescription: Emirati writer and journalist
notableFigures.significance: He is a notable Emirati writer and journalist representing the Al Dhaheri family in media and literature.
notableFigures.biography:
Nasser al-Dhaheri is an accomplished Emirati writer and journalist who has contributed to the UAE's literary and media landscape. As a member of the Al Dhaheri family, which traces its heritage to the Dhawahir tribe of the Al Ain region, he represents the intellectual and cultural contributions of the family to modern Emirati society. His work in journalism and writing reflects the growing importance of media and literature in the UAE's cultural development.

His career in journalism positions him as a voice in the UAE's media landscape, contributing to public discourse and cultural expression. The recognition of his work among notable figures from the Al Dhaheri family indicates his significance within the UAE's intellectual community. His contributions to literature and journalism represent the diversification of prominent Emirati families into creative and intellectual fields, moving beyond traditional roles in business and politics.

history: The Al Dhaheri family descends from the Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر) tribe of the United Arab Emirates, whose main center was the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain. The tribe originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman before settling in the Al Ain region. By the early 20th century, the Dhawahir owned much of the cultivated land and property in Al Ain oasis, except for Buraimi village itself. The tribe consisted of three subsections: the Daramikah who populated Hili and Qattara, the Jawabir in Al Ain, and the Bani Saad in Jimi. They maintained large flocks of sheep, herded camels, and traded in charcoal from the fertile woodlands of the oasis. The Dhawahir established a strong and longstanding alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family and the Bani Yas confederation. This relationship was formalized in 1840 when Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakbut Al Nahyan took full responsibility for the Dhawahir tribe. The alliance was further strengthened through marriage, as Sheikh Nasser bin Zayed Al Nahyan's mother was Sheikha Amna bint Salah Al Badi from the Bedouin branch of the Dhawahir tribe. Despite occasional conflicts, including an uprising against Zayed the Great in 1877 over land purchases, the tribe maintained its position in the region. Modern Al Dhaheri family members have achieved prominence in various fields, including business, politics, sports, and entertainment, with Ali Saeed bin Harmal Al Dhaheri serving as chairman of Abu Dhabi University and various business ventures.
modernStatus: Prominent in business, government, sports, and entertainment sectors across the UAE, with notable figures like Ali Saeed bin Harmal Al Dhaheri in education and business leadership

migrationPath.description: Migration from original area in Al Dhahirah (Oman) to Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain region

They built their wealth through pearl trade and traditional commerce, later expanding into modern business sectors following UAE's formation.

history: The Al Mansoori family represents a prominent merchant lineage of Bani Yas tribal origin from the Liwa Oasis. According to existing family records, they migrated to Abu Dhabi in the early 1800s where they established themselves as successful pearl traders and merchants. The research reveals notable modern descendants including Hazzaa Al Mansoori (born 1983), the UAE's first astronaut who flew to the International Space Station in 2019, becoming the first Emirati and Arab from the Gulf region to reach space. His full name is Hazzaa Ali Abdan Khalfan Al Mansoori, and he was born in Al Wathba, Abu Dhabi, spending his childhood in the Liwa desert region where his family originated. Another prominent family member is Dr. Hawaa Al Thahak Al Mansoori, an accomplished physician and politician who serves on the Federal National Council of the UAE since 2019. She was the first Emirati woman to receive a UAE Presidential Scholarship to study medicine in the United States, completing her education at the University of Maryland and George Washington University. The family has maintained strong connections to their ancestral Liwa region while achieving prominence in modern UAE society across space exploration, medicine, politics, and government service.

notableFigures.name: Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi
notableFigures.title: Sheikha
notableFigures.roleDescription: Wife of Abu Dhabi ruler and matriarch of the Al Nahyan ruling dynasty
notableFigures.significance: She was the wife of Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan and mother of Sheikhs Shakhbut and Zayed, who ruled Abu Dhabi and founded the UAE.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi was from the prominent Al Qubaisi Bedouin tribe from Liwa, who also settled Khor Al Adaid. She was originally from Mezairaa in Liwa. She married Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, who became Ruler of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi from 1922.

After her husband's assassination in 1927, she demonstrated remarkable leadership by taking her son Zayed from Abu Dhabi to Al Ain for safety. She made her sons swear an oath that they would not kill or fight against each other, helping to maintain family unity during a turbulent period. Her sons included Sheikh Shakhbut (who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1928 to 1966), Sheikh Zayed (who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1966 to 2004 and became the first president of the UAE), and Hazza bin Sultan, who served as the Ruler's Representative of the Western Region.

In early 1955, her sons Zayed, Hazza and Shakhbut helped arrange her return to Abu Dhabi on board a Gulf Air plane. She died in October 1970, during the time when her son Zayed was the Ruler of Abu Dhabi. Her legacy is commemorated through the Sheikha Salama Mosque, which was named after her.
achievements: Mother of two Abu Dhabi rulers including UAE founding president Sheikh Zayed

notableFigures.name: Khaled Abdulla Al Qubaisi
notableFigures.title: CEO and Racing Driver
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former CEO of Real Estate & Infrastructure Investments at Mubadala Investment Company and professional racing driver
notableFigures.significance: He is a prominent Emirati businessman who led major investments at Mubadala and achieved international success in motorsport, including winning the Dubai 24 Hour race twice.
notableFigures.biography:
Khaled Abdulla Al Qubaisi was born on December 22, 1975, and is an accomplished Emirati businessman and racing driver. He holds a Master of Science in Project Management from George Washington University and a bachelor's degree in finance and operations management from Boston University. He was awarded the CFA designation in 2003, demonstrating his financial expertise.

Al Qubaisi served as CEO of Mubadala's Real Estate & Infrastructure Investments platform from 2021 to 2024, overseeing major investment portfolios. Prior to this, he held various senior positions including Chief Human Capital Officer at Mubadala, Chief Investment Officer at International Capital, and Head of Corporate Finance & Business Development at the National Bank of Abu Dhabi. He currently serves as Chairman of the National Central Cooling Company (Tabreed) and sits on numerous corporate boards.

In motorsport, Al Qubaisi has achieved remarkable success in international grand touring races since the early 2010s. His biggest accomplishments include overall victories at the 2012 and 2013 Dubai 24 Hour races, driving a Black Falcon Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG GT3. He also won the Yas Marina 12-Hour Race in 2013 and competed three times in the 24 Hours of Le Mans, with his best finish being 19th overall in 2014. He is married with three children, including two daughters who are also racing drivers.

notableFigures.title: Former IPIC Managing Director
