notableFigures.significance: He was a senior executive at IPIC who became involved in the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal and was arrested in 2016.
notableFigures.biography:
Khadem al-Qubaisi served as the managing director of the International Petroleum Investment Company (IPIC), a major Abu Dhabi-based sovereign wealth fund focused on energy investments. He held a senior position in the UAE's investment landscape and was responsible for managing significant financial portfolios on behalf of the Emirates.

Al-Qubaisi became prominently known due to his involvement in the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal, one of the world's largest financial fraud cases. He was arrested in 2016 as authorities investigated the complex web of financial transactions and alleged corruption surrounding the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund. Following the scandal's exposure, IPIC was folded into Aabar Investments as part of restructuring efforts.

According to reports, al-Qubaisi blamed UAE authorities, including Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan, for using him as a scapegoat in the affair. His case highlighted the complex relationships between sovereign wealth funds, international finance, and the challenges of oversight in large-scale investment operations.
achievements: Managing Director of IPIC
Senior executive in UAE sovereign wealth fund sector

history: The Al Qubaisi are a sub-tribe of the Bani Yas confederation, historically based in the Liwa Oasis before migrating to Abu Dhabi's coastal areas in the early 19th century to pursue pearl trading and maritime commerce. They hold significant historical importance through their close relationship with the Al Nahyan ruling family, most notably through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, who was the mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. The family has maintained prominent positions in both business and politics throughout the UAE's development. In modern times, the Al Qubaisi have established themselves across diverse sectors including sovereign wealth funds, motorsports, and international business. Khaled Abdullah Al Qubaisi serves as CEO of Real Estate and Infrastructure Investment at Mubadala Investment Company and is an accomplished racing driver who was the first Emirati to compete at Le Mans 24 Hours. His daughters Amna and Hamda Al Qubaisi are pioneering female racing drivers, with Hamda becoming the first woman to achieve a podium finish in Italian F4 history in 2021. However, the family name has also been associated with controversy through Khadem Abdullah al-Qubaisi, who was sentenced to 15 years in prison in 2019 for corruption and money laundering related to the 1Malaysia Development Berhad scandal while serving as managing director of IPIC. The family's influence extends through various government positions and business leadership roles, maintaining their status as one of the prominent merchant families within the UAE's political and economic landscape.
modernStatus: Prominent family with leadership positions in sovereign wealth funds, government ministries, and international business, alongside pioneering roles in motorsports

notableFigures.biography:
Major General Mohammed Khalfan Al Rumaithi served as the Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force from 2016 to 2019, during a critical period of modernization and development for the force. His tenure came during the modernization, qualitative and strategic development stage of Abu Dhabi Police, which began in 1995 under Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan's leadership.

As a member of the Al Rumaithi family, his appointment to this senior security position demonstrates the family's trust and standing within UAE security establishments. His leadership of Abu Dhabi Police placed him in charge of one of the most technologically advanced police forces in the region, overseeing approximately 36,000 total staff including civil defence, ambulance and fire services and border security.

notableFigures.title: Brigadier, Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police Force
notableFigures.significance: Served as Brigadier and Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, contributing to the force's development during the progressive stage.
notableFigures.biography:
Brigadier Thani Obeid Khamees Al Rumaithi served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, during what is known as the progressive stage of the force's development. His tenure came during a crucial period when Abu Dhabi Police was evolving toward eventual affiliation and merging into the Federal Ministry of Interior, following the 1979 merger that made the force part of the federal police and security forces.

His leadership bridged an important transitional period for Abu Dhabi Police, serving just before Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan assumed the role of General Commander in 1995, which marked the beginning of the modernization and strategic development phase of the force.
achievements: Served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police (1991-1995)
Led force during progressive development stage

notableFigures.name: Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi
notableFigures.title: Board Member, Central Bank of the UAE
notableFigures.roleDescription: Member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the UAE
notableFigures.significance: Serves as a board member of the Central Bank of the UAE, representing the Al Rumaithi family in the country's highest financial institution.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi serves as a member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates (CBUAE), the state institution responsible for managing the country's currency, monetary policy, banking and insurance regulation. His appointment to this prestigious position places him among the key decision-makers for the UAE's financial and monetary policies.

As a board member of the CBUAE, Al Rumaithi participates in overseeing the institution's mandate to issue and manage the UAE dirham, ensure currency stability, manage the UAE's credit policy, develop and oversee the banking system, and represent the UAE in international financial institutions such as the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. His role demonstrates the Al Rumaithi family's involvement in the UAE's financial sector governance.
achievements: Board member of the Central Bank of the UAE
Involved in UAE monetary policy governance

history: The Al Rumaithi family, while traditionally described as part of the Bani Yas confederation from the Liwa Oasis, has produced notable figures in modern UAE government and business. The research reveals Saeed Khalifa Humeid Al Rumaithi as a prominent government official who served as secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi for approximately two decades, resigning in late 2004. He previously worked in the Abu Dhabi Culture and Heritage Department and later became involved in various charitable and business organizations, serving on the Zayed Charitable Foundation Board of Trustees, GIC Board of Directors, and founding the Care Institute. The family name derives from 'Rumaith' meaning 'small spear,' reflecting their historical tribal warrior heritage. Additionally, Major General Hamidi al-Rumaithi is mentioned as commander of the UAE Navy as of 2026, indicating the family's continued involvement in high-level government and military positions.
modernStatus: Active in government service, military leadership, and charitable organizations with members serving in ministerial and naval command positions

notableFigures.title: Senior Government Official
notableFigures.roleDescription: Senior aide to Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed and Manchester City board member
notableFigures.significance: Senior aide to UAE President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and served on Manchester City's board during the club's financial fair play violations.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohamed Al Mazrouei served as one of the two senior aides of Mohamed bin Zayed (MbZ) and worked as undersecretary at the Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court (CPC). He held significant influence in the UAE government structure and was closely involved in the Crown Prince's operations.

Al Mazrouei also served on Manchester City's board during a controversial period when the club was investigated for financial fair play violations. His dual role as a senior government official and football club board member highlighted the close connections between UAE leadership and the club's ownership under Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
achievements: Senior aide to UAE President Mohamed bin Zayed
Board member of Manchester City F.C.

history: The research confirms the Al Mazrouei as a prominent merchant family from the Bani Yas tribal confederation, originally from the Liwa Oasis region. A key piece of evidence for their prominence comes from the marriage of Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei to Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the second President of the UAE, on October 18, 1964. The research specifically notes that 'Sheikha Shamsa Bint Suhail was from a famous Al Khamis family of the Al Shikr branch of the Mazaria tribe in Liwa.' This marriage produced eight children, including sons Sultan and Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, and daughters who married into other prominent Al Nahyan branches. The Al Mazrouei family's integration into the highest levels of UAE leadership through this union demonstrates their evolution from Bani Yas merchants to influential political allies. Their connection to the Al Khamis family and the Al Shikr branch provides specific genealogical detail about their tribal structure within the broader Mazaria/Al Mazrouei confederation. The family's prominence is further evidenced by Shamsa bint Suhail's position as the widow of a UAE President and mother to influential members of the next generation of Emirati leadership.
modernStatus: Prominent political and social family through marriage alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family, with direct connections to UAE presidential lineage

notableFigures.name: Mahmoud Pasha Hammadi
notableFigures.title: Pasha and Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of Nag Hammadi city and major landholder
notableFigures.significance: Founded the city of Nag Hammadi in Egypt and was a prominent opponent of British colonial rule.
notableFigures.biography:
Mahmoud Pasha Hammadi was a member of the Hammadi family in Sohag, Egypt, who founded the town of Nag Hammadi in the 19th century. The city was established on the site of older villages Ansan and al-Luaqi and was named after its founder. He was a major landholder in Sohag and became known for his strong opposition to British rule in Egypt beginning in 1882.

As a pasha, Mahmoud held significant political and social influence in Upper Egypt. His opposition to British colonial administration made him a notable figure in Egyptian resistance during the early period of British occupation. The city he founded, Nag Hammadi, would later become famous for the discovery of ancient Gnostic texts in 1945, though this occurred long after his time.
achievements: Founded the city of Nag Hammadi
Led opposition to British rule in Egypt from 1882

notableFigures.name: Sa'dun Hammadi
notableFigures.title: Foreign Minister and Prime Minister of Iraq
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician, writer, and economist who served in senior government positions
notableFigures.significance: Served as Iraq's Foreign Minister for nearly a decade and briefly as Prime Minister, playing key roles in oil nationalization and international diplomacy.
notableFigures.biography:
Sa'dun Hammadi was born to a Shi'ite family in Karbala on 22 June 1930. He pursued higher education, obtaining a Master's degree in economics from the American University of Beirut and a PhD in economics from the University of Wisconsin–Madison in 1956. Hammadi joined the Ba'ath Party in 1949 and is credited with introducing Ba'athism to Iraq, becoming believed to be the first Iraqi member of the party.

Hammadi's political career began in earnest after the 1963 coup, when he was appointed Minister of Agrarian Reform. He later became head of the Iraq National Oil Company in 1968 and Minister of Oil in 1970, overseeing the historic nationalization of Iraq's oil industry in 1972. From 1974 to 1983, he served as Iraq's Foreign Minister, representing the country during crucial periods including the Iran-Iraq War and conducting diplomatic missions worldwide.

In March 1991, following the Gulf War, Hammadi was appointed Prime Minister by Saddam Hussein but was forced out by September due to his reformist views. He then served as Speaker of the National Assembly from 1996 until the 2003 U.S. invasion. After being detained by American forces for nine months, he was released in 2004 and eventually settled in Qatar, where he engaged in cultural activities until his death from liver cancer in Germany on 14 March 2007.
achievements: Oversaw Iraq's oil nationalization in 1972
Served as Foreign Minister for nearly a decade (1974-1983)
Introduced Ba'athism to Iraq as first Iraqi party member
Authored numerous cultural and political works

Her career in football demonstrates the family's continued prominence in contemporary UAE society, extending beyond traditional business and political spheres into sports and athletic achievement.

The research shows several prominent Al Hammadi individuals in modern UAE society, including Areej Al Hammadi, a pioneering female footballer born in 1986 who has represented the UAE national team and holds Guinness World Records, and Ismail Al Hammadi, born in 1988, who has served as a footballer for the UAE national team including at the 2012 Summer Olympics. The name Al Hammadi appears in various contexts throughout UAE society, from sports to what appears to be merchant activities, though the research does not provide definitive information about the family's historical origins, migration patterns, or whether they share common ancestry. history: The Al Ameri family is a prominent merchant family originating from the Bani Yas tribal confederation in Abu Dhabi, with historical roots in the Liwa Oasis region before migrating to coastal areas where they established themselves as influential merchants and traders. The family has produced notable figures including Sheikh Mohammed Bin Musallam Bin Ham Al-Ameri (born 1972), an Emirati billionaire businessman, author, and former UAE Federal National Council member who serves as Deputy Chairman of the Bin Ham Group. His father Sheikh Musallam bin Salem bin Ham Al Ameri and grandfather Sheikh Salem bin Ham played formative roles in the early years of the UAE. The family has maintained significant commercial and social influence from the traditional pearling era through to the modern oil economy, with Sheikh Mohammed being notable for joining The Giving Pledge to donate half his wealth to charity and publishing several books on UAE governance. The family has also produced athletes like footballer Zayed Al-Ameri who plays for Al Jazira club in the UAE Arabian Gulf League, and has business connections through companies like Trojan Construction & Holding Group founded by Eng. Hamad Al Ameri.
modernStatus: Active in business leadership through Bin Ham Group and Trojan Construction & Holding Group, with family members holding prominent positions in politics, business, and sports in the UAE

notableFigures.name: Juma Mohammed Al Kaabi
notableFigures.title: UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
notableFigures.roleDescription: Emirati career diplomat who served as UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
notableFigures.significance: Emirati diplomat who died from injuries sustained in the 2017 Afghanistan bombings while on a humanitarian mission.
notableFigures.biography:
Juma Mohammed Al Kaabi was born in 1966 in the village of Wadi Al Qor in Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates. He was the eldest of his siblings, having 2 brothers and 6 sisters. His father died when he was 18 years old, and he became the primary caretaker of his family. After finishing high school, he joined the United Arab Emirates Armed Forces, then worked for the UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs for 8 years before being appointed as the UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan on July 28, 2016.

On January 10, 2017, Al Kaabi was on a humanitarian mission to Kandahar province, Afghanistan, to lay the foundation stone of an orphanage sponsored by the UAE government. A bombing occurred while he was at a dinner visit at the Kandahar governor's house, killing 13 people and injuring 18 others, including Al Kaabi. He was evacuated to Abu Dhabi and then transferred to a specialized hospital in France for treatment. On February 15, 2017, the Ministry of Presidential Affairs announced that Al Kaabi had died due to his injuries. He was married and had 3 daughters, 2 sons, and 2 grandchildren.
achievements: Served as UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
Died while on humanitarian mission to open an orphanage
notableFigures.birthPlace: Wadi Al Qor, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates

notableFigures.name: Saad Sherida al-Kaabi
notableFigures.title: Qatar Energy Minister
notableFigures.roleDescription: Current Qatar Energy Minister who succeeded Mohammed Saleh Al Sada
notableFigures.significance: Current Qatar Energy Minister who has played a key role in Qatar's energy sector and made significant statements during the 2026 Iran war regarding global energy security.
notableFigures.biography:
Saad Sherida al-Kaabi serves as Qatar's Energy Minister, having succeeded Mohammed Saleh Al Sada in this role. He has been instrumental in overseeing Qatar's vast energy resources and managing the country's position as a major global supplier of liquefied natural gas. During the 2026 Iran war, al-Kaabi made significant statements about the potential global economic impact of the conflict on energy markets.

In March 2026, during the Iran conflict, al-Kaabi warned that if the war continued, other Gulf energy producers might be forced to halt exports and declare force majeure, stating that 'this will bring down economies of the world.' His warnings came after QatarEnergy announced it was declaring Force Majeure on its contracts with buyers following reported drone attacks on Qatari gas facilities. Al-Kaabi's statements highlighted the critical importance of Gulf energy exports to the global economy and the potential catastrophic consequences of prolonged regional conflicts on worldwide energy security.
achievements: Current Qatar Energy Minister
Made significant statements on global energy security during regional conflicts

history: The research provided does not contain information about the Al Kaabi merchant family from the UAE/GCC region. The only Al Kaabi mentioned is Ahmed Bin Na'aman Al-Kaabi, who served as an emissary for the Omani Sultan Said bin Sultan in 1840, visiting the United States aboard the ship Al-Sultanah. His portrait can be seen in the Peabody Museum in Massachusetts, representing the first Arab diplomatic mission to the New World. However, this individual appears to be connected to Omani rather than UAE Al Kaabi lineage. The research also mentions Ayoub El Kaabi, a Moroccan footballer, but this represents a different Al Kaabi family entirely from North Africa with no connection to the GCC merchant families. Without additional research specifically covering the UAE Al Kaabi merchant family's origins, business activities, and historical development, their actual background cannot be verified from the provided sources.

notableFigures.name: Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair
notableFigures.title: Merchant and Uncle of Juma al Majid
notableFigures.roleDescription: Early Dubai merchant who mentored Juma al Majid
notableFigures.significance: Played a crucial role in launching Juma al Majid's business career by providing him with his first commercial opportunity.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair was Juma al Majid's uncle and an established merchant in Dubai's Deira district. When Juma turned 15 years old, Ahmad asked him to assist at his shop in Deira, providing the young man with his first business experience. Recognizing Juma's potential, Ahmad eventually opened a second small shop for his nephew, which became the foundation for what would later grow into the Juma Al Majid business empire.

Ahmad's mentorship was instrumental in Juma's early commercial development, teaching him the fundamentals of trade and business operations in Dubai's emerging economy. This early guidance and opportunity provided by Ahmad helped establish the business acumen that would later make Juma one of the UAE's most successful entrepreneurs.

notableFigures.name: Mohammad Al Qaz
notableFigures.title: Business Partner and Co-founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Financier, developer, diplomat, and philanthropist
notableFigures.significance: Co-founded the Mohamed & Juma Al Majid company and served as first Deputy Chairman of the UAE Central Bank.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz was born in Al Dhagaya, Deira in 1920 to a modest family. After his father died when he was young, he was educated at Al Ahmadiya School. During World War II, he worked as a carpenter in Dammam and started a commodity trading operation. He became a trader in textiles and commodities between Middle Eastern markets and entered the historic Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade in the 1950s after partnering with Juma Al Majid.

AlGaz rose to political prominence as a trusted advisor to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum and was nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council in 1958. He served as a diplomatic channel between Dubai and Abu Dhabi during land negotiations. After the Indian gold trade declined in the early 1970s, AlGaz and Al Majid invested in franchise rights for international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia. After 11 years of partnership, they amicably split their assets, with AlGaz focusing on real estate development.

Post-federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 into the mid-1990s under Chairman Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was instrumental in establishing educational institutions and co-founded the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair, in 1989 with Juma Al Majid. He died in 2016 at age 96.
achievements: First Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE (1980-1990s)
Co-founded Beit Al Khair Society (1989)
Built first three-story building in Dubai's history
UAE Pioneers Award (2018, posthumous)
President's Merit Award (2012)

notableFigures.biography:
Hana bint Juma Al Majid is a member of the prominent Juma Al Majid merchant family who married into the Al Qasimi ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah. She became the mother of Sheikh Mohammed bin Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, who was born on February 9, 1987, and later became Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah.

Her marriage represents an important alliance between one of the UAE's most successful merchant families and the ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, demonstrating the interconnected nature of prominent Emirati families across the federation.

history: The Juma Al Majid family represents one of the UAE's most successful merchant dynasties, with their modern prominence centered around Juma al Majid (born 1930 in Al Shandagah, Dubai). Born as the son of a pearl diver within the traditional maritime economy of the Trucial States, Juma transformed his family's fortunes through entrepreneurial acumen and strategic business expansion. Starting at age 15 working in his uncle Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair's shop in Deira, he established his own business in 1950, initially selling fabric before pivoting to air conditioners. His partnership with Mohammad Al Qaz, a trader importing goods from Kuwait and Bahrain, proved transformative as they expanded into cross-Gulf trade, taking tobacco and Omani dry lime to Kuwait and Bahrain while returning with Swiss and French fabric and watches. The family's business empire grew systematically through the decades, with Juma securing exclusive distribution agreements for international brands like Yokohama Tires (1958), General Electric appliances, and later Samsung, Hyundai, and Hitachi. By the 1970s, his company had become the second largest distributor of General Electric products, selling up to 700,000 air conditioners annually. The 1971 UAE Federation marked further expansion as he partnered with Al Qaz to open a Pepsi factory in Dubai and diversified into automotive, real estate, construction, hospitality (managing Taj Palace Hotel), and heavy equipment. Beyond commerce, Juma Al Majid established himself as a significant cultural and educational philanthropist, founding the Islamic and Arabic Studies College (1987), the Juma Al Majid Center for Culture and Heritage (1989), and the National Charity Schools (1983), while serving in key economic positions including Chairman of Dubai Economic Council and Vice Chairman of UAE Central Bank.
modernStatus: Major diversified conglomerate through Juma Al Majid Holding Group with interests in automotive, real estate, hospitality, consumer goods, and construction; significant cultural and educational institutions; Juma al Majid ranked among richest Arabs globally in 2016

name: Al Dowsari
originStory: The Al Dowsari family originates from the powerful Dawasir tribal confederation of the Najd region in central Arabia. They migrated to the Gulf coast during the 18th and 19th centuries, establishing themselves as influential merchants in the pearl trade.
description: The Al Dowsari family is a prominent merchant family descended from the influential Dawasir tribe of central Arabia. They established themselves as successful traders and businesspeople across the Gulf region, particularly known for their involvement in the pearl trade and later diversification into modern commercial enterprises.

notableFigures.name: Ahmed Al Dowsari
notableFigures.title: Merchant

notableFigures.name: Abdulla Al Dowsari

history: Based on the research, one member of the Al Dowsari family, Abdulla Al Dowsari, was identified as an individual alleged to have committed or overseen torture during the repressive period in Bahrain between 1974 and 1999 under the State Security Act. This places at least one branch of the Al Dowsari family in positions within Bahrain's security apparatus during this era. The research confirms the family's connection to the broader Dawasir tribal confederation, which has deep historical roots in the Arabian Peninsula. The Dawasir tribe, from which the Al Dowsari family descends, originated in the Najd region of central Arabia, specifically in Wadi Al-Dawasir, and became one of the most powerful and influential Arab tribes. The tribe's migrations included movements to Bahrain, where they settled mainly in Zallaq and Budaiya, and were heavily involved in the pearl industry. The Dawasir were described as the 'second largest and most powerful tribe after the Utub in Bahrain' and were so influential that they 'recognized Sheikh Isa Al Khalifa as ruler in name only and considered themselves immune from taxation.' The tribe's opposition to British influence led to their mass exodus from Bahrain to Dammam, Saudi Arabia in 1923, where they founded the cities of Dammam and Khobar. They were officially allowed to return to Bahrain in April 1927.
modernStatus: Established merchant family with historical involvement in Gulf political and security institutions

name: Al Muhadib
originStory: The Al Muhadib family originated from the merchant communities of central Arabia's Najd region. They were known for their expertise in desert trade routes and pearl commerce.
description: The Al Muhadib family is a traditional merchant family with roots in Najdi trading communities. They have maintained their commercial heritage across generations, adapting from traditional pearl and livestock trading to modern business enterprises.

history: The Al Muhadib family originated from the merchant communities of central Arabia's Najd region, where they established themselves as specialists in desert trade routes and pearl commerce. As traditional Najdi merchants, they built their reputation through expertise in navigating the complex trading networks that connected the Arabian Peninsula's interior with coastal markets. The family maintained their commercial heritage across generations, successfully adapting from traditional pearl and livestock trading to modern business enterprises while preserving their merchant identity rooted in the Najdi trading communities.
modernStatus: The family maintains trading businesses across the GCC region, with interests in retail, real estate, and import-export operations. They are active in local chambers of commerce and merchant associations.

name: Al Rajhi
originStory: The Al Rajhi family originated from the Aniza tribe in Al-Bukayriyah, Al-Qassim Province, where they began as currency exchangers and traders serving Hajj pilgrims. Their reputation for honesty and adherence to Islamic principles laid the foundation for what would become a global banking empire.
description: The Al Rajhi family is Saudi Arabia's most prominent banking dynasty, having transformed from Najdi desert traders into the founders of the world's largest Islamic bank. Their business empire, built on strict Islamic principles, has made them one of the wealthiest families in the Middle East.

notableFigures.biography: Mohammed Al Rajhi was one of the four brothers who founded Al-Rajhi Bank in 1957, working alongside his brothers Saleh, Sulaiman, and Abdullah to establish what would become the world's largest Islamic banking institution. The bank initially began as a group of banking and commercial operations which, in 1978, joined together under the umbrella of the Alrajhi Trading and Exchange Company. The company changed to a joint stock company in 1987, and after two years was rebranded as the Alrajhi Banking and Investment Corporation, before becoming Alrajhi Bank in 2006.

notableFigures.biography: Abdullah bin Sulaiman Alrajhi currently serves as the Chairman of the Board of Directors of Al-Rajhi Bank, one of the four Alrajhi family members on the eleven-member board. He has been involved in defending the bank against various allegations following the September 11 attacks, with U.S. courts ultimately dismissing all claims against him and the bank in 2014. The U.S. Supreme Court issued an order on June 30, 2014, leaving intact the dismissal with prejudice of all claims against the bank and its officers, including Abdullah bin Suleiman Alrajhi, related to the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001.

notableFigures.title: Minister of Labor, Saudi Arabia
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi government minister and businessman
notableFigures.significance: Appointed as Saudi Arabia's Labor Minister in 2018, representing growing private sector expertise in government.
notableFigures.biography: Ahmed al-Rajhi is a businessman who was appointed as Saudi Arabia's Labor Minister in June 2018 by King Salman as part of government reforms. His appointment signaled a growing role for private sector expertise in the Saudi government, reflecting the kingdom's efforts to diversify its economy and implement Vision 2030 reforms. The appointment was part of broader government changes that also included the establishment of the Ministry of Culture and the Council of Royal Reserves.

Represents private sector expertise in Saudi government

history: The Al Rajhi family traces their origins to the Aniza tribe in Al-Bukayriyah, Al-Qassim Province, where they began as currency exchangers and traders serving Hajj pilgrims. The family's transformation from Najdi desert traders to global banking dynasty began with Sheikh Saleh Abdul Aziz Al Rajhi (1921-2011), who started working odd jobs in local marketplaces in the early 1930s, initially as a porter before establishing a bastah (small trading place) in 1937 in front of the Grand Mosque in Riyadh, changing money for a 5% commission. By 1947, Saleh opened a formal currency shop in Riyadh and brought his brothers Mohammed and Sulaiman into the business, with Sulaiman later expanding operations to Mecca and Jeddah to serve pilgrims and businesspeople. The brothers formally established the beginnings of Al-Rajhi Bank in 1957, connecting to international banking networks across Europe, Asia, and the United States by the 1960s. In 1983, they won permission to open Saudi Arabia's first Islamic bank, capitalizing on the flood of migrant workers during the 1970s oil boom who needed to send earnings home to places like India and Pakistan. The family's business empire expanded beyond banking into agriculture, real estate, construction, and other industrial sectors. Sheikh Sulaiman bin Abdulaziz Al Rajhi became chairman of what is recognized as the Tadawul's most venerable institution, with his wealth estimated at $7.7 billion by Forbes in 2011, making him the 120th richest person in the world. The family continues as Al Rajhi Bank's majority shareholders and remains among Saudi Arabia's wealthiest non-royals and leading philanthropists, with Sulaiman establishing Sulaiman Al Rajhi University and announcing in 2011 his intention to donate most of his fortune to charity.
modernStatus: Majority shareholders of Al Rajhi Bank, the world's largest Islamic bank, with diversified investments across multiple sectors including agriculture, real estate, construction, and industrial ventures. The family continues as Saudi Arabia's most prominent banking dynasty.

name: Al Olayan
originStory: The Al Olayans originated as merchants in the Najdi town of Unayzah in the Qassim region during the late 18th century. They built their early wealth through trade in dates, livestock, and serving as intermediaries in trans-Arabian commerce.
description: The Al Olayan family built one of Saudi Arabia's largest business conglomerates, the Olayan Group, transforming from Najdi traders into international business leaders. Founded by Sulaiman Al Olayan in 1947, the family empire spans distribution, manufacturing, services, and investments across multiple continents.

notableFigures.name: Sulaiman Saleh Al Olayan
notableFigures.title: Founder and Chairman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded Olayan Group and built it into major Saudi business conglomerate

notableFigures.name: Suliman S. Olayan
notableFigures.title: Founder of The Olayan Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Entrepreneur and founder of multinational business empire
notableFigures.significance: Founded The Olayan Group in 1947, which became a major multinational enterprise with operations worth over $10 billion.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan was a Saudi entrepreneur who founded The Olayan Group in 1947, establishing what would become one of the region's most successful business empires. The group grew into a private multinational enterprise engaged in distribution, manufacturing, services and investments, with operations spanning the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Middle East. Under his leadership, the company developed partnerships with leading multinationals and became one of the largest investors in Saudi and regional stock markets.

Olayan's business acumen and vision transformed the company from its modest beginnings into a diversified conglomerate. By the time of his death, the private family fortune was estimated to top $10 billion. His legacy continued through his children, particularly his daughter Lubna, who became CEO of Olayan Financing Company. The Olayan Group's influence extended beyond business, with significant investments in education, infrastructure, and various sectors across the Middle East.
achievements: Founded The Olayan Group in 1947
Built multinational enterprise worth over $10 billion
Established partnerships with leading international companies
Created one of the largest investment portfolios in Saudi Arabia and the region

notableFigures.name: Lubna Suliman al-Olayan
notableFigures.title: Former CEO of Olayan Financing Company
notableFigures.roleDescription: Prominent Saudi businesswoman and banking executive
notableFigures.significance: First Saudi woman to head a bank and former CEO of Olayan Financing Company, one of the most powerful women in global business.
notableFigures.biography:
Lubna Suliman al-Olayan was born on August 4, 1955, in Khobar, in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, as the youngest child of Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan, founder of The Olayan Group. She obtained a Bachelor of Science in Agriculture from Cornell University and a Master's degree in Business Administration from Indiana University. After working at Morgan Guaranty Trust Bank in New York City from 1979 to 1981, she returned to Saudi Arabia to join the family business.

She served as CEO of Olayan Financing Company (OFC) until May 2019, overseeing the holding entity for The Olayan Group's operations in Saudi Arabia and the Middle East. Under her leadership, OFC operated or participated in more than 40 companies and became one of the largest investors in Saudi and regional stock markets. In June 2019, she made history by becoming the first Saudi woman to head a bank when she was named Chairwoman of the Saudi British Bank (SABB), a position she has held through multiple re-elections.

Beyond her corporate role, Lubna has served on numerous prestigious boards including WPP, Citigroup's International Advisory Board, and Rolls-Royce. She has been a trustee of Cornell University, a board member of INSEAD, and serves on the Board of Trustees of the World Economic Forum. Forbes has consistently ranked her among the world's most powerful women, listing her as the 86th most powerful woman globally as of 2014.
achievements: First Saudi woman to head a bank (SABB)
Former CEO of Olayan Financing Company
Served on boards of major international companies including WPP, Citigroup, and Rolls-Royce
Ranked among Forbes' most powerful women globally
Cornell Entrepreneur of the Year (2010)
Member of MIT Corporation and various prestigious international boards

notableFigures.name: Khaled Olayan
notableFigures.title: Board Member of The Olayan Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Family member and board member of The Olayan Group
notableFigures.significance: Serves on the board of The Olayan Group alongside his sisters, continuing the family's business legacy.
notableFigures.biography:
Khaled Olayan is the son of Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan, founder of The Olayan Group, and brother to Lubna Olayan. He serves on the board of The Olayan Group along with his sisters Lubna, Hayat, and Hutham, representing the second generation of family leadership in the multinational business empire.

As a board member, Khaled plays a role in overseeing the group's diverse operations across distribution, manufacturing, services, and investments. The Olayan family's collective involvement in the business has helped maintain the company's position as one of the region's most successful private enterprises, with the family fortune estimated to exceed $10 billion.
achievements: Board member of The Olayan Group
Part of second generation leadership of major multinational enterprise

Hayat Olayan is the daughter of Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan, founder of The Olayan Group, and sister to prominent businesswoman Lubna Olayan. She serves on the board of The Olayan Group alongside her siblings Lubna, Khaled, and Hutham, representing the family's continued involvement in the leadership of their multinational business empire.

As a board member, Hayat contributes to the strategic oversight of the group's extensive operations across various sectors including distribution, manufacturing, services, and investments. The family's collective stewardship has maintained the company's position as one of the most successful private enterprises in the region.

Hutham Olayan is the daughter of Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan, founder of The Olayan Group, and sister to renowned businesswoman Lubna Olayan. She serves on the board of The Olayan Group along with her siblings Lubna, Khaled, and Hayat, representing the second generation of family leadership in the business.

As a board member, Hutham participates in the governance and strategic direction of the group's diverse portfolio of companies and investments. The family's collective involvement has been instrumental in maintaining the Olayan Group's status as one of the region's premier private multinational enterprises, with operations spanning multiple sectors and countries.

history: The Al Olayan family originated as merchants in the Najdi town of Unayzah in the Qassim region during the late 18th century, building their early wealth through trade in dates, livestock, and serving as intermediaries in trans-Arabian commerce. The family's transformation into a modern business empire began with Sulaiman Al Olayan, who founded the Olayan Group in 1947. The research reveals that Lubna Olayan, born in Khobar in the Eastern Province in 1955, represents the family's evolution into international business leadership. As the youngest child of Sheikh Sulaiman Al Olayan and former CEO of Olayan Financing Company until 2019, she obtained education from Cornell University and Indiana University before working at Morgan Guaranty Trust Bank in New York City from 1979 to 1981. The family's business empire spans distribution, manufacturing, services, and investments across multiple continents, with operations in more than 40 companies and significant investments in Saudi and regional stock markets. Lubna Olayan made history in 2019 as the first Saudi woman to head a bank when she became Chairwoman of the Saudi British Bank (SABB), a position she continues to hold. The family fortune is estimated to exceed $10 billion, with the Olayan Group being recognized as one of Saudi Arabia's largest business conglomerates and a major player in the kingdom's private sector alongside other major Saudi firms.
modernStatus: One of Saudi Arabia's wealthiest business families with an estimated fortune exceeding $10 billion, controlling the Olayan Group which operates in more than 40 companies across multiple continents. Family members hold prominent positions including Lubna Olayan as Chairwoman of Saudi British Bank.

name: Bin Laden
originStory: The family originated from the Kindah tribe in Yemen's Hadhramaut region, rising to prominence when Mohammed bin Laden migrated to Saudi Arabia in the 1920s and established a construction empire.
description: A prominent Saudi Arabian family of Yemeni origin that built one of the Middle East's largest construction empires through close ties with the Saudi royal family. The family's reputation was significantly impacted by Osama bin Laden's terrorist activities, though their business operations continue.

notableFigures.title: Founder, Saudi Binladin Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Family patriarch who built construction empire and established close ties with Saudi royal family

notableFigures.name: Osama bin Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Founder of Al-Qaeda
notableFigures.roleDescription: Terrorist leader who orchestrated September 11 attacks, estranged from main family business

notableFigures.name: Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Founder and Patriarch

notableFigures.name: Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Founder and Patriarch
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of the bin Laden family fortune and construction empire
notableFigures.significance: Founded what is today the Saudi Binladin Group and became the wealthiest non-royal Saudi, establishing the wealth and prestige of the bin Laden family.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden was born around 1908 on the Hadramawt coast of south Yemen to Awad, a Kindite Hadrami tribesman from al-Rubat, a village in Wadi Doan. Poor and uneducated, he emigrated to Saudi Arabia before World War I, initially working as a porter in Jeddah. In 1931, he started his own construction business and came to the attention of Abdul Aziz Ibn Saud through construction projects. He made his initial fortune from exclusive rights to construct all mosques and other religious buildings not only in Saudi Arabia, but as far as Ibn Saud's influence reached.

Muhammad strategically built strong ties with the Saudi royal family, notably constructing a ramp at the palace in Jeddah to help the aging king move between floors. In 1964, King Faisal issued a royal decree awarding all future construction projects to Muhammad's company, helping the company amass assets exceeding $5 billion. He fathered 54 children by 22 wives, following Islamic polygyny principles. On September 3, 1967, Muhammad was killed when his airplane crashed during landing in Usran, 'Asir Province. His funeral drew nearly 10,000 people, and King Faisal placed his children under royal protection.
achievements: Founded the Saudi Binladin Group construction empire
Secured exclusive rights to construct all mosques in Saudi Arabia
Built strong ties with Saudi royal family through major construction projects
Amassed company assets exceeding $5 billion

notableFigures.name: Osama bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Terrorist Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder and leader of al-Qaeda terrorist organization
notableFigures.significance: Co-founded the terrorism group Al-Qaeda and was responsible for attacks including the September 11 attacks.
notableFigures.biography:
Osama bin Laden was born in 1957 in Saudi Arabia, the 17th child of Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden and the only son of his tenth wife, Hamida al-Attas, who was of Syrian origin. His parents divorced soon after his birth, and his mother was given in marriage to one of his father's company executives around 1958. Osama was raised in the household of his stepfather Muhammad al-Attas in Jeddah. In 1994, the bin Laden family publicly disowned him, and the Saudi government revoked his passport and citizenship for speaking out against the government for permitting U.S. troops to be based in Saudi Arabia.

Osama founded al-Qaeda and became responsible for numerous terrorist attacks, most notably the September 11, 2001 attacks on the United States. He lived in exile in Sudan and Afghanistan before ultimately hiding in a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan. He was killed on May 2, 2011, during a U.S. Navy SEAL operation at his compound. He had multiple wives and over 20 children, and was one of the FBI's Most Wanted Terrorists until his death.

notableFigures.name: Omar bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Artist and Cultural Ambassador
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi artist, author, and businessman who publicly disavowed his father's actions
notableFigures.significance: The only son of Osama bin Laden to publicly disavow his father's actions and became an advocate for peace.
notableFigures.biography:
Omar bin Laden was born on March 1, 1981, as the fourth-eldest son of Osama bin Laden and his first wife Najwa Ghanhem. He accompanied his father during exile to Sudan and then to Afghanistan, and trained in al-Qaeda camps beginning at age 14. After six years of training and sharing a house with al-Qaeda's second-in-command Ayman al-Zawahiri, Omar left al-Qaeda in 2000 because he disagreed with killing civilians, with his father not objecting to his departure.

Omar returned to Saudi Arabia and ran his own company in Jeddah as a contractor and scrap metal dealer. In 2006, he married Jane Felix-Browne (Zaina Mohamed Al-Sabah), a British parish councillor 24 years his senior. He has been denied visas to the UK and Europe, and has sought asylum in various countries. Omar co-authored the book 'Growing Up bin Laden' with his mother in 2009, detailing their harsh life under Osama's control. He has consistently advocated for peace and condemned his father's terrorist activities while expressing personal love for him as his father.
achievements: Co-authored 'Growing Up bin Laden' memoir
Became advocate for peace between Muslims and the West
Only son of Osama bin Laden to publicly disavow father's actions

notableFigures.name: Hamza bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Al-Qaeda Member
notableFigures.roleDescription: Key member of al-Qaeda and son of Osama bin Laden
notableFigures.significance: Was considered a key leader of Al-Qaeda and was being groomed as his father's potential successor before being killed in a U.S. counterterrorism operation.
notableFigures.biography:
Hamza bin Laden was born on May 9, 1989, in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, the son of Osama bin Laden and Khairiah Sabir. In January 2001, he attended his brother Mohammed's wedding in Kandahar, Afghanistan, and video footage from November 2001 shows 12-year-old Hamza handling U.S. helicopter wreckage alongside the Taliban. From March 2002 to July or August 2010, Hamza was detained in Iran under prison-like conditions during his adolescence, from age 12 to 21.

After his release, Hamza moved to Qatar for several years to study Islamic religious texts as his father had recommended, then returned to Pakistan around 2015. Between 2015 and 2017, he released audio and video messages calling for attacks against Western targets. The U.S. classified him as a Specially Designated Global Terrorist in January 2017 and offered a $1 million reward for information on his whereabouts in February 2019. Saudi Arabia revoked his citizenship in March 2019. On September 14, 2019, President Donald Trump confirmed that Hamza had been killed in a U.S. counterterrorism operation in the Afghanistan-Pakistan region, though some 2024 reports claim he may still be alive.

notableFigures.name: Yeslam bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Business Executive
notableFigures.roleDescription: Geneva-based head of family's European operations
notableFigures.significance: Head of the family's European holding company and became a Swiss citizen, representing the international expansion of bin Laden business interests.
notableFigures.biography:
Yeslam bin Laden was born in 1950 and studied in the 1970s at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles. He later settled in Switzerland and became a Swiss citizen in 2001. Yeslam serves as the Geneva-based head of the family's European holding company, the Saudi Investment Company, representing the bin Laden family's international business interests beyond the Middle East.

Yeslam was scrutinized by Swiss and American investigators because of a financial stake he held in a Swiss aviation firm. He has claimed to not have had contact with his half-brother Osama since 1981, distancing himself from Osama's terrorist activities. Yeslam married Carmen Dufour and has three daughters: Wafah Dufour (born 1975), who became an American model and aspiring singer-songwriter, Najia Dufour (born 1981), and Noor Dufour (born 1987). The family lived in Geneva, and after Yeslam and Carmen separated in 1988, the children remained primarily in Switzerland.
achievements: Established European operations for bin Laden business interests
Obtained Swiss citizenship while maintaining family business ties
Studied at University of Southern California

notableFigures.name: Abdullah bin Osama bin Laden
notableFigures.title: Businessman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Eldest son of Osama bin Laden who runs advertising firm in Jeddah
notableFigures.significance: The eldest son of Osama bin Laden who completely broke with his father and established his own business in Saudi Arabia.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah bin Osama bin Laden was born in 1976 as the eldest son of Osama bin Laden and Najwa Ghanem. Although his father initially showed him favor, taking him to Afghanistan in 1984 when he was only eight, Abdullah later became disgusted with life in Sudan. He moved back to Jeddah to marry his first cousin and refused to rejoin his father in Sudan, except for a brief, unpleasant visit. Abdullah never saw or communicated with his father again after 1995.

Abdullah now resides in Jeddah where he runs his own firm called Fame Advertising. He has been closely watched by the Saudi government, which restricted his travel from the kingdom after 1996, at least for a time. He has stated that the media have misrepresented his father and is known to dine occasionally with his uncle, Saudi Binladin Group chairman Bakr bin Laden, at the Intercontinental Hotel in Jeddah. In 2015, WikiLeaks revealed that Abdullah had requested his father's death certificate from the U.S. embassy in Saudi Arabia, but was told that no death certificate was issued for Osama.
achievements: Founded Fame Advertising company in Jeddah
Successfully distanced himself from father's terrorist activities
Maintained business operations despite family scrutiny

history: The Bin Laden family traces its origins to the Kindah tribe in Yemen's Hadhramaut region, specifically from the village of al-Rubat in Wadi Doan of the Tarim Valley. The family patriarch, Awad bin Laden, was a Kindite Hadrami tribesman whose son Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden (1908-1967) emigrated from Hadramawt to Saudi Arabia before World War I. Mohammed initially worked as a porter in Jeddah, then as a bricklayer with Aramco, before establishing his own construction business in 1931. He strategically built close relationships with the Saudi royal family, particularly by constructing a ramp at King Abdulaziz's Jeddah palace to help the aging monarch move between floors. His remarkable success came through shrewd business sense, reliability, and willingness to offer the lowest bids on construction contracts. By the 1940s, he had become a multi-millionaire, securing major projects including a palace for future King Saud in 1948 and the crucial Jeddah-Medina road in the early 1950s. King Faisal issued a royal decree awarding Mohammed exclusive rights to all future construction projects, leading to assets exceeding $5 billion. The company held exclusive rights to mosque construction across Saudi Arabia and several Arab countries, including restoration work at Jerusalem's Al-Aqsa Mosque until 1967. Mohammed married 22 times and fathered 54 children, including his 17th child, Osama bin Laden, born to his Syrian wife Hamida al-Attas. After Mohammed's death in a 1967 plane crash, his eldest son Salem took over until his own death in 1988, followed by Bakr bin Laden. The family was publicly disowned Osama in 1994 due to his terrorist activities, though their construction empire, now the Saudi Binladin Group, continues as one of the Middle East's largest firms. In 2018, following Bakr's detention in Saudi Arabia's corruption crackdown, the family transferred their stake to the government, effectively nationalizing the company.
modernStatus: The Saudi Binladin Group was effectively nationalized in 2018 when the government acquired majority control following the family's detention in the corruption crackdown, though it remains one of the region's largest construction conglomerates

originStory: The Al Dabbagh family originated in the Hejaz region of the Arabian Peninsula, where they established themselves as skilled leather tanners and traders. Their name derives from the Arabic 'dabbagh' meaning tanner, reflecting their ancestral profession.

history: The Al Dabbagh family has established itself as a prominent Saudi merchant family with significant business and government influence. The family's modern prominence began with Abdullah Mohammed Ali Al-Dabbagh, who served as Minister of Agriculture and founded the Al-Dabbagh Group (ADG) in 1962. His son Amr Al-Dabbagh, born in 1966, expanded the family's influence by heading the Saudi Arabian General Investment Authority (SAGIA) from 2004 to 2012 and serving as chairman and CEO of the family conglomerate. Amr founded the Jeddah Economic Forum think tank, served on the Jeddah Chamber of Commerce & Industry board, and created international philanthropic initiatives including Philanthropy University in 2015 and the UK-based Stars Foundation (2001-2020). The family faced challenges during the 2017 anti-corruption crackdown when Amr was detained at the Ritz Carlton in Riyadh alongside other prominent Saudi businessmen and princes, though he was released without charges in January 2019. The Al-Dabbagh Group continues to operate as a significant family conglomerate in Saudi Arabia.
modernStatus: Major Saudi business conglomerate through Al-Dabbagh Group, with continued operations following the 2017-2019 detention period during the anti-corruption crackdown

notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad Hamad Al Gosaibi was one of the three sons of Hamad Ahmad Al Gosaibi who came together to establish 'Ahmad Hamad Algosaibi and Bros.' (AHAB). Along with his brothers Abdulaziz and Sulaiman, he expanded the company's operations significantly from the original trading business to include diverse sectors. The company grew to encompass Pepsi Cola bottling franchises, oilfield services for Saudi Aramco, real estate, hospitality, food & beverages, and investments in banking, insurance, shipping, trading, finance, and manufacturing.

Ahmad died in 1991, and his equity in the general partnership was transferred to his sons Yousef Ahmad Al Gosaibi, Abdulmohsin Ahmad Al Gosaibi, Khalid Ahmad Al Gosaibi, and three of his daughters. Each of the three founding brothers owned one third of AHAB, making the Al Gosaibi Group a significant family-owned business empire in the Middle East.

Developed major Pepsi Cola bottling franchises

Built diverse business portfolio across multiple sectors

notableFigures.biography:
Abdulaziz Hamad Al Gosaibi was one of the three sons of Hamad Ahmad Al Gosaibi who came together to establish 'Ahmad Hamad Algosaibi and Bros.' (AHAB). Along with his brothers Ahmad and Sulaiman, he played a key role in expanding the company's operations from the original trading business established by their father in the late 1940s. The company grew to include diverse sectors such as Pepsi Cola bottling franchises, oilfield services for Saudi Aramco, real estate, hospitality, food & beverages, and investments in banking, insurance, shipping, trading, finance, and manufacturing.

Abdulaziz died in 2003, and his equity in the general partnership was transferred to his wife, son Saud, and his six daughters. The three founding brothers each owned one third of AHAB, establishing the Al Gosaibi Group as one of the most respected business houses in the Middle East with diverse corporate partnerships and manufacturing operations throughout the region.

Developed business operations across multiple sectors

Built manufacturing operations throughout the Middle East

history: The Al Gosaibi family originated from the Al Ahsa oasis in eastern Arabia during the 18th century, initially engaged in pearl diving and trading. Based on the research, they are of Najdi origin and belong to the Banu Abdul Qais tribal confederation. The family established major commercial operations across the Gulf region, particularly in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia. Hamad Ahmad Al Gosaibi founded the family business in the 1940s, initially in trade and banking sectors. His sons Ahmad, Abdulaziz, and Sulaiman built this into a diversified business empire known as Ahmad Hamad Al Gosaibi & Brothers Group of Companies, which became one of Saudi Arabia's most formidable merchant empires. The family produced notable figures including Ghazi Abdul Rahman Al Gosaibi, a prominent Saudi politician, diplomat, technocrat, poet, and novelist who served in multiple ministerial positions including Industry and Electricity (1975-1983), Health (1983-1984), and Labor (2004-2010). He was also instrumental in founding SABIC and served as Saudi ambassador to Bahrain and the United Kingdom. The business empire was managed by the next generation including Yousef Ahmad Al Gosaibi as Group President and Saud Abdul Aziz Al Gosaibi as Group Managing Director, though the family faced significant challenges in recent decades.
modernStatus: The family business Ahmad Hamad Al Gosaibi & Brothers Group was once considered among Saudi Arabia's most formidable merchant empires, though it has faced significant challenges in recent decades. The patriarch Sulaiman Hamad Al Gosaibi died in 2009, and management has been delegated to nephews and family members.

notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah Al-Hamad Al Zamil was originally from Onaiza, Central Arabia, and established a food and textile trading entity in Bahrain in the 1930s. He later expanded his business interests into real estate investment. He was the father of twelve sons, including Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Zamil who would later become a prominent industrialist and government minister.

Upon his death in 1961, the family chose to continue operation of his business as a group under the leadership of his eldest son, Mohammed. This decision laid the foundation for what would become the Zamil Group Holding Company, which now employs more than 12,000 people in 60 countries and is involved in diverse sectors including general construction, paints, plastics, petrochemicals, shipbuilding, and port management.

He completed his final year of high school in 1959 in Kuwait together with his brother Abdulaziz. As part of the Zamil Group's expansion into petrochemicals after 1995, he worked alongside his brother Abdulaziz to establish the Saudi International Petrochemical Company (Sipchem), where Abdulaziz served as Chairman.

Upon his father's death in 1961, the family chose to continue operation of the business as a group under Mohammed's leadership. history: The provided research focuses extensively on the Houthi movement of Yemen, which is completely unrelated to the Al Zamil family of Saudi Arabia. The research contains detailed information about Houthi political activities, military operations, and governance, but provides no additional information about the Al Zamil family's history, origins, or business development beyond what is already in the existing data. The research also includes information about Zamil Group Holding as a major Saudi industrial conglomerate founded in 1920, and mentions Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Zamil (1942-2019) as an important industrial engineer who served as CEO of SABIC and Minister of Industry and Electricity. However, the research does not provide any new information about the family's tribal origins, migration patterns, or early settlement history that would supplement the existing data about their Anizah tribal heritage and 18th century settlement in Al-Ahsa.
modernStatus: The family operates Zamil Group Holding, one of Saudi Arabia's largest investment holdings with publicly listed subsidiaries including Zamil Steel Company, Sipchem, and Zamil Industrial, employing over 12,000 people in 60 countries across diverse sectors including construction, petrochemicals, and shipbuilding.

notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah Al Turki served as secretary-general of the World Muslim League and was a member of the Council of Senior Ulema. He was directly challenged by Prince Turki bin Faisal Al Saud after the 9/11 attacks regarding the proper role of religious scholars versus rulers in matters of state.

Prince Turki bin Faisal argued against Sheikh Al Turki's position, stating that 'those responsible for affairs of state are the rulers,' whereas religious scholars 'only act in an advisory capacity.' This public disagreement highlighted tensions within Saudi religious and political establishments following the September 11 attacks.

history: Based on the research, the Al Turki name appears across multiple distinct lineages in the Gulf and Saudi context, with no clear evidence of a unified merchant family origin. The research reveals several prominent Al Turki figures: Prince Turki Al-Faisal, a senior Saudi royal who served as intelligence chief and diplomat; Turki Al-Sheikh, a government official and entertainment authority chairman; Prince Turki bin Salman, a businessman and media company chairman; and Homaidan Al-Turki, a Saudi national convicted in the US. However, these individuals appear to represent different family lines rather than a cohesive merchant dynasty. The research does not provide evidence of a specific Al Turki merchant family with origins in Najd that migrated to establish trading operations, as suggested in the existing data. The documented Al Turki figures are primarily associated with the Saudi royal family, government positions, or individual cases, rather than representing a traditional Gulf merchant family lineage with documented business operations in pearling, trade, or other commercial ventures.
modernStatus: Various individuals bearing the Al Turki name hold positions in Saudi government, entertainment industry, and business, but no unified family business structure is documented

history: The Al Muhaidib family originated from the Tamim tribe and settled in Buraidah, Qassim during the 18th century, establishing themselves as successful merchants specializing in date trading and livestock commerce. The modern business empire was founded in 1946 by Abdul Kadir bin Abdul Muhsin Al-Muhaidib under the name Abdulatif and Abdulkadir Al-Muhaidib Company, initially focusing on wholesale building materials and foodstuffs. The company underwent several name changes - becoming Al-Muhaidib and Al-Nafea Company in 1959, then A.K Al-Muhaidib & Sons Group of Companies in 1979. The current chairman is Sulaiman A. K. Al-Muhaidib. The group expanded significantly into food distribution starting with rice marketing in 1959, eventually establishing the Giant Stores retail chain (later merged with Panda) and becoming one of the top 15 suppliers to Saudi retailers through Mayar Foods. Their building materials division grew to serve Saudi Arabia's economic and industrial development, with companies like Masdar Technical Supplies and Masdar Building Materials operating over 70 branches with more than 1 million square meters of storage capacity. The family's business interests now span multiple sectors including financial services (SABB bank, Gulf Union insurance), real estate development (Al Oula Development, Rafal Real Estate), contracting (Thabat with over 14,000 employees), and energy (ACWA Power). A member of the Al Muhaidib family, Ibrahim al-Muhaidib, served as president of Al-Nassr Football Club until his resignation in 2024.
modernStatus: Major diversified business conglomerate with operations across food retail, building materials, financial services, real estate, contracting, and energy sectors throughout Saudi Arabia and the Gulf region

history: The Al Subeaei family has established itself as a prominent Saudi merchant dynasty with documented tribal origins in the Subay' (Subaie') tribe of southern Najd. The research indicates the broader Subay' tribe is of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock, tracing lineage to the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation that dominated central Arabia in medieval times. The Al Subeaei family achieved significant prominence in modern Saudi business, with brothers Mohammed and Abdullah Ibrahim Al Subeaei becoming significant stakeholders in Bank Albilad, an Islamic Saudi bank established in 2004. Their influence extends to major investment firms, as Mohammed and Abdullah Ibrahim Al Subeaei Company are founding partners of Jadwa Investment, Saudi Arabia's premier investment management firm with over SAR 70 billion in assets under management. The family has also made substantial investments in real estate development, as evidenced by the Al Subeaei Tower Complex in Khobar, completed in 2004 at a cost of $22.4 million. Nasser Mohammed Al-Subeaei currently serves as chairman of the board of directors of Bank Albilad as of February 2021, demonstrating the family's continued leadership in the Saudi financial sector.

history: The research reveals multiple distinct individuals with the Al Mana name but no clear connection to the existing Qatar-based merchant Al Mana family data. Aisha al-Mana emerges as a prominent Saudi activist and businesswoman born in 1948 in Khobar, working as director of Al-Mana General Hospitals and board member of Ebrahim M. Almana and Brothers. She became one of the first Saudi women to earn a PhD (1982, University of Colorado Boulder), established the first all-women company in Saudi Arabia (Al-Khalijiah Development Company, 1985), and became the first female hospital director in the kingdom (1990). She participated in three driving ban protest campaigns (1990, 2011, 2013) and established educational foundations supporting Saudi women's health education. The research also identifies several Al Hajri family members connected to Qatar's ruling family, including Mana bin Abdul Hadi Al Hajri (former Qatari ambassador to Jordan) and his children Al-Anoud (second wife of Emir Tamim) and Abdulhadi (businessman who purchased London's Ritz Hotel in 2020). However, no direct genealogical connection is established between these various Mana-named individuals and the original Qatari Tamim-descended Al Mana merchant family.
modernStatus: Research shows Al Mana involvement in Saudi healthcare/business (Aisha al-Mana with Al-Mana General Hospitals) and separate Al Hajri family prominence in Qatari diplomacy and international business, but connections unclear

notableFigures.biography:
Ahmed Al Fardan is a Bahraini former rally driver who competed in national and regional rallying championships across the Middle East. He was a regular participant in the Bahrain National Rally Championship and events organized under the Bahrain Motor Federation (BMF). His active racing years coincided with the growth of international motorsport across the Middle East, where he participated in regional events such as the Bahrain International Rally and other rounds of the FIA Middle East Rally Championship.

Following his retirement from active competition, Al Fardan transitioned into a managerial and advisory role, becoming instrumental in managing the career of his son, Hamad Al Fardan, guiding him through various international open-wheel categories including Formula BMW Asia, British Formula 3, and the GP2 Asia Series. He was also known for his involvement with Gulf Finance House (GFH) racing initiatives, which sponsored Bahraini drivers on the international stage to bridge the gap between regional racing and global platforms like Formula 1.
achievements: Competed in Bahrain National Rally Championship
Participated in FIA Middle East Rally Championship
Managed son Hamad's international racing career
Involved with Gulf Finance House racing initiatives

notableFigures.name: Hamad Al Fardan
notableFigures.title: Racing Driver
notableFigures.roleDescription: First Bahraini driver to compete at GP2 Series level
notableFigures.significance: First Bahraini racing driver to compete at GP2 Series level, pioneering Bahraini participation in international motorsport.
notableFigures.biography:
Hamad Al Fardan was born on June 29, 1987, in Manama, Bahrain, as the son of rally driver Ahmed Al Fardan. He began his formula racing career in 2004 with the Formula BMW Asia series, where he achieved significant success. In 2005, he completed a full season with the Malaysian team Meritus and finished third overall, also competing in the Formula BMW World Final. His career progressed through various international series including Formula Renault, Toyota Racing Series in New Zealand, and British Formula 3.

Al Fardan made history in 2008 when he signed with iSport International to compete in the GP2 Asia Series, becoming the first Bahraini and Arabian driver to race at this level. His participation was backed by Gulf Finance House, which led to the team being renamed GFH Team iSport for the duration of the championship. Throughout his career, he competed across multiple continents, from Asia to Europe to New Zealand, winning races in Formula BMW Asia, Formula Asia V6 Renault, and the New Zealand Grand Prix. His achievements helped establish Bahrain's presence in international motorsport before the country hosted its first Formula 1 Grand Prix in 2004.

Formula Asia V6 Renault champion (2009)

history: Based on the Wikipedia research on Dana Al Fardan, the Al Fardan family emerges as a prominent Qatari business dynasty with deep roots in the Gulf merchant tradition. Dana Al Fardan, born in 1985 as the daughter of Ali Al Fardan and Shafiqa Habib, represents the contemporary face of this merchant family. The family operates the Alfardan Group, which was created in 1954 by Dana's grandfather Ibrahim Alfardan, establishing them as a major business conglomerate in Qatar. Dana herself initially worked in the family business after obtaining degrees in international relations and gemology, before transitioning to become Qatar's first female contemporary composer and cultural pioneer. The family's business empire spans multiple sectors, and their cultural influence is demonstrated through Dana's groundbreaking work as a composer, her role as cultural ambassador for the Qatar Philharmonic Orchestra, and her establishment of DNA Records in 2015 as Qatar's first record label. Her philanthropic work with organizations like the Qatar Red Crescent Society and UNRWA shows the family's continued engagement in regional affairs and humanitarian causes.

notableFigures.name: Moza bint Nasser Al-Missned
notableFigures.title: Sheikha and Chair of Qatar Foundation
notableFigures.roleDescription: Co-founder and chair of the Qatar Foundation, consort of former Emir Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
notableFigures.significance: Influential figure in Qatar's education and social development, mother of current Emir Sheikh Tamim, and major force behind Qatar's soft power strategy.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al-Missned was born on August 8, 1959, daughter of Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned, a well-known opposition activist and former head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer. She spent much of her childhood in Kuwait during her father's exile following his imprisonment for political activities against the policies of the deposed emir Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani. She married Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, the heir apparent of Qatar, in 1977, the same year her father returned to Qatar. She is one of his three wives and the mother of seven children, including the current Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani.

Sheikha Moza received a BA in Sociology from Qatar University in 1986 and holds an MA in Public Policy in Islam from Hamad Bin Khalifa University. She has received honorary doctorates from prestigious institutions including Virginia Commonwealth University, Texas A&M, Carnegie Mellon, Imperial College London, and Georgetown University. She co-founded the Qatar Foundation for Education, Science and Community Development in 1995 and has been instrumental in establishing Education City in Doha, bringing Western universities to Qatar as part of the country's soft power strategy.

She has held numerous influential positions including UNESCO's Special Envoy for Basic and Higher Education (2003-2023), chairperson of Silatech since 2008, and founder of the Supreme Council for Family Affairs in 1998. She established Education Above All in 2012, focusing on making education accessible to marginalized children globally. She has been recognized as one of the '100 Most Powerful Women' by Forbes and has significant political influence in shaping Qatar's international relations and domestic policies.
achievements: Co-founder and chair of Qatar Foundation (1995)
Established Education Above All foundation (2012)
UNESCO Special Envoy for Basic and Higher Education (2003-2023)
Listed among '100 Most Powerful Women' by Forbes (2007, 2010)
Endowed Sidra Medical and Research Center with $7.9 billion

Saeed bin Abdullah Al Misnad served as the chief executive of Qatar National Bank, one of the region's leading financial institutions. He held this position until 2005, when he was succeeded by Ali Sharif Al Emadi. His tenure at the bank contributed to its growth and development during a crucial period in Qatar's economic expansion.

As a member of the Al Misnad family, he represented the family's involvement in Qatar's financial sector. His role at Qatar National Bank positioned him as an important figure in the country's banking and business community during his years of service.

notableFigures.biography:
Shaikha Rodha Al-Misnad is married to Sheikh Fahad bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who is the second son of former Emir Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani. Through this marriage, she connects the Al Misnad merchant family with the ruling Al Thani family, representing an important alliance between prominent Qatari families.

She and Sheikh Fahad have four children together: one son, Shaikh Hamad bin Fahad Al-Hamad Al-Thani, and three daughters: Shaikha Mariam, Shaikha Asma, and Shaikha Nayla bint Fahad Al-Hamad Al-Thani. Her marriage represents the continued importance and social standing of the Al Misnad family within Qatar's elite circles.

notableFigures.biography:
Khalid Bin Nasser Bin Abdulla Al Misnad serves as the president of the Misnad Group, described as an international trading and construction company. This positions him as a key figure in the Al Misnad family's business operations and their involvement in international commerce and construction projects.

He became notable in legal proceedings when he, along with Omar Ali Babtain, lodged a challenge against Adel Flaifel with the Supreme Court in Brisbane, Australia. They claimed that Flaifel had sold them properties in the Middle East worth 59.5 million Australian dollars but never transferred the contracts, and had used that money to purchase Australian properties. This case highlighted the Al Misnad family's international business dealings and their involvement in significant commercial transactions across different countries.

history: The Al Misnad family originates from the Banu Tamim tribe, with their name deriving from the Arabic word for 'support,' reflecting their traditional role as advisors within tribal society. The family has achieved extraordinary prominence in modern Qatar through both political leadership and marriage alliances with the ruling Al Thani family. The most notable figure is Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al Misnad, daughter of Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned who was a well-known opposition activist and former head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer. Her father was exiled for political activities against Emir Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani, with the family spending years in Kuwait before returning to Qatar in 1977. That same year, Moza married Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, becoming one of three consorts to the future emir and mother to current Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani. Through her leadership of Qatar Foundation since 1995 and numerous educational initiatives, she has been described as 'the actual ruler of Qatar' and represents the enlightened face of Qatar's transformation. The family's influence extends beyond royal connections - Mariam bint Ali bin Nasser Al Misnad has served as Minister of Social Development and Family and currently holds the position of Minister of State for International Cooperation. Additionally, family members have married into the Al Thani ruling family, with Shaikha Rodha Al-Misnad married to Sheikh Fahad bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, further cementing their position within Qatar's political elite.
modernStatus: One of Qatar's most influential families through royal marriage alliances and high ministerial positions, with members serving as Minister of State for International Cooperation and holding leadership roles in Qatar Foundation and other major institutions
