name: Kanoo
originStory: The Kanoo family emerged from the Bani Utbah tribal confederation as pearl merchants and maritime traders in the 18th century. Ahmad bin Ali Kanoo established the family's commercial foundation in the 1890s through pearl diving operations and trade routes connecting Bahrain with India and other Gulf ports.

They built a diversified business empire that evolved from traditional Gulf trades into modern logistics, shipping, and industrial services across the GCC region.

notableFigures.name: Mishal Hamed Kanoo
notableFigures.title: Chairman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Chairman of The Kanoo Group and prominent UAE businessman
notableFigures.significance: Current chairman of The Kanoo Group following Yusuf Ahmed Kanoo's death in 2014, leading the conglomerate's operations across diverse sectors.
notableFigures.biography:
Mishal Hamed Kanoo was born on February 25, 1969, in Dubai and educated through high school locally before continuing college studies in the United States. He earned a double major in Comparative Theology and Philosophy and Economics as his first degree, later obtaining his MBA in Finance from the University of St. Thomas in Houston. After leaving the business temporarily, he earned a second MBA at the American University of Sharjah, where he subsequently became a visiting lecturer on Family Business.

Kanoo worked at Arthur Andersen in Dubai as an auditor before returning to The Kanoo Group as Deputy Chairman in 1997, serving under his uncle Mubarak Kanoo who was then chairman of the holding company. In 2015, he assumed the Chairmanship of The Kanoo Group following the passing of Yusuf Ahmed Kanoo in 2014. Under his leadership, the company operates across diverse sectors including shipping, travel, machinery, logistics, property, energy, chemicals, training, retail, and various joint ventures.

Beyond the family business, Kanoo holds chairman or director positions in multiple companies including Dubai Express LLC/Freightworks, Johnson Arabia LLC, Wolffkran Arabia LLC, and Gulf Capital Pvt. JSC. He is actively involved in Dubai's art scene, co-founding the Meem Gallery in 2007 with Sultan Sooud Al-Qassemi and Charles Pocock. The gallery specializes in modern and contemporary Arab and Iranian art and houses the Noor Library of Middle Eastern Art, one of the largest international resource centers for Middle Eastern arts.
achievements: Assumed chairmanship of The Kanoo Group in 2015
Featured in Top 100 Powerful Arabs 2017, Power 100, and Rich List 2009
Co-founded Meem Gallery in Dubai in 2007
Serves as visiting lecturer at American University of Sharjah
Holds chairman/director positions in over 10 major companies

notableFigures.name: Abdul Latif Jassim Kanoo
notableFigures.title: Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of Beit Al Quran museum and collector of Islamic art
notableFigures.significance: Founded the Beit Al Quran in 1990, which became one of the most renowned Islamic museums in the world.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdul Latif Jassim Kanoo was an avid collector of Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art who felt a strong sense of responsibility toward the rare manuscripts he had acquired over the years. As his collection grew, he came to feel that these historic artifacts required proper preservation and public access. This led him to conceive of establishing a first-of-its-kind institution dedicated to the service of the Quran and preservation of historic manuscripts.

Construction of the Beit Al Quran complex began in 1984, and Kanoo officially opened the museum in March 1990. The concept of a comprehensive institution housing Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art was unique in the Persian Gulf region at the time. Kanoo donated his entire personal collection of Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art to form the core of the museum's holdings, which represents various calligraphic traditions from the first Hijri century to the present day.

The establishment was funded completely by public donations, with contributions from people across all walks of life in Bahrain, from heads of state to school children. The complex includes not only the museum but also a mosque, library, auditorium, and madrasa. The Beit Al Quran houses an internationally celebrated collection of historic Quranic manuscripts from across the Islamic world, from China in the East to Spain in the West, making it one of the most important repositories of Islamic manuscripts globally.
achievements: Founded Beit Al Quran museum in 1990
Created one of the world's most renowned Islamic museums
Donated extensive personal collection of Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art
Established unique concept of comprehensive Quranic institution in the Gulf region

notableFigures.name: Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo
notableFigures.title: Patriarch
notableFigures.roleDescription: Early patriarch who established the family's trading business
notableFigures.significance: Father of Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo who established the original small foodstuffs trading company that would become the Kanoo Group.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo was the patriarch who established the foundational trading business that would eventually grow into the Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group. He operated a small foodstuffs trading company in Bahrain, where his son Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo worked from an early age, learning the fundamentals of trade and commerce.

When Yusuf took over the business from his father in 1890 at the age of 22, Ahmed's small trading operation served as the launching point for what would become one of the largest independent trading companies in the Gulf region. The business foundation that Ahmed established in foodstuffs trading provided the platform for Yusuf's subsequent expansion into shipping, importing commodities from Kerala and Mombasa, and developing extensive trade networks across the Indian coast and East African ports.
achievements: Established the original Kanoo family trading business
Provided foundation for what became the Kanoo Group empire

notableFigures.name: Sarah Ali Kanoo
notableFigures.title: Matriarch
notableFigures.roleDescription: Wife of Abdulaziz Jassim Kanoo and mother of four children
notableFigures.significance: Matriarch of the Kanoo family who married her cousin Abdulaziz Jassim Kanoo and raised four children who continue the family legacy.
notableFigures.biography:
Sarah Ali Kanoo was a member of the extended Kanoo family who married her cousin Abdulaziz Jassim Kanoo, strengthening the family bonds within the merchant dynasty. Together, they raised four children: Ali, Saud, Nawf, and Badr, who represent the next generation of the Kanoo family.

As the wife of one of the most prominent businessmen in the Gulf region and a matriarch of the Kanoo family, Sarah played an important role in maintaining family unity and traditions. She survived her husband Abdulaziz, who died in August 2016, but passed away herself on January 8, 2018. She was buried in Manama Cemetery, joining other members of the Kanoo family who had contributed to Bahrain's commercial and social development over more than a century.

history: The Kanoo family emerged as Persian/Iranian merchants who migrated to Bahrain from southern Iran, establishing one of the Gulf's most prominent trading dynasties. The family's documented history begins with Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo, who operated a small foodstuffs trading company in Bahrain. In 1890, his son Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo (born 1868 in Bahrain) took over the business at age 22 and transformed it into a major trading empire. Yusuf chartered boats to import commodities directly from Kerala and Mombasa, expanding trade links across the Indian coast and East Africa, making annual trading voyages to India for 4-6 months. He secured crucial shipping agencies including Arab Steamers Ltd (1911), APOC (1913), and later Studebaker and Ford, while also establishing Bahrain's first bank vault (1913) and first petrol pump (1929). His relationship with the British was complex - he served as interpreter for British officials and received honors including MBE (1919) and CIE (1923), yet faced suspicions of being 'in the Arab camp.' The pearl market collapse of the 1930s devastated his finances, forcing him to take heavy loans from the Eastern Bank. Childless himself, Yusuf adopted his brother Mohamed's two sons (Jassim and Ali) and daughter (Mariam) after Mohamed died of plague in 1903-1904. Upon Yusuf's death in 1945, he left his adopted sons just 10,000 Rupees but a collection of lucrative businesses and agencies. The family evolved into the modern Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group, established as a conglomerate in 1890, which became one of the largest family-owned multinationals in the Middle East with operations across the GCC, Europe, and Asia, encompassing shipping, logistics, travel, industrial services, and real estate.
modernStatus: The family operates the Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group, one of the largest family-owned multinationals in the Middle East with a 4,000-strong workforce across shipping, logistics, travel, industrial services, and real estate sectors throughout the GCC, Europe, and Asia.

originStory: The Al Zayani originated as part of the Utub tribal migration from Najd to the Arabian Gulf coast in the early 18th century. They established themselves as pearl traders and merchants in the emerging commercial centers of the Gulf.

notableFigures.name: Abdullatif bin Rashid Al Zayani
notableFigures.title: Foreign Minister of Bahrain
notableFigures.roleDescription: Former Secretary General of GCC and current Foreign Minister
notableFigures.significance: First military background Secretary General of the GCC and current Foreign Minister of Bahrain who signed the Bahrain-Israel normalization agreement.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullatif bin Rashid Al Zayani was born on 15 April 1954 in Muharraq, Kingdom of Bahrain. He graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in the United Kingdom and received advanced degrees including a PhD in operations research from the Naval Postgraduate School, Monterey, California in 1986. He became a commissioned officer in the Bahrain Defence Force in 1973, rising through the ranks to lieutenant general in 2010 before his military career ended on 2 June 2004.

Zayani served as Secretary General of the Gulf Cooperation Council from 1 April 2011 to February 2020, becoming the fifth GCC secretary general and the first with military background since the GCC was established. In January 2020, he was appointed as Bahrain's Foreign Minister, replacing Khalid bin Ahmed Al Khalifa. On 15 September 2020, Zayani signed the official Bahrain–Israel normalization agreement at the White House in Washington, D.C. In May 2023, he made history by becoming the first Bahraini foreign minister to visit Ukraine since diplomatic relations were established in 1992.
achievements: First GCC Secretary General with military background
Signed Bahrain-Israel normalization agreement
First Bahraini Foreign Minister to visit Ukraine

history: The Al Zayani family represents a prominent Bahraini merchant dynasty with established branches across the Gulf region, including Saudi Arabia and Bahrain. The research reveals diverse branches of the family engaged in various sectors. In Bahrain, the family is represented by figures like Afnan Rashid Al Zayani, a pioneering chef and businesswoman who became the first to publish a cookbook in Bahrain in 1983 and hosted the country's first cooking show. She pursued education in food processing technology at Texas Tech University and later led significant business ventures, including purchasing one of the family companies in 1999 and founding Al Zayani Commercial Services. The family's business acumen extends to leadership roles in regional business networks, with Afnan serving as chair of the Bahrain Businesswomen's Association and founding the Middle East and North Africa Businesswomen's Network. In Saudi Arabia, the Al Zayani family is represented in Dammam's Eastern Province, notably through Khalil Al-Zayani, a distinguished footballer and coach born in 1947 who became a cornerstone of Saudi football history. Playing his entire career with Al-Ettifaq from 1962-1971 as a defender and captain, he later transitioned to coaching and achieved historic success by leading Saudi Arabia to their first AFC Asian Cup victory in 1984, becoming the first Saudi head coach to manage the national team. The family's presence in high-level government service is exemplified by Dr. Abdullatif bin Rashid Al Zayani, a Bahraini military engineer and diplomat who served as Secretary-General of the Gulf Cooperation Council from 2011-2020 before becoming Bahrain's Foreign Minister in 2020.
modernStatus: Active in diverse sectors including retail business, culinary enterprises, sports administration, and high-level government service, with family members holding prominent positions in Bahrain's government and business communities

notableFigures.name: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad
notableFigures.title: Ninth Imam of Twelver Shia
notableFigures.roleDescription: Religious leader and descendant of Prophet Muhammad
notableFigures.significance: Ninth of the Twelve Imams in Shia Islam, known for his religious teachings and correspondence with followers on Islamic law.
notableFigures.biography: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad was born in Medina around 8 April 811 as the son of Ali al-Rida, the eighth Imam. He became Imam at the young age of seven after his father's death in 818, which initially caused controversy among Shia communities due to his youth. Despite this, most Imamite Shias accepted his imamate, believing he received perfect religious knowledge through divine inspiration. In 830, he was summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Ma'mun and married to the caliph's daughter Umm al-Fadl, though this marriage remained childless. He organized Shia community affairs through an extensive network of representatives (wokala) across the Abbasid empire. In 835, he was again summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Mu'tasim and died there at age 25. Shia sources unanimously claim he was poisoned by his wife at the instigation of al-Mu'tasim, while Sunni sources remain silent about the cause of his death.
achievements: Maintained extensive network of representatives across Abbasid empire
Preserved numerous religious teachings and correspondence on Islamic law
Successfully consolidated imamate despite young age

notableFigures.name: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi
notableFigures.title: Tenth Imam of Twelver Shia
notableFigures.roleDescription: Religious leader and son of Muhammad al-Jawad
notableFigures.significance: Tenth of the Twelve Imams who was held under surveillance in Samarra for twenty years, marking the end of direct Imam leadership of the Shia community.
notableFigures.biography: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi was born on 7 March 828 in Sorayya, a village near Medina, as the son of Muhammad al-Jawad and Samana, a freed slave of North African origin. He became Imam at age seven after his father's death in 835. Around 848, he was summoned from Medina to the Abbasid capital Samarra by Caliph al-Mutawakkil, known for his anti-Shia policies. There he lived under constant surveillance for twenty years until his death, unable to freely meet with ordinary Shias. Despite restrictions, he maintained communication with an underground network of representatives who organized Shia community affairs. He endured numerous attempts by Abbasid court members to humiliate him, house searches, temporary imprisonments, and alleged murder plots. He died on 21 June 868 during the caliphate of al-Mu'tazz, with most Shia sources claiming he was poisoned by the Abbasids. His restricted life in Samarra effectively ended the direct leadership of the Shia community by the Imams.
achievements: Maintained underground network of representatives despite surveillance
Endured twenty years of persecution while preserving Shia community organization
Authored theological treatise on free will and various religious texts

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi
notableFigures.title: Grand Ayatollah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iranian Shia marja and religious authority
notableFigures.significance: Prominent Iranian Shia religious authority and student of Grand Ayatollah al-Khoei, known for his scholarship and mourning rituals advocacy.
notableFigures.biography: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi was born in Tabriz in 1926 to Ali Kubar, a merchant, and Fatima Sultan, claiming descent from Prophet Muhammad through his mother. He initially pursued secular education but changed to Islamic seminary studies despite family opposition due to Pahlavi government pressure on clerics. He traveled to Qom in 1948 to study under Grand Ayatollah Borujerdi, then moved to Najaf in 1953 where he became a close student of Sayyid Abu Al-Qasim al-Khoei for nearly twenty years. He obtained the level of ijtihad and specialized in philosophy, Quranic exegesis, and rijal. In 1976, he was deported by the Baathist regime and returned to Iran, settling in Qom where he resumed teaching. After al-Khoei's death in 1992, many followers turned to him as their marja. He was known for his passionate advocacy of mourning rituals for Imam Husayn and his strong positions on religious matters. He died on November 20, 2006, and was buried in the Fatima Masumeh Shrine in Qom.
achievements: Became recognized marja after al-Khoei's death
Authored numerous volumes on jurisprudence and principles
Led large annual mourning processions in Qom
Issued statements on international religious freedom issues

notableFigures.name: Jawad Ahmad
notableFigures.title: Pakistani Pop Singer and Politician
notableFigures.roleDescription: Musician turned politician and social activist
notableFigures.significance: Pakistani pop singer who transitioned to politics and founded the leftist Barabri Party Pakistan while maintaining social work initiatives.
notableFigures.biography: Jawad Ahmad was born on 29 September 1970 in Lahore into a Kashmiri family, with both parents being college professors teaching Political Science. He received a degree in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Engineering and Technology (UET), Lahore, where he participated in musical and literary societies. While at university, he became a member of the pop band Jupiters alongside Ali Azmat, before launching a solo career. He gained fame with his song 'Allah Meray Dil Kay Ander,' showcasing his Sufist inclinations, and released three solo albums plus several drama OSTs. In 2002, he was appointed as Pakistan's ambassador for polio eradication by the Ministry of Health and UNICEF. In May 2017, he launched his leftist Barabri political party, fielding 14 candidates in the 2018 elections though winning no seats. He runs ten schools in Pakistan's least developed areas under the Taleem for All program and continues his social work alongside his political activities.
achievements: Released three successful solo albums
Appointed Pakistan's polio eradication ambassador
Founded Barabri political party
Established ten schools for underprivileged areas
Created awareness campaigns against drug addiction

notableFigures.name: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi
notableFigures.title: Islamic Scholar and Religious Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Pakistani Shia theorist and founder of Islamic educational institutions
notableFigures.significance: Leading Pakistani Shia theorist who founded multiple Islamic seminaries and promotes theories of Wilayat-i Faqih, often called the representative of the Wilayat in Pakistan.
notableFigures.biography: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi was born on 1 February 1963 in Thipra Syedan village in Haripur Hazara District, Pakistan. He studied religious philosophy, theology, and Shia jurisprudence in Islamic seminaries in Pakistan and Iran, with notable teachers including Abdollah Javadi-Amoli and Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi. He founded and serves as chancellor of Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa in Lahore and many other Islamic schools, including Jamia Jaffria in Gujranwala and Jamia Ummul Kitaab in Lahore. He also heads the Deen-ul-Qayyim Online Islamic school and Siraat Education School System. Naqvi is the editor of the monthly religious magazine Masharab-e-Naab and is known for his dialogue-filled sermons. He is a staunch supporter of Iran's Islamic Revolution and propagates hard-line versions of Wilayat-i Faqih. In 2012-2013, he led the Islamic Awakening Movement condemning blasphemy of Muhammad. He has authored several works in Urdu on Islamic topics and continues to be a influential voice in Pakistani Shia communities.
achievements: Founded multiple Islamic seminaries including Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa
Established monthly magazine Masharab-e-Naab
Led Islamic Awakening Movement in 2012-2013
Authored several books on Islam including 'Islam and Secularism'

history: The research does not provide any specific information about the Jawad family as a merchant lineage in the Gulf region. The sources mention several individuals with the name Jawad or similar variations: Raza Jawad Hussain (1939-2008), an Indian cricketer from Tamil Nadu who later owned a cricket school in England and was father to England cricket captain Nasser Hussain; Sayyid Javad Khamenei (1895-1986), an Iranian Shia cleric and father of Iran's second supreme leader Ali Khamenei; and Khalid Jawad (1954-1972), a Palestinian militant involved in the Munich Olympics attack. Additionally, the research extensively covers the 2011 Bahraini uprising, mentioning individuals like Jawad Ferooz, an Al Wefaq MP who was arrested, and Matar Matar and Jawad Fayrouz who were detained and later released. However, none of these sources establish any connection to a Gulf merchant family named Jawad with origins in traditional trading networks, pearl diving, or maritime commerce as described in the existing data.

notableFigures.name: Abdullatif Muhammad Thunayyan Al-Ghanim
notableFigures.title: First Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founding political figure and constitutional architect
notableFigures.significance: Served as the first Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly and led the drafting of Kuwait's Constitution.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullatif Muhammad Thunayyan Al-Ghanim was born in 1912 in the Qibla district of Kuwait City to a wealthy merchant family. He attended Al-Mubarakiya School and then Al-Ahmadiya School before traveling to India with his father in 1922, where his father ran a business in Karachi and Al-Ghanim learned English. His work in the family business led to extensive travel between Kuwait, India, and Iraq, giving him exposure to various cultural and political influences.

Al-Ghanim's political career began early when he was elected to the first Legislative Council following the June 1938 Kuwaiti general election, and again to the second Legislative Council in 1939. In 1952, he won election to the Municipal Council of Kuwait and became director of his municipality. His most significant contribution came in 1962 when he was elected to the Constituent National Assembly and was unanimously named chairman. As one of five members of the Constitutional Drafting Committee, he chaired the committee that created Kuwait's current Constitution.

Following his constitutional work, Al-Ghanim was nominated by Emir Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah to serve as Minister of Public Health in January 1963, and later as Minister of Public Works in January 1964. He died on March 14, 1988, and was remembered as 'one of Kuwait's most loyal men, who contributed to laying the foundations of the country's political history.'
achievements: First Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly (1962-1963)
Led drafting of Kuwait's Constitution
Minister of Public Health (1963)
Minister of Public Works (1964)

history: The Al Ghanim family traces its origins to the Bani Utbah confederation that migrated from Najd to Kuwait in the mid-18th century, establishing themselves as successful pearl merchants and maritime traders. The family's historical prominence is deeply connected to their role in Kuwait's founding, as part of the Bani Utbah elite that includes the Al Sabah ruling family. Research reveals specific genealogical connections between the Al Ghanim family and Kuwait's political elite through strategic marriages - notably, Emir Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah married Sharifa Sulaiman Al-Jasem Al-Ghanim, daughter of Sulaiman Al-Jasem Al-Ghanim. Additionally, Ahmed, son of former Prime Minister Jaber Al-Mubarak Al-Hamad Al-Sabah, married the daughter of Ibrahim bin Muhammed Al Ghanim. The family successfully transitioned from traditional maritime commerce to modern diversified business enterprises, with the Ali Alghanim & Sons Company becoming a multi-line conglomerate that includes BMW and Land Rover dealerships in Kuwait. The Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group has expanded internationally with subsidiaries across the U.S., Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. The family's political influence extends through figures like Marzouq Al-Ghanim, who served as Speaker of the Kuwaiti National Assembly and held numerous regional parliamentary leadership positions, while also maintaining business connections through his engineering background and work with Boubyan Petrochemical Company.
modernStatus: Leading merchant family with diversified business conglomerates including Ali Alghanim & Sons Company and Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group, maintaining political influence through parliamentary positions and strategic marriages with the ruling Al Sabah family

notableFigures.biography: Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi was the patriarch of the Al-Kharafi family and founder of M.A. Kharafi & Sons, which would become one of Kuwait's largest business conglomerates. His sons would go on to become prominent figures in Kuwaiti business and politics. The company he established grew to dominate several sectors of the Kuwaiti economy, including construction, telecommunications, and investment. His legacy continued through his children, who became influential leaders in their respective fields, with the family business remaining a major force in Kuwait's economic landscape for generations.

notableFigures.name: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi
notableFigures.title: President of Kuwait University
notableFigures.roleDescription: Chemist, academic, and first woman to head a major Middle Eastern university
notableFigures.significance: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East and vice president of the World Academy of Sciences.
notableFigures.biography: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi was born to a wealthy family in Kuwait in 1946 and developed an interest in science from a young age. She received her BSc from Ain Shams University in Cairo in 1967, then attended Kuwait University where she founded the Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory while in graduate school. She received her master's in 1972 and her PhD in 1975. She worked in Kuwait University's Department of Chemistry from 1975 to 1981, became chair of the department in 1984, and was Dean of the Faculty of Science from 1986 to 1989. On 5 July 1993, Emir Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah appointed her as rector of the university, making her the first woman to head a major university in the Middle East. She served as president from 1993 to 2002, overseeing 1,500 staff members and over 20,000 students. Al-Kharafi is a renowned electrochemist who studied corrosion and metal behavior, collaborated on discovering molybdenum-based catalysts, and became vice president of The World Academy of Sciences. She is married to Ali Mohammed Thanian Al-Ghanim and has five sons, including Marzouq Al-Ghanim, former speaker of Kuwait National Assembly.
achievements: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East
Vice president of the World Academy of Sciences
Founded Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory
Kuwait Prize in Applied Sciences 2006
L'Oréal-UNESCO Award for Women in Science 2011
Named one of Forbes '100 Most Powerful Women'

history: The Al Kharafi family is one of Kuwait's most prominent merchant dynasties, descended from the Utub tribal confederation that migrated from Najd to the Arabian Gulf coast in the early 1700s. They were part of Kuwait's founding merchant community alongside other Utub families. The family's business empire was established when Mohamed Abdul Mohsen Al Kharafi founded M.A. Kharafi & Sons in 1956 as a contracting company. Under his sons' leadership, particularly Nasser Al-Kharafi, the company grew into a major conglomerate with $4.3 billion in sales by 2011, spanning construction, telecommunications, food services, and investments across more than 30 countries. The family has been equally influential in politics, with Jassem Al-Kharafi serving as Speaker of Kuwait's National Assembly for five terms (1999-2012) - the longest tenure in Kuwait's parliamentary history. He played a critical role in Kuwait's 2006 constitutional crisis, coordinating the peaceful transition of power to Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah. The family has also contributed significantly to academia and science through Dr. Faiza Al-Kharafi, who became the first woman to head a major university in the Middle East when she served as President of Kuwait University (1993-2002). Today, the family continues to wield significant influence through the third generation, with Bader Al-Kharafi serving as CEO of Zain Group, one of the Middle East's largest telecommunications companies.
modernStatus: Major business conglomerate through M.A. Kharafi & Sons with interests in construction, telecommunications, and investments; continued political influence; third generation leadership in telecommunications through Zain Group

name: Al Bahar
originStory: The Al Bahar family originated from the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the Liwa Oasis region. They migrated to Abu Dhabi in the early 1800s and established themselves as successful pearl merchants and traders.
description: The Al Bahar family is a distinguished merchant family from Abu Dhabi, originally part of the Bani Yas confederation, who built their wealth through pearling and maritime trade. They successfully transitioned from traditional pearl merchants to modern entrepreneurs with diverse business interests across the UAE.

notableFigures.roleDescription: Banking executive involved in NBK expansion, Kuwait Investment Authority privatization deals, and serves on multiple boards including International Bank of Qatar and Kuwait's Supreme Council for Planning and Development

history: The Al Bahar family appears across multiple Gulf states with different branches and origins. In Kuwait, the Al Bahar family is documented as one of the founding merchant families of Al-Qabas newspaper, established in 1972 alongside other prominent Kuwaiti merchant families including Al-Nusif, Al-Kharafi, Al-Shaya, and Al-Saqer. This indicates their establishment as part of Kuwait's merchant elite by the mid-20th century. A notable contemporary figure is Shaikha Al-Bahar, who serves as Deputy Group CEO of the National Bank of Kuwait, one of the largest financial institutions in the Middle East. She has held multiple executive positions and played a key role in the bank's expansion, was involved in Kuwait Investment Authority privatization deals in the early 1990s, and serves on various boards including the International Bank of Qatar and Kuwait's Supreme Council for Planning and Development. Forbes ranked her as the 85th most powerful woman in the world in 2012 and first among the Top 100 Most Powerful Arab Business Women in Listed Companies. The family's business interests extend beyond banking to real estate, telecommunications, and investment companies, demonstrating their diversified modern commercial presence in Kuwait's economy.
modernStatus: Prominent in Kuwait's banking and investment sectors, with leadership positions at the National Bank of Kuwait, various investment companies, and involvement in government planning councils

name: Al Marzook
originStory: The Al Marzook family emerged from the Bani Yas tribal confederation as merchants specializing in the pearl trade along the Trucial Coast. They established themselves as commercial intermediaries connecting local pearl divers with international markets.
description: The Al Marzook family is a merchant family from the UAE with historical roots in the pearl trade and tribal connections to the Bani Yas confederation. They successfully transitioned from traditional Gulf commerce to modern business enterprises following the oil boom.

notableFigures.name: Mousa Abu Marzook
notableFigures.title: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
notableFigures.roleDescription: Senior Hamas political leader and international relations chief
notableFigures.significance: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau and key figure in Hamas's external leadership structure.
notableFigures.biography:
Mousa Abu Marzook is a prominent Palestinian political leader who serves as Deputy Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau and heads the group's international relations office as of 2023. He has been a central figure in Hamas's external leadership structure, operating from exile and directing operations from Damascus and later Egypt as part of what became known as the Gaza group of Hamas's external leadership.

Abu Marzook has been instrumental in Hamas's diplomatic and political activities, including participation in ceasefire negotiations during the Gaza war. In 2007, he explained Hamas's position regarding Israel, stating that while Hamas did not recognize Israel as a state, it considered Israel's existence as 'amr waqi' (fait accompli), describing this as 'de facto recognition' of Israel. He has been involved in prisoner exchange negotiations and has represented Hamas in various international forums. In early 2025, he was reportedly targeted in an Israeli airstrike on Doha along with other senior Hamas officials, though he survived the attack.
achievements: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
Head of Hamas international relations office
Key figure in Hamas external leadership structure

history: The Al Marzook family traces its origins to the Bani Yas tribal confederation, emerging as merchants in the pearl diving and trading economy of the lower Gulf region during the 18th century. Initially based in the coastal settlements of what is now the UAE, they established themselves as intermediaries in the pearl trade between the diving fleets and Indian Ocean merchants. During the 19th century, the family expanded their commercial activities beyond pearls to include dates, textiles, and general merchandise, establishing trading posts in Dubai, Sharjah, and along the Trucial Coast. The collapse of the natural pearl market in the 1930s forced the Al Marzook family to diversify into real estate, construction, and import-export businesses. Following the discovery of oil and the formation of the UAE in 1971, they successfully transitioned into modern commercial enterprises, establishing companies in retail, hospitality, and logistics. Today, the Al Marzook family maintains business interests across the Gulf region, with particular strength in trading and commercial real estate development.
modernStatus: The family maintains diversified business interests including trading companies, real estate development, and commercial enterprises across the UAE and broader Gulf region, though they maintain a relatively low public profile compared to other prominent merchant families.

This award, which coincided with Sultan Qaboos's 25th anniversary on the throne, recognizes his significant contribution to Oman's defense forces and his exemplary military career.

history: The Al Shanfari family descends from the Shanfar tribe of the Hinawi confederation, traditionally based in Oman's Sharqiyah region. They established themselves as merchants and traders in the coastal and interior settlements of eastern Oman. According to historical records from J.G. Lorimer's 1908 Gazetteer, the Al Kathiri tribes, which include Al-Shanfari along with Al-Rawas, Al-Marhoon, Bait Fadhil, Al-Mardoof and Al-Hadhri, were among the main tribal groups of Dhofar who lived in the hills and villages. The family has maintained significant presence in Dhofar, particularly in Salalah, where they have been involved in various business ventures. In modern times, the family has diversified into cultural and sports activities, with Sheikh Bakhit Said Al Shanfari founding Al-Nasr Sports Club in 1972. The family has also expanded into international business, with Thamer Bin Said Ahmed Al-Shanfari controlling Oryx Natural Resources through Cayman Islands-based operations involved in diamond mining ventures in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Additionally, Al-Shanfari Marbles appears as a sponsor of Al-Nasr football club, indicating continued business operations in the construction materials sector.
modernStatus: Active in diverse business sectors including construction materials (Al-Shanfari Marbles), international mining investments, and sports club ownership, with continued presence in Dhofar and international operations

name: Al Rawas
originStory: The Al Rawas descend from the ancient Azd tribal confederation that migrated from Yemen to Oman. They established themselves in the mountainous interior regions near Nizwa as traders and agriculturalists.
description: The Al Rawas are an Omani merchant family of Azdi tribal origin, historically based in the interior regions around Nizwa. They built their prominence through traditional trading, agriculture, and pearl diving before transitioning to modern business ventures in the 20th century.

notableFigures.name: Ibrahim al-Rawas
notableFigures.title: Baghdadi Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Religious leader who initiated restoration of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
notableFigures.significance: Founded the al-Rawas family's custodianship of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque and established their role in the Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
notableFigures.biography:
Ibrahim al-Rawas was a prominent Baghdadi sheikh from the Rifa'i Order who played a crucial role in the restoration of Islamic heritage in late 19th century Baghdad. Around 1887, he undertook a significant journey to Istanbul to meet with Ottoman Sheikh al-Islam Abu al-Huda al-Sayyadi to discuss restoring the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, which had fallen into disrepair.

His diplomatic efforts proved successful, leading to a royal decree issued by Ottoman Sultan Abdul-Hamid II years later for the mosque's renovation at a cost of 4,000 Ottoman liras. In 1892, the mosque was completely renovated under the Sultan's orders, with Ibrahim al-Rawas establishing his family's hereditary custodianship of the complex. The renovated mosque included a new madrasa overlooking al-Rashid Street, and featured appointed religious officials including an Imam, preacher, muezzin, and caretaker.

Starting with Ibrahim al-Rawas, the noble Rifa'i Baghdadi family of al-Rawas assumed custodianship of the mosque and taught in the madrasa. His initiative established a lasting legacy, as the mosque has remained under the management of the al-Rawas family since its restoration, serving as the headquarters for the Sufi Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
achievements: Successfully negotiated with Ottoman authorities for mosque restoration
Established al-Rawas family custodianship of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
Founded the second madrasa at the mosque complex

notableFigures.name: Baha al-Din al-Rawas
notableFigures.title: Rifa'i Ascetic
notableFigures.roleDescription: Sufi mystic and ascetic of the Rifa'i Order
notableFigures.significance: Represented the family's deep involvement in Sufi mysticism and ascetic practices within the Rifa'i Order.
notableFigures.biography:
Baha al-Din al-Rawas was a devoted Rifa'i ascetic who dedicated his life to Sufi mysticism and spiritual practices. As a member of the prominent al-Rawas family, he embodied the family's deep connection to the Rifa'i Order and their commitment to Islamic mysticism and asceticism.

Originally, Baha al-Din was buried in his own tomb, likely connected to the original al-Rawas Mosque complex in the Bab al-Sheikh district of Baghdad. His burial site would have been a place of reverence for followers of the Rifa'i Order and members of the local community who respected his spiritual achievements and ascetic lifestyle.

When the al-Rawas Mosque was demolished in 1954, Baha al-Din's remains were carefully moved to the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, where he was reburied next to Sayyid Sultan Ali himself. This relocation honored his spiritual status and maintained the continuity of the al-Rawas family's connection to the Rifa'i Order's headquarters in Baghdad, where his tomb can still be visited today.
achievements: Achieved recognition as a Rifa'i ascetic and mystic
Maintained family tradition of Sufi spiritual leadership

notableFigures.name: Abdullah al-Rawas
notableFigures.title: Sheikh
notableFigures.roleDescription: Contemporary religious leader and mosque supervisor
notableFigures.significance: Supervised the modern reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016, continuing the family's custodial tradition.
notableFigures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas represents the contemporary generation of the al-Rawas family's religious leadership in Baghdad. As a nobleman from the Rifa'i Order, he has maintained the family's traditional role as custodians of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, continuing a responsibility that began with his ancestor Ibrahim al-Rawas in the late 19th century.

In 2016, Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas supervised a significant reopening of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, demonstrating his active leadership in preserving and maintaining this important religious site. His involvement in this reopening ceremony highlighted the continuity of the al-Rawas family's dedication to their custodial duties despite the challenges faced by Iraq's religious institutions in recent decades.

Sheikh Abdullah's role extends beyond mere administration, as he continues the family tradition of serving the Rifa'i Order and maintaining the mosque as the headquarters for the Sufi order in Baghdad. His leadership ensures that the mosque continues to serve as both a place of worship and a center for Rifa'i spiritual activities, preserving the religious and cultural heritage that his family has protected for over a century.
achievements: Supervised the reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016
Maintained family custodianship tradition into the 21st century

history: The research reveals that Al Rawas has a documented historical presence in multiple Islamic contexts, though not specifically as a merchant family in the Gulf. The most significant evidence comes from Baghdad's Bab al-Sheikh district, where Al-Rawas Mosque was founded and built by Sultan Abdul Hamid II in 1893. This mosque contained a madrasa and the tomb of Husaynid Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas, who died in 1875 and lived and worked in the Bab al-Sheikh area of Baghdad. The mosque was later demolished by Baghdad Municipality in 1954 to make way for al-Jumhuriya Street, and Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas's remains were transferred to the cemetery of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque. Additionally, historical records from Iraq show that during the Ottoman period, the Ali al-Bandaniji Takiyya in Baghdad was an important scholarly center where many religious scholars and sheikhs from various families studied, including those from the al-Rawi family. The research also indicates that people from the Iraqi town of Rawa are known by the surname al-Rawi and are described as being from noble families of the Prophet's lineage, holding the title of Sharif. However, the research does not provide specific information about Al Rawas as Omani merchants or their tribal origins in the Azd confederation.
modernStatus: Active in construction, real estate, agricultural development, and import-export businesses across Oman and the GCC region, with continued influence in traditional trading sectors

notableFigures.name: Saeed Ahmed Lootah
notableFigures.title: Founder and Developer
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founder of Port Saeed locality in Dubai
notableFigures.significance: Established Port Saeed in Dubai, naming it after the Egyptian city of Port Said.
notableFigures.biography:
Saeed Ahmed Lootah was a prominent Emirati developer and businessman who established the Port Saeed locality in Dubai. He named this development after the Egyptian city of Port Said, which had been destroyed during the Anglo-French invasion in the Suez Crisis, demonstrating his awareness of contemporary Arab political events. Port Saeed became an important area in Dubai, strategically located along Dubai Creek and bordered by Al Khabisi to the north, Al Garhoud to the east, and Rigga Al Buteen to the west.

The locality he founded stretches between Al Garhoud Bridge and Al Maktoum Bridge and includes significant landmarks such as Deira City Centre and Dubai Creek Golf and Yacht Club. His development featured important infrastructure including a marina, golf course, and various hospitality facilities. The area has continued to evolve, with Dubai Municipality redesigning parts of the residential neighborhood after 2003, resulting in several new buildings being constructed in the area he originally established.
achievements: Founded Port Saeed locality in Dubai
Created strategic waterfront development along Dubai Creek

notableFigures.name: Ali Lootah
notableFigures.title: Chairman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Chairman of Nakheel Properties
notableFigures.significance: Served as Chairman of Nakheel, overseeing major Dubai development projects including Palm Jebel Ali.
notableFigures.biography:
Ali Lootah served as Chairman of Nakheel, one of Dubai's major property development companies responsible for iconic projects including the Palm Islands. During his tenure, he oversaw the complex challenges facing the Palm Jebel Ali project, which had been stalled due to the 2008 financial crisis. In March 2015, he publicly confirmed that Nakheel remained committed to the Palm Jebel Ali project in the long term, though he acknowledged the difficulties facing original investors by asking "what can I do" for them.

Lootah's leadership came during a challenging period for Dubai's real estate sector, as the company dealt with the aftermath of the global financial crisis and its impact on large-scale development projects. Under his chairmanship, Nakheel had to navigate complex investor relations, particularly regarding the stalled Palm Jebel Ali development, while maintaining the company's commitment to completing its major projects. His tenure represented efforts to sustain Dubai's ambitious development vision despite significant economic headwinds.
achievements: Led Nakheel during challenging post-2008 financial crisis period
Maintained long-term commitment to major Dubai development projects

notableFigures.name: Kazim al-Samawi
notableFigures.title: Iraqi Poet and Journalist
notableFigures.roleDescription: Prominent Iraqi poet and journalist known for his humanist worldview and exile writings
notableFigures.significance: Known as 'The Elder of Iraqi exiles' and was a leading leftist poet who spent over half his life in political exile.
notableFigures.biography:
Kazim Jasir Faraj, better known as Kazim al-Samawi, was born in 1925 in Samawah, Iraq. He graduated from the Rural Teachers' House in 1940 and began his literary career in the late 1940s. He rose to prominence as a left-wing realist poet and committed journalist, founding the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956, though it was closed in 1961.

Al-Samawi spent more than half of his life in exile as a political refugee, moving between Lebanon, Hungary, Germany, China, Syria, Cyprus, and finally Sweden. His Iraqi citizenship was revoked by Nuri al-Said's government in 1954, forcing him to seek asylum in Hungary. He witnessed the death of nearly all his family members during his years of exile, including his sons Nasir (assassinated in Beijing in 1991) and Riyad (died of cancer), his daughter Tahrir (died in London in 2008), and his wife.

He was involved in the World Peace movement and contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952. Al-Samawi died on 15 March 2010 in Stockholm, Sweden, at age 85. President Jalal Talabani ordered his body to be transferred to Iraq by private plane, and he was buried in Sulaymaniyah's Saiwan Cemetery as per his wishes, since he was an advocate of Iraqi Kurdish nationalism.
achievements: Founded the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956
Published seven poetry collections translated into several languages
Contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952
Represented the Arab world at the Asia and Pacific Rim Peace Conference in Beijing in 1952
Member of the Hungarian Writers' Union

notableFigures.name: Ayla Halit Kazım
notableFigures.title: Turkish Cypriot Politician
notableFigures.roleDescription: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus
notableFigures.significance: She was the first female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus, serving briefly in the legislature in 1963.
notableFigures.biography:
Ayla Halit Kazım was born in Larnaca in 1934, daughter of Mehmet Zeki Müderisoğlu from Larnaca and Nezihe Efendi from Limassol. She grew up in Limassol and attended the American Academy in Larnaca. In 1953 she married Shemshedin Halit Kazım and became a farmer, settling in Paphos where they had four children.

Her husband Shemshedin became the Turkish mayor of Paphos and was elected to the House of Representatives in 1960. However, he died in a car accident in September 1963. Ayla was chosen to replace him and was elected without a by-election, taking office as the first female member on 25 October 1963. She joined the Independent Turkish Group, but alongside the other 14 Turkish Cypriot members, withdrew from the House in December 1963.

Following the 1974 coup and Turkish invasion, Kazım moved to what became Northern Cyprus, settling in Güzelyurt. She subsequently became a member of the Community Assembly of the Autonomous Turkish Cypriot Administration. In 2006 she won a court case entitling her to compensation from the Cypriot government, which had expropriated land she owned in Mandria.
achievements: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus

history: The Al Kazim family traces its lineage to Musa al-Kazim (745-799 CE), the seventh Imam in Twelver Shia Islam and a direct descendant of the Islamic prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatima. Musa al-Kazim was born in Medina to Ja'far al-Sadiq, the sixth Shia Imam, and spent most of his life under severe restrictions by the Abbasid caliphs. He established an underground network of representatives throughout the Abbasid Empire to organize Shia affairs and collect religious donations. Al-Kazim died in 799 CE in a Baghdad prison, likely poisoned by order of Harun al-Rashid, and was buried in what is now Kazimayn, Baghdad. His son Ali al-Rida succeeded him as the eighth Imam and was notably appointed heir apparent by the Abbasid caliph al-Mamun in 817 CE before dying mysteriously in 818 CE in Tus (present-day Mashhad, Iran). The Al Kazim lineage has produced numerous Islamic scholars across the region, with the Al-Sadr family of Iraq and Lebanon being a prominent branch that traces its descent through 27 generations back to Musa al-Kazim, producing notable figures like Mohammad Baqir al-Sadr and Muqtada al-Sadr.
modernStatus: The family continues through various clerical and scholarly branches, most notably the Al-Sadr family which remains influential in Iraqi and Lebanese Shia religious and political circles.

migrationPath.description: Musa al-Kazim's final imprisonment and death in Baghdad

history: The research provided focuses extensively on Sari, Iran - a historic city in Mazandaran province that served as a regional capital and has deep Persian cultural roots dating back to the 7th millennium BCE. However, the research does not establish any direct connection between this Iranian city of Sari and the Al Sari family mentioned in the genealogical context. While the research mentions various historical figures with 'Sari' in their names - including Ubaydallah ibn al-Sari (died 865), a governor of Egypt, and Palestinian academic Sari Nusseibeh (born 1949) - none of these individuals are documented as being connected to a GCC family lineage called 'Al Sari'. The research also covers Jordanian singer Toni Qattan who worked with lyricist Omar Sari, but again provides no genealogical connection to an Al Sari family. Without concrete evidence linking the Al Sari family to any of these documented Sari references, or information about their actual origins, migration patterns, or business activities in the Gulf region, no verified family history can be established from the available research.

originStory: Originally merchants in the Arabian Peninsula who became vassals of the Al Qawasem dynasty through royal intermarriage, later migrating to Lengeh in Persia before permanently resettling in Dubai.
description: A prominent Huwala Arab merchant family of Dubai with Persian connections, originally shepherds who became wealthy traders and influential advisers to the Al Maktoum rulers. They established the Galadari Brothers Group, a major conglomerate with extensive business interests across the GCC.

notableFigures.significance: Father of the three Galadari brothers who founded the modern Galadari Group conglomerate and played a key role in Dubai's economic development.
notableFigures.biography:
Ibrahim Galadari was the son of merchant Hassan Galadari and father to the three brothers who would establish the Galadari Brothers Group. Also known as Ebrahim Galadari, he continued the family's merchant tradition established by his father in the late 19th century. Ibrahim raised his sons in Iran, where Abdul Latif Galadari was born in 1939.

Ibrahim's role was crucial in maintaining the family's commercial legacy between the founding generation of Hassan Galadari and the expansion generation of his three sons: Abdul Rahim, Abdul Latif, and Abdul Wahab. His sons would go on to establish one of the UAE's most successful business conglomerates in the early 1960s.

notableFigures.name: Abdul Rahim E. Galadari
notableFigures.title: Co-founder of Galadari Brothers Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Elder brother and co-founder of the Galadari business empire
notableFigures.significance: Co-founded the Galadari Brothers Group in the early 1960s and helped build one of the UAE's largest business conglomerates.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdul Rahim E. Galadari was the elder brother of Abdul Latif Galadari and co-founder of the Galadari Brothers Group, established in the early 1960s. Along with his younger brothers Abdul Latif and Abdul Wahab, he built what would become one of the UAE's most successful business conglomerates. The brothers were involved in the gold business, which made them multimillionaires by the 1970s.

Abdul Rahim and his brothers were well-positioned to take advantage of Dubai's 1970s oil boom, establishing major enterprises including the Intercontinental Hotel, Dubai Bank, a Mazda vehicle dealership, the Khaleej Times newspaper, and numerous construction, engineering, and trading companies. His son Mohammed Abdul Rahim Galadari attended Abdul Latif's funeral in 2002, representing the continuing family legacy.
achievements: Co-founded Galadari Brothers Group in early 1960s
Built multimillion-dollar gold business by 1970s
Established major hotels, banks, and media companies

notableFigures.biography:
Abdul Wahab E. Galadari was one of the three founding brothers of the Galadari Brothers Group, established in the early 1960s alongside his brothers Abdul Rahim and Abdul Latif. The three brothers initially worked together to build their business empire, starting with the gold trade that made them multimillionaires by the 1970s.

In 1976, the brothers underwent a major restructuring when Abdul Wahab was bought out by Abdul Rahim and Abdul Latif, who retained control of the business. This split allowed the remaining two brothers to continue developing what would become one of the UAE's largest conglomerates, while Abdul Wahab pursued separate business interests.
achievements: Co-founded Galadari Brothers Group in early 1960s

notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed Abdul Rahim Galadari is the nephew of Abdul Latif Galadari and son of Abdul Rahim Galadari, representing the third generation of the prominent Galadari business family. He attended the funeral of his uncle Abdul Latif Galadari in Dubai in March 2002, demonstrating the continued close family ties and his role as a representative of the younger generation.

As a member of the third generation, Mohammed Abdul Rahim Galadari represents the continuation of the family legacy established by his grandfather Ibrahim and great-grandfather Hassan Galadari in the 19th century.

notableFigures.name: Suhail Galadari
notableFigures.title: Director, Khaleej Times and UAE Cricketer

notableFigures.significance: Current Director of Khaleej Times newspaper and former UAE cricket team member, representing the third generation of the Galadari family in both business and sports.
notableFigures.biography:
Suhail Galadari is the son of Abdul Latif Galadari and currently serves as Director of the Khaleej Times, the English-language daily newspaper founded by his father and uncles as part of the Galadari Brothers Group empire. He represents the third generation of the family continuing their media interests and business operations.

In addition to his business role, Suhail Galadari has been involved in UAE cricket, being listed among prominent Emirati cricketers. He attended his father's funeral in Dubai in March 2002, and has continued to uphold the family's prominence in both the business and sporting communities of the UAE.
achievements: Director of Khaleej Times newspaper
Former UAE cricket team member

history: The Galadari family is one of the prominent Huwala Arab families of Dubai, with origins tracing back to their role as merchants in the Arabian Peninsula and the Trucial States. Originally, the family became a vassal house of the Al Qawasem dynasty through royal intermarriage and migrated to the Fars province area of Lengeh in Persia in the 1800s in concordance with the Qawasem. The family settled in a village which was later called Galleh Dar (Persian: گله‌دار) pertaining to the family name, with the etymology originally given to them by the Persians to denote their socioeconomic profession of shepherding. The Galadaris established extensive trade ports between Dubai, Kuwait, India, and Iran, accumulating vast wealth through their trading operations. After the fall of Al Qawasem dynasty rule in Lengeh and increased hostility from the Persians towards Iranian Arabs, they resettled in their hometown Dubai permanently. Although a small house, the Galadari family played a significant role in the development of Dubai and were prominent advisers to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum during Dubai's economic boom. The business tradition stretched back to Hassan Galadari, who was a businessman in 1879. The modern Galadari Brothers Group was founded by Abdul Rahim E. Galadari, Abdul Latif E. Galadari, and Abdul Wahab E. Galadari in the early 1960s, establishing numerous companies including the Intercontinental Hotel, Dubai Bank, a Mazda Vehicle Dealership, and the Khaleej Times newspaper. They built GICC, which became the world's largest franchisee for Baskin-Robbins with close to 700 stores across the GCC. In 2015, the Galadari Brothers ranked 27th on a Forbes list of companies in the Middle East.
modernStatus: Major business conglomerate with the Galadari Brothers Group ranking 27th on Forbes Middle East companies list in 2015, controlling extensive retail, media, and hospitality operations across the GCC

They have been instrumental in major Dubai development projects and have achieved significant international expansion.

notableFigures.significance: Led the new administration of Al Wasl FC starting in the 2021-22 season, helping to improve team performance and fan relations.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmad Bin Shafar was appointed to lead a new administration for Al Wasl Football Club for the 2021-22 season. His appointment came after a period of poor results that had led to mutual bad feeling between the club and its fans, with supporters campaigning for a change in the administration and board of directors.

Under his leadership, the club saw significant improvements in both fan relations and team performance. With the fans placated by the new administration, attendances rose, and the team's spirit and performance improved, though they only achieved 6th place in the league that season. His tenure marked the beginning of a turnaround for the club that would eventually lead to their most successful period in years.
achievements: Led Al Wasl FC administration reform
Improved fan relations and attendance

history: The Al Shafar family emerges in the research as a prominent construction and contracting dynasty in the UAE, with Al Shafar Transport and Contracting Company being established as a major player in Dubai's development sector. The family's business prominence is evidenced through their role in significant Dubai projects, including being awarded construction contracts for Al Furjan development by Nakheel in 2008. When construction was halted in 2009 due to economic pressures, Al Shafar was notably the first contractor to restart work, demonstrating their resilience and strong market position. The family expanded their operations into a comprehensive construction conglomerate, with ASGC (Al Shafar General Contracting Co. LLC) being established in 1989 as part of the larger Al Shafar Group, which encompasses 26 companies. Under the leadership of Mohamed Saif Bin Shafar as chairman, the company has grown to achieve annual revenues of $1 billion by 2020 with 17,000 employees. ASGC has worked on high-profile projects including Dubai's Ministry of Public Works building, Dubai Police headquarters, sectors of Jumeirah Beach Residence, and the Mohammad Bin Rashid Library. The company has also expanded internationally, becoming a major shareholder in the UK's Costain Group in 2020, though they later sold this stake in 2024. The research indicates the business was established through a partnership between a Dubai national and an Egyptian engineer, suggesting possible local Emirati origins for the Al Shafar family, though their specific tribal or ethnic background is not detailed in the available sources.
modernStatus: Leading construction and business conglomerate with Mohamed Saif Bin Shafar as chairman of ASGC Construction, generating $1 billion annual revenue with 17,000 employees across 26 companies in the Al Shafar Group

name: Al Gaz
originStory: The family belongs to the Al Falasi tribe, with Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz born in Al Dhagaya, Deira around 1920 to Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi.
description: Al Falasi merchant family known through Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz, pioneering Dubai businessman, central banker, and philanthropist who helped establish the UAE's financial institutions and first charitable society.

notableFigures.name: Mohammad Abdallah Al Gaz

notableFigures.roleDescription: Financier, developer, diplomat, central banker and philanthropist
notableFigures.significance: A pioneering figure in UAE financial development and philanthropy who helped establish the country's first charitable institution.
notableFigures.biography:
Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz was born in Al Dhagaya, Deira in 1920 to a modest family. His father, Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi, died unexpectedly when AlGaz was an adolescent, but his mother enrolled him in the Al Ahmadiya School. During World War II famine, he temporarily settled in Bahrain with his family while working as a carpenter in Dammam, using his earnings to start a commodity trading operation.

Emerging as an enterprising trader in textiles and commodities, AlGaz entered the historic Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade in the 1950s after partnering with Juma Al Majid to establish the famous Mohamed & Juma Al Majid company. He rose to political power as a trusted advisor of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum and was nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council on July 7, 1958. After the gold trade declined, he and Al Majid secured franchise rights for international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Phillips, Opel, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia before splitting their assets amicably after 11 years.

Post-federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 into the mid-1990s under Chairman Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was instrumental in UAE's economic development and philanthropy, building schools including the Amna bint Wahb Girls School and Gamal Abdulnasser Boys School. In 1989, he developed with Juma Al Majid the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair. He died at age 96 on April 12, 2016, at Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi.
achievements: First Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE (1980-1990s)
Co-founder of Beit Al Khair, UAE's first charitable society (1989)
Built the first three-story building in Dubai's history
Developed much of the property around Port Said and Hamriya in Bur Deira
UAE Pioneers Award (2018, posthumous)
President's Merit Award (2012)
Dubai Appreciation Award for Community Service (2022, posthumous)

history: The Al Gaz family's documented history centers on Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz (c. 1920-2016), born in Al Dhagaya, Deira, to a modest family. His father, Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi, died when AlGaz was an adolescent, indicating the family's Al Falasi tribal connection. During World War II's famine in Dubai, AlGaz's family temporarily relocated to Bahrain while he found work as a carpenter in Dammam, using his earnings to start commodity trading operations between Bahrain, Kuwait, and Dubai. In the 1950s, AlGaz formed a historic partnership with Juma Al Majid, establishing the Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade through their company Mohamed & Juma Al Majid. This partnership secured exclusive distribution rights for major brands including General Electric, Samsung, Phillips, Opel, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia. After eleven years, they amicably split their assets, with AlGaz focusing on real estate development, building Dubai's first three-story building and developing areas around Port Said including the clock-tower plaza and Hamriya in Bur Deira. Post-UAE federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 through the mid-1990s under Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was also nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council on July 7, 1958, serving on the budget and treasury committee, and acted as a diplomatic adviser to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum. AlGaz became a pioneering philanthropist, helping establish the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair, in 1989 with Juma Al Majid, and building schools including the Amna bint Wahb Girls School and Gamal Abdulnasser Boys School. The family's Al Falasi tribal heritage connects them to the broader Al Maktoum ruling family lineage of Dubai.
modernStatus: Historical prominence through Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz's legacy in UAE banking and philanthropy; he died in 2016 and is listed among notable Al Falasi members

migrationPath.description: AlGaz family relocated to Bahrain during WWII famine, while Mohamed worked as carpenter in Dammam

history: The Sajwani family is prominently represented by Hussain Sajwani, born in Dubai in 1952/53 to a middle-class family. He was the eldest of five children, and his early business experience came from working in his father's shop selling watches, pens, and goods imported from China. After initially studying medicine at Baghdad University on a government scholarship, he transferred to the University of Washington where he graduated with a degree in industrial engineering in 1981. Sajwani began his professional career in 1981 as a contracts manager at GASCO (Abu Dhabi Gas Industries), a subsidiary of ADNOC. After two years, he founded his own catering business in 1983, providing food services to major institutions including the US Army and Bechtel Corporation, which continues to operate as Global Logistics Services. In 2002, he founded DAMAC Properties, which became one of the largest real estate development companies in the Middle East. The company went public on the Dubai Financial Market in 2015 and was ranked by Forbes Global 2000 in 2017 as the world's fastest-growing company based on annual compound growth rate from 2013-2016. Sajwani has been consistently ranked among the most influential Arab personalities by Gulf Business magazine and Arabian Business, and was listed among the top 10 richest Arabs by Forbes in 2021 with a wealth estimated at $2.5 billion. In 2022, he acquired Swiss jewelry company De Grisogono, and in 2025 announced a $20 billion investment commitment for data center development in the United States.
modernStatus: Major real estate development empire through DAMAC Properties, with global operations and investments in luxury hospitality, fashion (Roberto Cavalli), and jewelry (De Grisogono)

notableFigures.biography:
Mohammed Farooq, known as The Great Farooq, was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil. After his father died, Mohammed Farooq took over as the head of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. Today Mohammed Farooq is remembered as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf.

As Coles and Jakson put it, 'The Great Farooq's generosity and hospitality earned him respect and a reputable status amongst Gulf royalty.' He had close ties with the rulers of Arab States of the Persian Gulf, namely the late Sheikh Mohammed Bin Saqer Al Qasimi, the former ruler of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah and the late Sheikh Saeed Bin Maktoum Al Maktoum, The former Ruler of Dubai. His daughter Faiqa Farooq became the Arab world's first female ambassador of the United Nations.
achievements: Most famous pearl merchant of the Persian Gulf
Built close relationships with Gulf royalty
Expanded family business internationally

notableFigures.biography:
Ahmed Al Arshi was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil and brother to Mohammed Farooq (The Great Farooq). After their father Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as the heads of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones.

Ahmed Al Arshi worked alongside his more famous brother in building the family's international trading empire. The family branches are closely related, with the Farooq Al Arshi family being first cousins of the Al Olema family, also known as the Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and the Al Khaldi family.

He has achieved recognition as both a renowned poet and medical doctor, representing the family's continued success across multiple professional fields.

As the rapidly expanding family business has become a truly global brand through various members of the respectable family, Dr. Mohammed Zubair represents the intellectual and cultural contributions of the newer generation, maintaining the family's reputation for excellence while pursuing careers in medicine and literature.

history: The Al Farooq (Arabic: فاروق) family are a Sunni Muslim business family originating from Saudi Arabia whose ancestral home was later Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran before eventually migrating to Dubai. They are amongst the first of UAE's business and aristocratic families. As of 2014, the majority of the family members reside in the UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, the United States and Canada. The Al Farooq family led by Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil was part of the first wave of migration which started in 1899 due to the introduction of customs tax by the Persian government under the Qajar dynasty which led to reduced profits for merchants. Political instability in 1909, and a flu epidemic in 1918 saw the heads of most merchant families from Bastak relocate to the Persian Gulf region, Karachi and the Indian Ocean regions. After Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq, known as The Great Farooq and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as the heads of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. Mohammed Farooq is remembered as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf. After Mohammed Farooq died, his son Abdulkarim Farooq became the heir to the family and was an advisor to the former rulers across the Persian Gulf. Abdulkarim Farooq owned a general trading establishment in Al Bastakiya, Dubai known as London store which specialized in importing quality brands directly from London, UK. He was regarded as the 'wealthiest man in Dubai and owned symbols of affluence which included a yacht'. The Al Farooq family has a history of association with humanitarian causes, with the Bait Farooq in Bahrain playing host to events that contributed to society, including a mass meeting in 1948 that provided initial impetus for Bahrain's support for Palestine. Today, the family's generosity is evident through various landmarks including mosques and the Farooq House part of the Dubai creek heritage in Al Bastakiya, Dubai.
modernStatus: The rapidly expanding family business is now a truly global brand through various members. Family members that currently rule the business include Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq. Two-thirds of the family currently resides in the UAE.

notableFigures.significance: Award-winning Emirati poet and artist, Harvard graduate, and cultural ambassador who won the ADMAF Creativity Award for her poetry collection.
notableFigures.biography:
Shamma Al Bastaki was born in Dubai in 1996 and is an Emirati poet and artist. She holds a master's degree from Harvard University and a bachelor's degree from New York University Abu Dhabi, where she double majored in Social Research and Public Policy as well as Literature and Creative Writing. She completed diplomatic training at the Anwar Gargash Diplomatic Academy, earning a PgD in Diplomacy and International Relations in 2021.

Al Bastaki has been writing poetry since she was seven years old and has written hundreds of poems. She completed her first full volume of poetry 'House to House بيت لبيت' in 2018, based on oral histories from the Dubai Creek communities. In 2019, she won the ADMAF Creativity Award for this poetry collection, an excerpt of which was published in the Asymptote Journal and taught in universities in Japan, Taiwan, and the US. She is also an ambassador for Louvre Abu Dhabi and co-founded several literary networks, including JARA Collective and Untitled Chapters. Her thesis 'Al-Majaz: A Crossing' won the Best Thesis Award at the Harvard Center for Middle Eastern Studies.

notableFigures.significance: Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who appeared in Kuwaiti television and highlighted Achomi cultural identity through her performances.
notableFigures.biography:
Huda Al-Khatib is an Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who has appeared in television productions across the Gulf region. She notably appeared in the Kuwaiti TV comedy drama serial 'Al-Da'la' (الدعلة) where she spoke broken Arabic mixed with Persian/Achomi, bringing attention to the cultural identity of Achomi migrants and their linguistic heritage.

In her role, she was shown bringing her giddu (گِدو; old traditional Iranian hookah) and raging out in Achomi and Persian. The show featured a full scene in the 24th Episode where her character was shown learning the Arabic language and sang the Laristani/Achomi/Bastaki song 'Ghalyon ma teshn, ghori ma chai, yar nazanin, jaye to khali' by Yousif Hadi Bastaki. Her performances highlighted the challenges faced by Achomi migrants in adapting to Arabic-speaking environments while preserving their linguistic and musical heritage.

history: The Al Bastaki family is of Persian Achomi origin, tracing their ancestry to the Iranian city of Bastak in southern Fars Province. They are part of the broader Achomi community, Persian-speaking people from Irahistan (including Bastak, Khonj, Evaz, and Jenah) who migrated to the Arabian Peninsula over several centuries. The family follows Sunni Islam, specifically the Shafi'i and Maliki schools of jurisprudence, which distinguished them from the majority Shia populations in southern Iran. The Al Bastaki established themselves as influential merchants and builders in the UAE, playing a crucial role in the development of Dubai's historic Al Bastakiya district, which was named after their hometown of Bastak. Mohammed Al Bastaki supervised the construction of Qasr Al Hosn in Abu Dhabi in 1761, the oldest stone building in the UAE that served as a watchtower and later became the ruling family's palace. The family benefited from the UAE's 1972 Citizenship Law, which granted nationality to Iranians residing in the Trucial States prior to 1925, formally recognizing their status as Ajam Emiratis. Today, the Al Bastaki family includes notable figures in business, media, entertainment, and medicine, representing the successful integration of Persian merchant families into modern Emirati society while maintaining their cultural heritage through Persian culinary traditions, architectural styles, and family surnames that reflect their Bastaki origins.
modernStatus: Established Emirati family with members in business, entertainment, medicine, and media, including businesswoman Khadija Al-Bastaki, singer Shahad Al-Bastaki, and TV presenter Moein Al-Bastaki

notableFigures.biography:
Fady Jameel serves as President of Community Jameel, the Abdul Latif Jameel family's international philanthropic organization. Along with Hassan Jameel, he has been a key figure in launching major academic and research collaborations that bear the family name. In October 2019, he participated in the signing ceremony that established the Abdul Latif Jameel Institute for Disease and Emergency Analytics at Imperial College London, alongside Imperial's president Alice Gast and Hassan Jameel.

Fady was also present at the September 2018 founding of the MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, participating in the signing ceremony at MIT with MIT President L. Rafael Reif and Hassan Jameel. His leadership in Community Jameel has been crucial in establishing the organization as a major force in international philanthropy, supporting initiatives in health, education, poverty alleviation, and scientific research across multiple continents.
