history: The Abdul Latif Jameel family represents one of Saudi Arabia's most successful business dynasties, founded by Sheikh Abdul Latif Jameel (1909-1993) in Jeddah in 1945. Abdul Latif Jameel was the son of Hussein Jameel Mohammed Al-Saadi, and had brothers Abdullah and Abdul Aziz, the latter being one of the founders of Al-Ittihad football club in Jeddah. Starting with simple goods purchased at public auctions in Jeddah's Islamic port alongside his friend Abdullah Hashem, Abdul Latif built a small car sales company that achieved a major breakthrough in 1955 when he secured the Toyota distributorship for Saudi Arabia, introducing the first four-wheel drive 'BJ' model from Toyota. He became the first to establish an automotive parts business in Mecca and expanded to secure agencies for global brands like Toshiba. Upon his death in 1993, his son Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel assumed leadership as Chairman and CEO. The family business has grown into a diversified conglomerate operating across seven core sectors in over 30 countries, with Mohammed's sons Fady serving as Vice Chairman International and Hassan as Vice Chairman of Saudi operations. Hassan Jameel, born 1979-1980, studied at Sophia University in Tokyo and London Business School, trained with Toyota in Japan, and became instrumental in the company's major investment in electric vehicle startup Rivian. The family has established significant philanthropic initiatives including Community Jameel (founded 2003), which supports the MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab, MIT Jameel Clinic, and various educational and poverty alleviation programs globally.
modernStatus: Led by Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel as Chairman, with sons Fady and Hassan as Vice Chairmen. The conglomerate operates across transportation, manufacturing, financial services, energy, real estate, advertising, and consumer goods sectors globally.

notableFigures.biography:
Walid Ahmed Juffali was born in Jeddah on 30 April 1955, the son of Ahmed Abdullah Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. His mother was Suad bint Ibrahim Al Husseini, granddaughter of Kamil of Jerusalem and niece of Hajj Amin of Jerusalem. After completing elementary and secondary education between Jeddah and Switzerland, he received a bachelor's degree in International Business and Political Sciences from the University of San Diego in 1977. He later earned a doctorate in neurosciences from Imperial College in 2012, with his PhD thesis entitled 'A Novel Algorithm for Detection and Prediction of Neural Anomalies.'

Walid assumed the chairmanship of E. A. Juffali and Brothers after his father's death in 1994. He also served as chairman of Saudi American Bank, The Saudi Cement Company, and sat on boards of Saudi Hollandi Bank and many other companies. In 2005, he was appointed chair of Dow Chemical Arabia and Siemens Arabia. He owned his personal investment company 'W Investments' and had significant stakes in major international companies, including 5% of Hitachi and 11% of Manchester United. Walid developed a great interest in brain theory and launched the annual brain forum conference, which by 2016 hosted over 1,400 participants from 31 countries.

Walid's personal life included high-profile divorces, including a £75 million settlement with Christina Estrada in 2016, which was the largest such settlement in English legal history at the time. He died in Zürich, Switzerland on 20 July 2016 after a lengthy battle with cancer. His estate was inherited by his brother Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali, who also became the legal guardian of his five daughters.

Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali is one of the sons of Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali. After his brother Walid's death in July 2016, Khalid became the inheritor of Walid's estate and took on the responsibility of being the legal guardian of Walid's five daughters.

As part of the Al-Juffali family, Khalid belongs to the wealthy and secretive family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, who are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty. The family built one of Saudi Arabia's most successful business empires through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became the largest privately owned enterprise in the country.

He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali. As part of the Al-Juffali family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, Tarek belongs to the wealthy business dynasty that are members of the Bani Khalid.

The Al-Juffali family built their business empire through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise. The family has been instrumental in introducing various international companies and industries to Saudi Arabia, including power generation, telecommunications, and vehicle manufacturing.

notableFigures.title: Racing Driver

notableFigures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian racing driver who competed as a wildcard entry in the 2024 F1 Academy season at Jeddah.
notableFigures.biography:
Reema Juffali is a Saudi Arabian racing driver born in 1992. She made history as one of the pioneering female racing drivers from Saudi Arabia, competing in GT racing before moving into single-seater competition. In 2024, she participated in the F1 Academy season as a wildcard entry for the opening round at the Jeddah Street Circuit.

During her F1 Academy debut weekend in Jeddah, Reema faced challenges typical of motorsport competition. In the first race, she was involved in a collision with Amna Al Qubaisi on lap 2, and in the second race, she crashed out on lap 6, which caused the sole safety car period of that race. Despite the difficulties, her participation represented an important milestone for women in motorsport in Saudi Arabia and the broader Middle East region.
achievements: Pioneering female racing driver from Saudi Arabia

history: The Al Juffali family originates from Unayzah in Al-Qassim province of Najd, and are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty, who ruled Eastern Arabia from the early 1600s to the late 1800s before suffering military defeats against the Saudis. The family claims descent from Khālid ibn al-Walīd, the famous Arab military commander. The modern business empire was founded by Abdullah bin Ibrahim Al Juffali, whose sons Ibrahim and Ali initially oversaw the family's electricity operations (later becoming Saudi Electricity Company). The most prominent founder was Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali (1924-1994), who established E.A. Juffali and Brothers in 1946, which became Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise. Ahmed was instrumental in founding SAMA (Saudi Arabian Monetary Authority) and served on boards of international companies including IBM and Wells Fargo. The company expanded rapidly during Saudi Arabia's oil boom, introducing Mercedes-Benz, Siemens, IBM, and numerous other multinational brands to the Saudi market. After Ahmed's death in 1994, leadership passed to his sons Walid (1955-2016) and Khaled (born 1958), who continued expanding the business empire. Walid became chairman of Saudi American Bank and developed interests in neuroscience, while Khaled serves as managing partner and vice chairman. The family maintains significant philanthropic activities and has been involved in various humanitarian projects throughout the Middle East.
modernStatus: Controls Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise E.A. Juffali and Brothers with 49,218 employees worldwide, representing major international brands across multiple industries including automotive, technology, and manufacturing

notableFigures.significance: Founded the American Muslim Council and was involved in a plot to assassinate Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdul Rahman Al-Amoudi was born in the Province of Eritrea in Ethiopia and moved to the United States in 1979, becoming a U.S. citizen in 1996. Before his arrest, he was one of America's best-known Muslim activists who met with senior Clinton and Bush administration officials and founded the American Muslim Foundation. He played a role in establishing a U.S. Department of Defense Muslim chaplain program and was invited by the Bush White House to speak at a Washington National Cathedral prayer service after 9/11.

In 2003, Al-Amoudi was indicted for illegal dealings with Libya and involvement in a plot financed by Muammar Gaddafi to assassinate Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah. He pleaded guilty in 2004 to three federal criminal charges including violating terrorism-related sanctions and was sentenced to 23 years in prison. He was automatically stripped of his U.S. citizenship following his conviction.
achievements: Founded American Muslim Council
Established U.S. Department of Defense Muslim chaplain program

notableFigures.name: Muhammad Sa'eed Al Amoudi
notableFigures.title: Saudi Arabian Journalist and Literary Critic

notableFigures.significance: A pioneering writer in 20th-century Hejaz, known for classical Arabic text editing and literary criticism.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad bin Sa'eed Al-Amoudi was born in Mecca in 1905 to a merchant family descended from the Hejazi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Bakri. After graduating from Al-Falah School, he initially worked in commerce with his father before transitioning to government positions. He headed the editorial board of the General Post and Telegraph Authority and served as editor-in-chief of its magazine until 1971.

From 1951 to 1954, he was a member of the Saudi Consultative Assembly and later served as editor-in-chief of the Muslim World League magazine from 1965 to 1978. He was chosen by the Ministry of Education for various committees and was one of the founding members of the Publishing and Writing Committee. Al-Amoudi was recognized as an inspiring poet and distinguished writer, publishing works on history, poetry, and short stories. He died in Mecca on February 16, 1991, after a long illness at age 86.

notableFigures.biography:
Omar Abdulrahman was born in Riyadh in 1991 to a working-class family from the Al-Amoudi lineage, descending from 13th century Sufi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi. After being spotted by a scout at Al Malaz yard, he joined Al Ain's youth academy in 2008 when his family was granted UAE citizenship. He made his professional debut in 2009 and became a first-team regular despite suffering multiple cruciate ligament injuries.

Throughout his career, Abdulrahman won multiple UAE Pro-League titles with Al Ain and was voted Emirati Player of the Year multiple times. He had a two-week trial with Manchester City in 2012 and later played for Al Hilal, Al Jazira, and Shabab Al-Ahli. Internationally, he represented the UAE in major tournaments including the 2012 Olympics, 2015 AFC Asian Cup, and won the 2013 Arabian Gulf Cup where he was named Player of the Tournament. ESPN FC ranked him first among the top ten Asian players of 2012.
achievements: UAE Pro-League champion multiple times
Arabian Gulf Cup winner and MVP (2013)
AFC Asian Cup third place (2015)
Emirati Player of the Year multiple times
ESPN FC Top Asian Player (2012)

notableFigures.name: Samia al-Amoudi
notableFigures.title: Obstetrician, Gynecologist and Healthcare Activist
notableFigures.roleDescription: Head of Sheikh Mohammed Hussein Al-Amoudi Center of Excellence in breast cancer
notableFigures.significance: First woman from the Gulf Cooperation Council to sit on the Union for International Cancer Control board and pioneering breast cancer awareness advocate.
notableFigures.biography:
Samia al-Amoudi graduated from King Abdulaziz University's medical college in 1981, among the first doctors from that institution. In April 2006, she diagnosed herself with breast cancer and underwent chemotherapy, completing treatment in 2012. She became the first Saudi woman to write publicly about her experience with the disease, publishing a series of articles in Al Madinah newspaper and appearing on daily TV shows to raise awareness.

Since 2012, she has served as head of the Sheikh Mohammed Hussein Al-Amoudi Center of Excellence in breast cancer and scientific chair for breast cancer at King Abdulaziz University. She has been recognized internationally for her advocacy work, receiving the International Women of Courage Award from U.S. Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice in 2007. Arabian Business ranked her among the most powerful Arab women, placing her 5th on their Power 100 list in 2010.
achievements: International Women of Courage Award (2007)
Ranked 5th on Arabian Business Power 100 list (2010)
First Gulf woman on Union for International Cancer Control board
Published 'Breast Cancer, Break the Silence' (2007)

history: The Al Amoudi family traces its origins to Sheikh Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi (1203-1272 CE), a prominent Sunni Shafi'i Islamic scholar from Hadhramaut, Yemen. Born in Qaydun in the Wadi Daw'an region, he was descended from the first Muslim Caliph Abu Bakr As-Siddiq through a lineage that includes Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Bakri, known as 'Amoududdin' (Pillar of the religion), from whom the family name derives. Sheikh Sa'eed's father, Sheikh Isa ibn Ahmad Al-Amoudi, was originally a Mufti of Medina who migrated to spread Islamic learning, eventually settling in Hadhramaut where he married into the Bani Muhammad of the Himyarite Siban tribe. Sheikh Sa'eed became a renowned Sufi master, founding the Tariqah Al-Amoudiyyah and allegedly receiving the Staff of Moses and Coat of Joseph from his teacher Muhammad ibn Ali Ba'Alawi. His descendants established the Al-Amoudi state in Wadi Daw'an from 1531-1899 CE, with their capital at Budha village. The family's modern prominence emerged through Mohammed Hussein Al Amoudi, born in Ethiopia in 1946 to a Yemeni father from Hadhramaut and an Ethiopian mother. He migrated to Saudi Arabia with his brother and became a Saudi citizen, building a business empire worth $9.3 billion through construction, oil refineries, mining, and agriculture across Saudi Arabia, Ethiopia, Sweden, and Morocco through his companies MIDROC and Corral Petroleum Holdings.
modernStatus: Global business empire led by Mohammed Hussein Al Amoudi with interests in construction, oil refining, mining, agriculture, and manufacturing across multiple countries, employing over 70,000 people.

migrationPath.description: Mohammed Hussein Al Amoudi migrated from Ethiopia to Saudi Arabia with his brother Mauricet

name: Bin Mahfouz
originStory: The family originates from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, with patriarch Salim Ahmed bin Mahfouz establishing himself in Saudi Arabia through the money-changing business before founding the country's first private bank.
description: A prominent Saudi banking family of Hadhramaut Yemeni origin who founded and controlled the National Commercial Bank, Saudi Arabia's first private bank. The family achieved billionaire status but faced significant controversies regarding financial irregularities and terrorism financing allegations.

notableFigures.name: Salim Ahmed bin Al Shaiba bin Mahfouz
notableFigures.title: Founder of National Commercial Bank
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded Saudi Arabia's first private bank and served as personal banker to the Saudi royal family

notableFigures.name: Salem Bin Mahfouz
notableFigures.title: Founder of National Commercial Bank
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi entrepreneur and banking pioneer
notableFigures.significance: Founded the first private Saudi bank and became personal banker to the Saudi royal family.
notableFigures.biography: Salem Ahmed bin Mahfouz was a Saudi entrepreneur who rose from being an illiterate small-time money-changer to becoming the founder of the National Commercial Bank (NCB), the first private bank in Saudi Arabia. He established himself as a significant figure in Saudi banking and finance, eventually becoming the personal banker of the Saudi royal family. His success in banking laid the foundation for one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent business families. Salem handed management of NCB, which became the largest bank in the country, to his son Khalid sometime in the 1980s, establishing a banking dynasty that would continue to influence Saudi finance for decades.
achievements: Founded the National Commercial Bank, the first private Saudi bank
Became personal banker to the Saudi royal family
Built one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent business families

notableFigures.name: Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz
notableFigures.title: Saudi Arabian Businessman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Businessman and philanthropist with ties to British institutions
notableFigures.significance: Saudi businessman involved in philanthropy and British institutions, later subject to cash-for-honors allegations.
notableFigures.biography:
Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz was born on December 14, 1969, in Mecca, Saudi Arabia. He became a prominent businessman with extensive connections to British institutions and charitable organizations. He was made a Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford in 2012 and became the inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge in July 2013. The Mahfouz Building at Pembroke College, Oxford was named after him, as was a room at Wolfson College, Cambridge. He was admitted to the Freedom of the City of London in December 2013 and served as a Vice-President of the British Forces Foundation.

Mahfouz became involved with Prince Charles's charitable organizations, serving as an Ambassador and Community Patron for The Prince's Foundation. He donated over £1.5 million to royal charities and met with Prince Harry in 2013 and 2014, contributing £50,000 to Harry's charity Sentebale and £10,000 to Walking With The Wounded. However, Harry stated in 2021 that he severed ties with Mahfouz in 2015 after expressing 'growing concerns' about his motives. Mahfouz was awarded an honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 2016 'for services to charities in the UK,' though this later became subject to cash-for-honors allegations involving claims that the honor was obtained through donations rather than merit.
achievements: Made Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford (2012)
Became inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge (2013)
Awarded honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (2016)
Admitted to the Freedom of the City of London (2013)

history: The Bin Mahfouz family originates from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, as confirmed by research indicating that Salim Ahmed bin Mahfouz was 'of Hadharem origin.' Salim bin Ahmed bin Al Shaiba bin Mahfouz began his career as a governor in Mecca before entering the money-changing business in partnership with the Al-Kaki family. Recognizing his business acumen, he proposed the establishment of Saudi Arabia's first bank during a newspaper meeting, which impressed the Minister of Finance Ibn Suleiman. This led to a meeting with King Abdulaziz Al Saud, who approved the concept, resulting in the founding of the Saudi National Commercial Bank (NCB), which became the largest banking institution in the Middle East. Salim's son Khalid bin Mahfouz inherited management of NCB in the 1980s and became a billionaire with an estimated net worth of $6 billion, ranking 24th in Arabian Business magazine's list of the world's most influential Arabs in 2008. The family's wealth extended internationally, with Khalid acquiring Irish citizenship through investment procedures in 1990 and owning substantial properties in Houston, Texas, including a $3.5 million chateau-style house and a 44,000-acre ranch. However, the family faced significant controversies, including Khalid's $225 million settlement related to the BCCI scandal in 1995 and various allegations regarding terrorism financing, though he successfully defended against many of these claims through legal action. Following Salim's death in 1994, his children established the Salim Bin Mahfouz Foundation for Civil and Development Work in 2012, continuing the family's philanthropic legacy.
modernStatus: The family retained 34% ownership of NCB after the Saudi government acquired controlling interest in 1999, though they surrendered management positions. The Salim Bin Mahfouz Foundation continues their philanthropic work.

migrationPath.description: Salim Ahmed bin Mahfouz migrated from Hadhramaut to establish himself in Mecca as a governor and money-changer

name: Bugshan
originStory: The family established themselves in Khailah within the Wadi Dawan branch of the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, where they built distinctive palaces with elaborate oil-based painted decorations.
description: A prominent Hadhrami family known for their elaborate palaces in Wadi Dawan and their modern conglomerate business operations with international reach.

notableFigures.name: Khalid Ali Bugshan
notableFigures.title: Saudi Businessman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman involved in art transactions and legal proceedings
notableFigures.significance: Saudi businessman who arranged art sales and was questioned in connection with French political corruption investigations.
notableFigures.biography:
Khalid Ali Bugshan is a Saudi businessman who became involved in high-profile French political investigations in the 2010s. He was connected to the sale of two Andries van Eertvelt paintings that were allegedly sold to a Malaysian attorney, with the transaction being arranged by Bugshan according to Claude Guéant's claims.

In March 2015, Bugshan was taken into police custody for questioning by French authorities in connection with the investigation into alleged Libyan financing of Nicolas Sarkozy's 2007 presidential campaign. While he was not charged, his arrest came on the same day as Claude Guéant, Sarkozy's former chief of staff, who claimed that money he received was payment for the art sale that Bugshan had arranged. Bugshan's involvement highlighted the complex web of international business relationships that became scrutinized during the French political corruption investigations.

notableFigures.name: Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan
notableFigures.title: Chairman of Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman and cultural patron funding Yemeni musical heritage projects
notableFigures.significance: Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation and is the main sponsor of the Heritage Symphonies cultural project promoting Yemeni folk music.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan is a prominent Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation. He is the principal financial backer of the Heritage Symphonies project, a major cultural initiative founded in 2019 that aims to promote and disseminate Yemeni folk music by integrating it into orchestral performances that combine traditional instruments with Western orchestral elements.

Bugshan's foundation has provided substantial funding for multiple international concerts, including performances in Malaysia, Egypt, France, Qatar, Kuwait, and other countries. The project has achieved significant commercial success, with concerts regularly selling out across the Arab world. However, his involvement has also drawn criticism from some Yemeni music critics who have questioned whether such generous funding might have been more effectively invested in reviving local musical institutions in Yemen, and who have suggested that his financial influence has affected the project's musical execution and vision.
achievements: Founded and chairs the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
Principal sponsor of the internationally successful Heritage Symphonies project
Funded major orchestral concerts promoting Yemeni culture across multiple countries

history: The Bugshan family is prominently mentioned in the research as a wealthy conglomerate with elaborate palaces in Khailah, within a branch of Wadi Dawan in the Hadhramaut region of Yemen. Their palaces are specifically noted for their elaborate oil-based painted decorations on exteriors, distinguishing them architecturally from other structures in the area. The family appears to have established significant wealth and influence in the Hadhramaut region, with their architectural legacy serving as a notable example of affluent Hadhrami construction. In modern times, the Bugshan family conglomerate has maintained its business prominence and has been identified as a client of high-profile legal services, including those of former French Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin's law firm, indicating their continued significance in international business circles.
modernStatus: International business conglomerate with legal representation by high-profile international law firms

name: Alireza
originStory: The House of Alireza originated from Laristan in southern Iran, arriving in Jeddah during the 1840s as Persian merchants.
description: Multiple distinct Alireza lineages including the House of Alireza merchant family from Iranian Laristan who founded Saudi Arabia's oldest commercial enterprise, and various Iranian political and religious figures.

notableFigures.name: Zainal Alireza
notableFigures.title: Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded the House of Alireza trading business in 1845, establishing Saudi Arabia's oldest organized commercial enterprise

notableFigures.name: Abdullah Alireza Zainal
notableFigures.title: Kaymakam of Jeddah
notableFigures.roleDescription: Served as governor of Jeddah during Hashemite and early al Saud rule, later became senior advisor to King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz

notableFigures.name: Muhammad Alireza
notableFigures.title: Minister of Commerce
notableFigures.roleDescription: Second Minister of Commerce from 1953 to 1958, later served as Saudi ambassador to Egypt and France

notableFigures.name: Ali Reza Pahlavi
notableFigures.title: Prince
notableFigures.roleDescription: Younger son of Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, studied ancient Iranian studies and philology at Harvard University

notableFigures.name: Qasim bin Zainal Alireza
notableFigures.title: Ottoman Parliamentarian
notableFigures.roleDescription: Deputy for Jeddah in Ottoman parliament
notableFigures.significance: First Alireza family member to hold political office, serving as deputy for Jeddah in the Ottoman parliament of 1908.
notableFigures.biography:
Qasim bin Zainal Alireza was the son of the family patriarch Zainal Alireza and represented the family's entry into formal political representation. In 1908, he was elected as the deputy for Jeddah within the Ottoman parliament, marking an important milestone in the family's political involvement during the final years of Ottoman rule in the Hijaz region.

His election to the Ottoman parliament demonstrated the family's growing influence and prominence in Jeddah's commercial and political circles. This political engagement would set a precedent for future generations of the Alireza family, who continued to play significant roles in Saudi Arabian politics and governance after the establishment of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
achievements: Elected deputy for Jeddah in Ottoman parliament of 1908

notableFigures.name: Muhammad Ali Zainal Alireza
notableFigures.title: Educator and Pearl Merchant
notableFigures.roleDescription: Educational pioneer and international pearl merchant
notableFigures.significance: Established the first schools in Jeddah (1905) and Mecca (1912) for poor children, and became a successful pearl merchant in Paris and Bombay.
notableFigures.biography:
Muhammad Ali Zainal Alireza, another son of Zainal Alireza, was a pioneering figure in education and international commerce. In 1905, he established the first school in Jeddah for poor children, offering free tuition at a time when education was not widely available. He followed this in 1912 by opening another school in Mecca, also providing free education to underprivileged children. As the region was still part of the Ottoman Empire, the language of instruction was Ottoman Turkish.

After establishing these educational institutions, Muhammad Ali expanded his business interests internationally, becoming a successful pearl merchant with operations in Paris and Bombay. He specialized in importing Persian Gulf pearls to both European and Indian markets, demonstrating the family's early international business acumen. Though his schools were eventually closed due to violations of British laws, his educational legacy marked an important early contribution to learning in the Hijaz region.
achievements: Established first school in Jeddah (1905)
Founded school in Mecca (1912)
Successful pearl merchant in Paris and Bombay

notableFigures.name: Abdullah Alireza
notableFigures.title: Founder of Rezayat Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded major Gulf business conglomerate
notableFigures.significance: Founded the Rezayat Group in Kuwait in 1949, which became a major Gulf business conglomerate with over 16,000 employees.
notableFigures.biography:
Abdullah Alireza was a member of the prominent House of Alireza merchant family and was educated in Bombay before beginning his career as secretary for Karl Twitchell, Director of the Saudi Arabian Mining Syndicate. Twitchell had previously been the local representative of California-Arabian Standard Oil Company (CASOC), one of the partners that created Saudi Aramco in 1933. Abdullah became liaison officer for CASOC, serving King AbdulAziz as an interpreter, most notably at the historic ceremony in Dhahran on 1 May 1939, when the King turned the wheel to load the first shipment of Saudi oil.

During World War II, Abdullah was asked to join the Allied administration in Iran with responsibility for distributing essential foodstuffs and supplies throughout the country. On a visit to Kuwait in 1949, he saw opportunities to develop business in the Kuwait-Saudi Arabia Neutral Zone and decided to base his new group in Kuwait. With the help of his sons Teymour and Fahd, he steadily expanded operations into other Gulf states and Saudi Arabia, building important relationships with major Western and Asian contractors that provided a foundation for growth across multiple continents.
achievements: Interpreter for King AbdulAziz at first Saudi oil shipment ceremony (1939)
Founded Rezayat Group in Kuwait (1949)
Built international business conglomerate with 16,000+ employees

notableFigures.name: Ahmed Alireza
notableFigures.title: Business Leader
notableFigures.roleDescription: Third-generation family business leader
notableFigures.significance: Zainal Alireza's grandson who led the family business interests until his death in 2012.
notableFigures.biography:
Ahmed Alireza was the grandson of family patriarch Zainal Alireza and represented the third generation of family leadership in the House of Alireza business empire. He played a significant role in continuing the family's commercial legacy and overseeing the expansion of their business interests across various sectors.

Ahmed died in 2012, after which the family business leadership passed to his surviving children. His sons Khaled, Mohammed, Abdullah, Hisham, Yousef, and his daughter Mariam, along with some of their children including Aamer, Hatim, and Sulaiman, continue to serve as executives for the House of Alireza's commercial interests, including major companies like SISCO, Tusdeer, and Xenel.
achievements: Led third generation of House of Alireza business empire

notableFigures.name: Khalid Alireza
notableFigures.title: CEO of Xenel Group
notableFigures.roleDescription: Current CEO of major family business conglomerate
notableFigures.significance: Current CEO of Xenel Group and son of Ahmed Alireza, leading the fourth generation of family business operations.
notableFigures.biography:
Khalid Alireza is the son of Ahmed Alireza and currently serves as CEO of the Xenel Group, a diversified company based in Jeddah that was founded in 1973 by descendants of the House of Alireza. Xenel, whose name is a spelling variation honoring family patriarch Zainal Alireza, has grown into a major conglomerate with expertise in energy, petrochemicals, construction, infrastructure development, healthcare, industrial services, information technologies, logistics, real estate, and global investing.

As part of the fourth generation of the Alireza family business leadership, Khalid works alongside his siblings Mohammed, Abdullah, Hisham, Yousef, and sister Mariam, as well as other family members including Aamer, Hatim, and Sulaiman, who serve as executives across the House of Alireza's various commercial interests including SISCO, Tusdeer, and Xenel.
achievements: CEO of Xenel Group
Fourth-generation family business leader

history: The name Alireza appears across multiple distinct lineages in the region. The House of Alireza represents Saudi Arabia's oldest organized commercial enterprise, founded by Zainal Alireza in 1845 after arriving in Jeddah from Laristan in southern Iran during the 1840s. This Persian merchant family initially traded food, textiles, and merchandise from the Middle East and Indian subcontinent, while serving as hajj agents for British-Indian shipping lines. They became early political supporters of Abdulaziz al Saud, with family members serving in Ottoman parliament and as governors of Jeddah. The business expanded to include the first Ford dealership in Saudi Arabia (1940) and rights to Western brands like Pepsi Cola and Goodyear, eventually incorporating as a limited liability company in 2003. The research also reveals the Iranian Pahlavi dynasty included Ali Reza Pahlavi (1966-2011), son of the last Shah, who studied ancient Iranian studies at Harvard before his death by suicide in Boston. Additionally, contemporary figures like Alireza Arafi, born 1959 in Maybod to a Lak-Kurdish family whose ancestors converted from Zoroastrianism to Islam in the 19th century, represent the clerical establishment in Iran.
modernStatus: The House of Alireza operates as a diversified Saudi business empire through companies like SISCO, Tusdeer, and Xenel, while Iranian Alireza figures hold various political and clerical positions.

migrationPath.description: Zainal Alireza migrated from Laristan in southern Iran to establish trading operations in Jeddah

name: Binzagr
description: The Binzagr family is a well-known merchant family from Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, most notably represented by pioneering artist Safeya Binzagr (1940-2024) who founded the Darat Safeya Binzagr museum.

notableFigures.name: Safeya Binzagr
notableFigures.title: Artist and Museum Founder
notableFigures.roleDescription: Pioneer Saudi Arabian artist who became the first woman to hold a solo exhibition in Saudi Arabia and founded the Darat Safeya Binzagr museum

history: The Binzagr family is described as a 'well-known merchant family' in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, based on information about prominent family member Safeya Binzagr (1940-2024). Safeya was born in 1940 to this established merchant family in Jeddah and became a pioneering Saudi Arabian artist. She was privately taught art in Egypt and earned a degree from St Martin's School of Art in London in 1965. In 1970, she became the first woman to hold a solo exhibition of her work in Saudi Arabia, though initially she was not allowed to attend the openings and was represented by male family members. The family's prominence in Jeddah's merchant community is evidenced by their ability to provide Safeya with international education and support her artistic career. In 2000, Safeya opened the Darat Safeya Binzagr museum and gallery in Jeddah, making her the only artist in Saudi Arabia to have her own museum. The museum, which she founded as a non-profit cultural institution, serves as both her home and studio while documenting Hejazi heritage and social life through art and artifacts. The family's merchant background and cultural contributions through Safeya's artistic work have established them as notable figures in Jeddah's cultural landscape.
modernStatus: Established merchant family in Jeddah with significant cultural contributions through the arts

name: Al Naghi
originStory: Descended from the Persian philosopher Mir Damad through his son Seyed Ali Naghi Astarabadi in the 17th century.
description: A family of Persian scholarly origin descended from the 17th-century philosopher Mir Damad, now involved in modern automotive business through Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors in Saudi Arabia.

notableFigures.name: Mir Damad (Seyyed Mir Mohammad Baqer Esterabadi)
notableFigures.title: Philosopher and Third Teacher
notableFigures.roleDescription: Iranian Twelver Shia philosopher, founder of the School of Isfahan, architect of Shah Mosque

notableFigures.name: Seyed Ali Naghi Astarabadi
notableFigures.title: Son of Mir Damad
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of the philosopher Mir Damad

notableFigures.name: Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi
notableFigures.title: Princess and Mother
notableFigures.roleDescription: Wife of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
notableFigures.significance: Mother of several prominent Iranian princes and princesses in the Qajar dynasty.
notableFigures.biography:
Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (1900-1984) was one of the eight wives of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma, a prominent member of the Qajar dynasty and former Prime Minister of Iran. She bore him seven children between 1918 and 1927, including Princess Mahsama, Prince Jamshid, Prince Kaveh, Prince Ali Naghi, Prince Alidad, Princess Bilqis (who died young in 1927), and Prince Hafez.

As part of the extended Farman Farma family, she lived through the major political transitions of 20th century Iran, including the fall of the Qajar dynasty and the rise of the Pahlavi dynasty. Her husband was known for ensuring all his children, including daughters, received both classical Iranian training and modern Western education. Fatemeh Khanum lived for nearly eight decades, witnessing the transformation of Iran from a traditional monarchy to a modern state.

notableFigures.name: Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian
notableFigures.title: Prince
notableFigures.roleDescription: Son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
notableFigures.significance: Member of the prominent Qajar dynasty Farman Farma family.
notableFigures.biography:
Prince Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian was born in 1923 as the son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma and Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (Shirazi). He was part of the large Farman Farma family, one of the most influential political dynasties in Qajar and early Pahlavi Iran. His father served as the 11th Prime Minister of Iran and held numerous other high governmental positions.

Like his siblings, Ali Naghi was raised with both traditional Iranian values and modern Western education, as his father believed in preparing his children for the changing political landscape of Iran. He lived through the major transformations of 20th century Iran, including the transition from Qajar to Pahlavi rule and later the Islamic Revolution of 1979.

history: The Al Naghi family traces its origins to Persian scholarly lineage, specifically connected to the prominent Iranian philosopher Mir Damad (c. 1561-1631/1632). Mir Damad, also known as Seyyed Mir Mohammad Baqer Esterabadi, was a leading Twelver Shia philosopher and founder of the School of Isfahan. His son was Seyed Ali Naghi Astarabadi, and his grandson was Sayyid Mahdi bin Sayyid Ali Naqi. The family name 'Naghi' appears to derive from this Ali Naghi lineage. In the modern era, the Al Naghi name appears in the business world through Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors in Saudi Arabia, which became part of a consortium that acquired the Bavarian Auto Group in Egypt in 2022. This consortium, comprising BMW importers and distributors from Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and local Egyptian investors, renamed BAG as the Global Auto Group and signed new BMW and MINI assembly agreements. The family's transition from Persian scholarly traditions to modern automotive business represents a significant evolution across centuries.
modernStatus: Involved in automotive business through Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors, part of consortium owning Global Auto Group (formerly Bavarian Auto Group)

timelineEvents.description: Death of the family patriarch and renowned philosopher Mir Damad

name: Zahid
originStory: The name Zahid originates from Arabic language and Islamic religious tradition, where it denotes someone who is a literal believer in religious law as opposed to mystical interpretation.
description: Zahid is an Arabic-origin name meaning 'Altruistic' or 'noble believer,' particularly popular in Middle Eastern and South Asian Muslim communities. The name carries religious significance in Islamic tradition, distinguishing literal believers from mystical Sufis.

notableFigures.name: Ghayas Zahid
notableFigures.title: Professional Footballer

notableFigures.name: Zahid Valencia
notableFigures.title: Professional Wrestler
notableFigures.roleDescription: American freestyle wrestler, 2025 World Champion and two-time NCAA Division I national champion

notableFigures.name: Zaid al-Rashidi (Muhammad Abdul Matin Khan Zahid)
notableFigures.title: Islamic Scholar
notableFigures.roleDescription: Pakistani Islamic scholar, writer, and founder-director of Sharia Academy in Gujranwala

notableFigures.name: Zahid Maleque
notableFigures.title: Politician and Former Health Minister
notableFigures.roleDescription: Bangladesh Awami League politician and former minister of health and family welfare
notableFigures.significance: Former Bangladesh health minister who received WHO award for reducing child mortality and handled COVID-19 pandemic response.
notableFigures.biography: Zahid Maleque was born on April 11, 1959, in Garpara Union, Manikganj Sadar Upazila, Manikgonj District in the then East Pakistan. He completed his undergraduate and postgraduate degrees in English from the University of Dhaka. His father was Colonel M. A. Maleque, a former minister, former mayor of Dhaka, and member of parliament. Maleque built a successful business career, serving as chairman of multiple companies including Sun Life Insurance Company Limited, Bangladesh Thai Aluminium Limited, and several other enterprises. He entered politics representing the Manikganj-3 constituency, first contesting in 2001 but losing to the Bangladesh Nationalist Party candidate. He won the seat in 2008 and was subsequently re-elected in 2014 and 2018. As health minister, Maleque received a WHO award in 2016 on behalf of the health ministry for reducing child mortality. During the COVID-19 pandemic, he faced significant criticism for his handling of the crisis, including issues with protective equipment quality and testing kit availability, with several parliamentary colleagues calling for his removal in June 2020.
achievements: WHO award recipient for reducing child mortality (2016)
Three-term Member of Parliament for Manikganj-3 (2009-2024)
Former Minister of Health and Family Welfare of Bangladesh
Chairman of multiple business enterprises

notableFigures.name: Mustafa Zahid
notableFigures.title: Musician and Singer
notableFigures.roleDescription: Pakistani music composer, singer, and lead vocalist of rock band Roxen
notableFigures.significance: Gained international success with Bollywood soundtracks and is among the top three most-followed Pakistani artists on Spotify.
notableFigures.biography: Mustafa Zahid was born on December 18, 1984, in Lahore, Pakistan. He comes from a notable musical family, being the nephew of Ali Azmat, the former lead vocalist of the famous rock band Junoon, and is also related to the late Sufi kalam singer, actor, director, and producer Inayat Hussain Bhatti. As a child, he never initially planned to become a singer, but his path changed when he was asked to perform at a college event where he sang 'Bheegi Bheegi Raaton Mein,' which motivated him to pursue music professionally. Mustafa formed the rock band Roxen in 2004 and serves as both the band leader and lead vocalist. His breakthrough came in mid-2007 when two of his songs were featured in the Bollywood movie Awarapan, including the hits 'Toh Phir Aao' and 'Tera Mera Rishta.' Since then, he has sung over a dozen Bollywood songs, including major hits in films such as Aashiqui 2 and Ek Villain. Roxen's debut album, Roxen-E-Dewaar, was released in late 2006 and topped charts across South Asia for nearly six weeks. Universal Music signed Mustafa Zahid and Roxen for worldwide album releases, and he has achieved remarkable digital success, becoming one of the top three most-followed Pakistani artists on Spotify with over 5.5 million followers.
achievements: Lead vocalist and band leader of successful rock band Roxen
Singer of major Bollywood hit songs in films like Aashiqui 2 and Ek Villain
Over 5.5 million followers on Spotify, among top three Pakistani artists
Debut album Roxen-E-Dewaar topped South Asian charts for six weeks
Signed with Universal Music for worldwide releases

notableFigures.name: Salma Zahid
notableFigures.title: Canadian Politician
notableFigures.roleDescription: Member of Parliament representing Scarborough Centre in the House of Commons of Canada
notableFigures.significance: First MP to wear a hijab in the Canadian House of Commons and advocate for racialized women's rights.
notableFigures.biography: Salma Zahid was born on May 26, 1970, in Coventry, England, and is a Pakistani Canadian politician. She holds a master's degree in educational management and administration from the University of London's Institute of Education, and an MBA from Quaid-i-Azam University in Pakistan. A mother of two, she has resided in Scarborough since 2000 and is a recipient of the Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal. Before entering politics, Zahid worked as a community organizer and held multiple positions with the Government of Ontario. She was elected as a Member of Parliament for Scarborough Centre in the 2015 Canadian federal election, succeeding Conservative Roxanne James. During the 42nd Parliament, she served as Vice-Chair for the Standing Committee on the Status of Women, where she was a spokesperson for racialized women. In February 2018, Zahid announced she was taking medical leave to treat Stage 4 Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. During her recovery from chemotherapy, she grew closer to her Islamic faith and began wearing a hijab for personal reasons. In May 2018, she made history by becoming the first MP to wear a hijab in the House of Commons. After completing six rounds of chemotherapy, she was declared cancer-free in July 2018 and returned to her parliamentary duties. She was re-elected in the 2019 Canadian federal election but has faced criticism for her positions on international issues, including attending events with Holocaust denialism publishers and calling for support of South Africa's genocide case against Israel in January 2024.
achievements: First Member of Parliament to wear a hijab in the Canadian House of Commons
Vice-Chair of the Standing Committee on the Status of Women
Recipient of the Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal
Successfully overcame Stage 4 Non-Hodgkin lymphoma
Re-elected Member of Parliament for Scarborough Centre

notableFigures.name: Zahid Nisar Quraishi
notableFigures.title: Federal Judge
notableFigures.roleDescription: United States District Judge and first Muslim Article III federal judge
notableFigures.significance: First Muslim Article III federal judge confirmed by the United States Senate and distinguished military veteran.
notableFigures.biography: Zahid Nisar Quraishi was born in 1975 in New York City and raised in Fanwood, New Jersey, by his parents Shahida P. Quraishi and Dr. Nisar A. Quraishi, immigrants from Pakistan. He graduated from Scotch Plains-Fanwood High School in 1993, received his Bachelor of Arts from John Jay College of Criminal Justice in 1997, and his Juris Doctor from Rutgers Law School in 2000. After law school, he clerked for Judge Edwin Stern and worked as a litigation associate before joining the U.S. Army Judge Advocate General's Corps in 2003. He served two combat deployments in Iraq (2004 and 2006-2007) in Tikrit and Ramadi, providing legal counsel on detainee operations and military justice under intense combat conditions. He was honorably discharged as a Captain, receiving numerous decorations including the Bronze Star and Combat Action Badge. After military service, Quraishi worked as assistant chief counsel at ICE, Assistant U.S. Attorney for New Jersey (2008-2013), and later became a partner at Riker, Danzig law firm. He was appointed as a U.S. Magistrate Judge in 2019, becoming the first Asian-American on the federal bench in New Jersey. On March 30, 2021, President Biden nominated him to be a U.S. District Judge, and he was confirmed by the Senate on June 10, 2021, by an 81-16 vote, making history as the first Muslim-American Article III federal judge. As a judge, he has made notable rulings including blocking New Jersey gun industry lawsuit laws and ending the controversial county line ballot system in New Jersey primaries.
achievements: First Muslim-American to serve as Article III federal judge
First Asian-American federal judge in New Jersey
Bronze Star and Combat Action Badge recipient for Iraq War service
U.S. Army Judge Advocate General's Corps veteran with two combat deployments
Landmark ruling ending New Jersey's county line ballot system
Confirmed by Senate vote of 81-16 in 2021

notableFigures.name: Ahmad Zahid Hamidi
notableFigures.title: Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia
notableFigures.roleDescription: Malaysian politician, UMNO President, and Deputy Prime Minister
notableFigures.significance: Current Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia and UMNO President, first UMNO president after independence to not become prime minister.
notableFigures.biography: Ahmad Zahid bin Hamidi was born on January 4, 1953, in Bagan Datuk, Perak, the eldest of nine children to Indonesian-born Malaysian parents. He was notably raised by a Chinese foster-father, Chen Jin Ting, and sold ice cream together for six years during elementary school, an experience he later cited when defending against accusations of being anti-Chinese. He studied at Sekolah Izzuddin Shah and graduated from University of Malaya in 1976, later earning a PhD in Communication from University of Putra Malaysia in 2008. Before politics, he worked as a banker for OCBC and held various corporate positions including directorships at Bank Simpanan Nasional and Permodalan Nasional Berhad. He began his political career in 1986 as political secretary to Najib Razak, was elected UMNO Youth Chief in 1996, and won the Bagan Datok parliamentary seat in 1995, which he has held continuously since. Zahid served in various ministerial positions under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi and Najib Razak from 2008-2018, including Minister of Defence (2009-2013), Minister of Home Affairs (2013-2015), and Deputy Prime Minister (2015-2018). After UMNO's defeat in 2018, he became party president and Leader of the Opposition. Despite leading BN to its worst electoral result in 2022, winning only 30 seats, he successfully negotiated a coalition government with Pakatan Harapan and was reappointed Deputy Prime Minister in December 2022, making him the first person to hold this position twice under different administrations. Throughout his career, Zahid has faced numerous corruption charges, including 87 charges involving RM114 million, though he was acquitted of 40 bribery charges in 2022 and received dismissals on remaining cases in 2023.
achievements: Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia (2015-2018, 2022-present)
President of UMNO since 2018
Minister of Defence and Home Affairs
Longest-serving MP for Bagan Datok constituency (1995-present)
Leader of Barisan Nasional coalition
Recipient of numerous Malaysian state honors including Dato' Seri titles

history: The name Zahid has its roots in Arabic language and culture, with meanings including 'Altruistic' and 'the noble man who stops others from wrongdoing.' In Islamic tradition, a Zahid is defined as 'the literal believer in the letter of the Law, opposed to the Sufi, who believes in its spirit,' representing an outsider (Zahiri) perspective versus the inner (Batini) spiritual approach. The name has gained prominence across Middle Eastern and South Asian regions, particularly in Pakistan where it appears frequently among notable figures in sports, entertainment, and religious scholarship. Contemporary bearers of the name include Pakistani cricketers, actors, and Islamic scholars, as well as individuals of Pakistani heritage who have achieved international recognition, such as Norwegian footballer Ghayas Zahid who became the first person of Pakistani descent to play in the UEFA Champions League. The name's widespread adoption reflects its cultural significance in Islamic communities and the diaspora populations who maintain connections to traditional Islamic values while adapting to modern contexts.
modernStatus: The name remains widely used across Muslim communities globally, with notable contemporary bearers in sports, entertainment, academia, and religious scholarship

name: Sharbatly
description: A prominent Saudi Arabian family with notable achievements in equestrian sports, contemporary art, and major real estate development projects. Family members have gained international recognition across multiple disciplines.

notableFigures.name: Abdullah Sharbatly
notableFigures.title: Olympic Equestrian Champion
notableFigures.roleDescription: International show jumping competitor with multiple Olympic and Asian Games medals

notableFigures.name: Shalimar Hassan Abbas Sharbatly
notableFigures.title: Abstract Artist
notableFigures.roleDescription: First woman commissioned by Saudi government for public street art, international contemporary artist

notableFigures.name: Hassan Abbas Sharbatly
notableFigures.title: Businessman
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman and father of artist Shalimar Sharbatly

notableFigures.name: Abdulrahman Hassan Sharbatly
notableFigures.title: Business Investor
notableFigures.roleDescription: Stakeholder in Jeddah Economic Company, involved in Jeddah Tower development project

notableFigures.name: Shalimar Sharbatly
notableFigures.title: Abstract Painter
notableFigures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian abstract painter
notableFigures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian artist specializing in abstract painting.
notableFigures.biography:
Shalimar Sharbatly was born in 1971 and is recognized as a prominent figure in contemporary Saudi Arabian art. She is listed among the notable Saudi Arabian artists and is specifically known for her work in abstract painting. Her inclusion in the comprehensive list of Saudi Arabian artists demonstrates her significance in the kingdom's artistic landscape.

As part of the contemporary generation of Saudi artists, Sharbatly represents the growing recognition of Saudi Arabian visual arts on both national and international levels. Her specialization in abstract painting places her within the modern artistic movements that have emerged in Saudi Arabia as the country has undergone cultural and social transformations.

history: The Sharbatly family represents a prominent Saudi Arabian business dynasty with documented members across multiple fields including equestrian sports, art, and business. The research reveals several notable contemporary figures: Abdullah Sharbatly, born in London in 1982, is a renowned equestrian who has achieved significant international success in show jumping, including Olympic bronze in 2012, Asian Games silver in 2014, and multiple Asian Games gold medals including individual and team gold in the 2022 Asian Games held in Hangzhou, China in 2023. Another prominent member is Shalimar Hassan Abbas Sharbatly, born in 1971, an abstract artist who became the first woman commissioned by the Saudi government to create public street art in Jeddah. She is the daughter of businessman Hassan Abbas Sharbatly and has gained international recognition for her moving art exhibitions at the Louvre and other prestigious venues. The family's business interests are evidenced by Abdulrahman Hassan Sharbatly's involvement as a stakeholder in the Jeddah Economic Company, which is developing the Jeddah Tower project, holding a 16.67% stake through Abrar Holding Company alongside other major Saudi investors.
modernStatus: Active in high-level business ventures including major real estate development (Jeddah Tower project), international equestrian competition, and contemporary art with global exhibition presence
